text.en: Who led Jesus into the wilderness?
The Spirit. "Jesus was led by the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil."
What did the devil ask Jesus to do first: turn stones into bread, jump from the temple, or worship him for the kingdoms of the world?
Turn stones into bread — the devil says, "If You are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread."
This passage names three places: the wilderness, where the Spirit led Jesus (v.1), and two more places the devil took him afterward. Name all three, in the order they happen.

In order: the wilderness, where the Spirit led Jesus and the devil first tempted him (v.1-3); the holy city, on the pinnacle of the temple (v.5); and a very high mountain, showing him all the kingdoms of the world (v.8).

Each time Jesus answers the devil, he appeals to Scripture: "It is written" (v.4), "It is also written" (v.7), "For it is written" (v.10). What do all three openings point to every time before Jesus gives his answer?

Scripture — what God has already spoken in his written Word. Before each answer Jesus takes his stand on what "is written" rather than on a new word invented for the moment. He does so as the Son who honors Scripture as God's own settled truth; the point is where he grounds his reply (the written Word), not that he is without authority of his own.
Jesus ends with "serve Him only" (v.10), right after the devil's offer in verse 9. What work does the little word "only" do here — what does it include, and what does it rule out?
"Only" makes worship and service exclusive: God alone is to be worshiped and served, and this rules out the devil's own offer in v.9 — falling down to worship him for the kingdoms of the world.
Without looking back at the passage, try to say back one of the lines Jesus quoted from Scripture when the devil tempted him (for example, his answer about bread in verse 4).

Any one of the three lines, recited close to word: (v.4) "It is written: Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God." (v.7) "It is also written: Do not put the Lord your God to the test." (v.10) "For it is written: Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only." Credit any answer that is substantially one of these three, not a paraphrase invented from scratch.

Jesus's answer in verse 4, "Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God," is a quotation from Deuteronomy 8:3. Read that cross-reference: what was Israel's own experience in the wilderness that this line was first spoken about?

In the wilderness God let Israel hunger and then fed them with manna, to teach them that man does not live by bread alone but by every word that comes from the mouth of the LORD. Jesus, hungry after forty days, answers the devil with the same lesson Israel was given.

In verse 6 the devil quotes a promise about angels protecting the Son of God — this is Psalm 91:11-12. Jesus replies in verse 7 with "Do not put the Lord your God to the test," a quotation from Deuteronomy 6:16. Read both cross-references: how does Jesus use one Scripture (a command) to answer the devil's misuse of another (a promise)?

The devil quotes a promise (Ps 91:11-12) to justify a reckless act; Jesus answers not by disputing the promise but by citing a command (Deu 6:16, "do not put the Lord your God to the test") that governs how any promise may rightly be claimed. Scripture interprets Scripture — a promise is never a license to presume on God.
After reading this passage, what is a question you have about the wilderness, or about the devil, or about the angels at the end?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder about the wilderness, who the devil is, why the Spirit led Jesus into temptation, or why the angels only appear at the end. Receive whatever question they bring rather than steering them toward one you'd prefer; if pressed, it's fine to say some of it stays a mystery.
What do you wonder about why the devil waited until Jesus had fasted forty days before tempting him with bread?

The passage itself notes the timing: forty days of fasting, then hunger (v.2), then the tempter comes (v.3) — the trial strikes at the point of greatest physical need. Let the child sit with that connection rather than supplying a settled explanation of the devil's motive or timing, which the text does not spell out.
Jesus answers all three temptations by quoting Scripture. Based on what the passage shows him doing, why do you think he chose to answer this way?

The text shows Jesus meeting all three temptations by taking his stand on what is written — grounding each answer in God's written Word rather than in a new word of his own, though as the Son he is himself its author (see the brief: he honors Scripture as an example to us, and answers one Scripture's misuse with another). Let the leader draw out reasons the youth offers — trust in God's word, resisting the devil, following God's own pattern — without collapsing it to a single correct phrase.
The devil offers Jesus "all the kingdoms of the world and their glory" in exchange for worship (vv.8-9). What does Jesus's refusal in verse 10 say about what he considers to belong to God alone?

Jesus refuses power and glory offered on the condition of worshiping someone other than God, and answers with "Worship the Lord your God and serve Him only" (v.10) — worship and ultimate allegiance belong to God alone. Let the discussion stay open to what this costs or means in the youth's own life rather than closing it with a formula.

Jesus met temptation by speaking God's Word out loud. Which line from this passage (v.4, v.7, or v.10) will you keep ready for the next time you are tempted — and where could you write it down so you will see it?

The point is personal readiness, not a right answer — any of the three quoted lines (v.4, v.7, v.10) is a legitimate choice depending on what temptation the person names for themselves. Let them choose and name their own place to write it down rather than assigning one.

Fold a paper into three panels. In each panel draw one of the three places from this passage: the wilderness (where the Spirit led Jesus), the temple, and the mountain (where the devil took him). Under each panel, write just one or two words for what happened there.

Panel 1 — the wilderness (v.1-3): fasting, hunger, devil tempts with bread. Panel 2 — the holy city / pinnacle of the temple (v.5-7): devil tempts him to throw himself down. Panel 3 — a very high mountain (v.8-10): devil offers the kingdoms of the world for worship. Use this to check each panel is in the right place and order.
Draw Jesus in the wilderness. Draw the devil next to him. At the end of your drawing, add angels, because the passage says angels came to Jesus after the devil left.
Listen for what happens right after Jesus finishes fasting forty days and forty nights.
He was hungry — and the tempter came to him and said, "If You are the Son of God, tell these stones to become bread."
Listen for the short words Jesus uses each time to point to Scripture before he answers the devil.

category.en: Listen for how Jesus introduces Scripture

text.en: "It is written" (v.4), "It is also written" (v.7), "For it is written" (v.10) — each introduces a quotation of Scripture just before Jesus answers.
Listen for what the devil offers Jesus in the third temptation, and for the reason Jesus gives when he refuses.

category.en: Listen for an offer and a refusal

text.en: The devil offers "all the kingdoms of the world and their glory" in exchange for Jesus falling down to worship him (vv.8-9); Jesus refuses, citing worship and service that belong to God alone (v.10). Let adults connect this to their own places of trading allegiance for gain, without prescribing the connection for them.
But Jesus answered, “It is written: ‘Man shall not live on bread alone, but on every word that comes from the mouth of God.’” (Matthew 4:4)
In your own words, retell what happened when Jesus was tempted in the wilderness: where he was, what the devil asked him to do each of the three times, how Jesus answered, and how the encounter ends.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness and fasts forty days (v.1-2); the devil tempts him three times — to turn stones to bread (v.3-4), to throw himself from the temple (v.5-7), and to worship the devil for the kingdoms of the world (v.8-10); each time Jesus answers by quoting Scripture ("it is written"); the devil leaves, and angels come and minister to Jesus (v.11).
Who came to Jesus after he sat down on the mountain?
His disciples came to him. (The crowds are present and within hearing, but the text names the disciples specifically as the ones who 'came to him.')
Where did Jesus go before he began to teach?
Up on the mountain -- he went up on the mountain (and sat down there) before teaching.
Put these in the order they happen: Jesus opens his mouth to teach; Jesus sees the crowds; the disciples come to him; Jesus sits down.

Correct order: 1) Jesus sees the crowds; 2) Jesus sits down (on the mountain, where he had gone up); 3) the disciples come to him; 4) Jesus opens his mouth to teach. 'Sat down' marks a formal, solemn discourse -- Jesus was positioning himself to be heard, not making a passing remark.
As we read, listen for who comes close to Jesus once he sits down on the mountain.
Listen for: his disciples -- they are the ones who come to him once he is seated on the mountain.

These four moments are printed out of order: 'sits down,' 'disciples come to him,' 'sees the crowds,' 'goes up the mountain.' Write the number 1 to 4 next to each to show the order they really happen in.
1) 'sees the crowds'; 2) 'goes up the mountain'; 3) 'sits down'; 4) 'disciples come to him.' Use this to check children's numbering.
Draw Jesus sitting on the mountain, with his disciples coming to him.
In your own words, retell what happens right before Jesus begins to teach: what he sees, where he goes, and who comes to him.

A complete retelling should touch: Jesus sees the crowds; he goes up on the mountain and sits down (a deliberate, formal posture for teaching); his disciples come to him; he then opens his mouth and begins to teach. Listen for these beats, not exact wording.
Picture Jesus going up the mountain, sitting down, and his disciples coming to him just before he begins to teach. What question would you want to ask about this moment?

This is a wonder question with no fixed answer -- let responses range freely. The text itself offers only spare detail (seeing, ascending, sitting, being approached, speaking), so resist supplying invented specifics (what Jesus was thinking, the disciples' emotions, the crowd's reaction) as if the passage stated them. If helpful, you may note the deliberateness of the scene -- Jesus sees, goes up, sits, is approached, then speaks -- as a prompt for noticing, not as the 'right' question to land on.
Without looking, name at least three of the kinds of people Jesus calls "blessed" in this passage (for example, "those who mourn" or "the merciful").

Any three of: the poor in spirit (theirs is the kingdom of heaven), those who mourn (they will be comforted), the meek (they will inherit the earth), those who hunger and thirst for righteousness (they will be filled), the merciful (they will be shown mercy), the pure in heart (they will see God), the peacemakers (they will be called sons of God), those persecuted for righteousness (theirs is the kingdom of heaven).
Reading verses 3-12, which "Blessed are..." saying does Jesus give first, and which "Blessed are..." saying is the last one?

First: "Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven" (v.3). Last "Blessed are..." saying: "Blessed are you when people insult you, persecute you, and falsely say all kinds of evil against you because of Me" (v.11).
Through verse 10, Jesus speaks about 'they' and 'those.' In verses 11-12, who is He speaking to instead, and what word marks the change?
Through v.10 Jesus speaks about others in the third person ("they"/"those"); in vv.11-12 He turns to direct address. The word "you" in "Blessed are you" marks the change.

Jesus pairs each kind of person with a promise. For "poor in spirit" (v.3), "meek" (v.5), and "pure in heart" (v.8), what promise does Jesus attach to each — and what does that promise suggest the phrase means?

Poor in spirit (v.3) -> "theirs is the kingdom of heaven," suggesting humble, needy dependence on God rather than material poverty. Meek (v.5) -> "they will inherit the earth," suggesting quiet, gentle submission is rewarded, not weakness or being overlooked. Pure in heart (v.8) -> "they will see God," suggesting inward, not merely ceremonial, holiness that gives access to God.
"Blessed are the peacemakers, for they will be called sons of God." — Matthew 5:9
Psalm 37:11 also says the meek will inherit the land. How does that verse match what Jesus promises the meek in Matthew 5:5?

Psalm 37:11 already promises "the meek shall inherit the land" — Jesus repeats this same promise almost word for word, showing the blessing on the meek is not a new idea but Scripture's own settled promise.
Psalm 34:18 says the LORD is near the brokenhearted and saves those crushed in spirit. How does that psalm connect to Jesus' first blessing on the 'poor in spirit' in Matthew 5:3?

Psalm 34:18 says the LORD is near the brokenhearted and saves those crushed in spirit — the same humble, needy, self-distrusting heart Jesus calls "poor in spirit" and pronounces blessed with the kingdom of heaven.
Revelation 21:4 says God will wipe away every tear and there will be no more mourning. How does that promise connect to Jesus' words to those who mourn in Matthew 5:4?
Revelation 21:4 describes the final, complete fulfillment of Jesus' promise that those who mourn "will be comforted" — God Himself wiping away every tear so that mourning is no more.
Which one of these eight blessings do you wonder about most? Write down a question you have about it.

Open-ended by design — there is no expected answer to supply. If someone is unsure where to start, you may point back to any one of the eight "Blessed are..." sayings (vv.3-10) or the turn to "you" in vv.11-12, but let their own question stand rather than resolving it for them.

The people Jesus calls 'blessed' — those who mourn, the meek, the persecuted — are not usually the people the world calls fortunate. From what the passage itself promises each of them, why does Jesus call them blessed anyway?

The text's own answer lies in what each "Blessed are..." clause promises (kingdom, comfort, inheritance, fullness, mercy, seeing God, sonship, kingdom again) rather than in circumstances the world would call fortunate. Let the group work from those promises rather than supplying a summary verdict; avoid steering toward either "the suffering itself is the good thing" or "these traits earn the reward" — the text pronounces blessing, it does not explain away the hardship.

Verses 3 and 10 both say "theirs is the kingdom of heaven." Look at how Jesus states it — "Blessed are... for theirs is...": does the wording present the kingdom as something these people earn by their qualities, or as the promise He attaches to them? Point to the words that guide your answer.

Notice the grammar: "Blessed are... for theirs is..." attaches the kingdom as a promise that follows the blessing, not a wage earned by the quality named. Common misreading to watch for: treating "poor in spirit" as a merit that purchases the kingdom. Let the group reason from the wording itself rather than announcing the promise-versus-merit distinction as a settled rule.
This week, do one specific act of peacemaking — help settle a disagreement, or say a kind word to someone you've been at odds with. Write down what you did and what happened.

No right answer to check for. Look for whether a genuine, specific act took place (not a vague intention) and listen to what happened. Record what was done as evidence — do not judge whether the peacemaking "succeeded."
Choose one act of mercy to do this week — something kind for someone who needs help. Write or draw what you did.

No right answer to check for. Look for a specific, concrete act of mercy and what happened, not for a particular kind of deed. Record what was done as evidence, without judging its size or effectiveness.
Listen as the verses are read aloud. Every time you hear the word 'blessed,' hold up one finger. Count how many fingers you have at the end!
The word "blessed" is said nine times: once at the start of each of the eight sayings in vv.3-10, and once more at the start of v.11 ("Blessed are you").
Listen for how many separate "Blessed are..." sayings about other people Jesus gives before He turns and says "Blessed are you" in verse 11.
Eight separate "Blessed are..." sayings about other people (vv.3-10) come before Jesus turns and says "Blessed are you" in v.11.
Listen for the phrase 'kingdom of heaven' — notice how many times it's said and which blessings it's attached to.
"Kingdom of heaven" is said twice: attached to the poor in spirit (v.3) and to those persecuted for righteousness (v.10) — the phrase brackets the whole set of sayings, opening and closing it.
Listen for where Jesus shifts from 'they' to 'you' in verses 11-12, and notice why He links His disciples' reproach to the prophets before them.

Have the group notice that vv.11-12 move from describing others ("they") to addressing the listener directly ("you"), and that Jesus ties the disciples' coming reproach to how "the prophets before you" were treated — placing them in a long line of faithful suffering rather than an isolated hardship. Let listeners voice what that link suggests rather than stating the conclusion for them.
In your own words, retell the blessings Jesus gives in this passage, in order, and what each one promises.

In order: poor in spirit -> kingdom of heaven; those who mourn -> comforted; the meek -> inherit the earth; those who hunger and thirst for righteousness -> filled; the merciful -> shown mercy; the pure in heart -> see God; the peacemakers -> called sons of God; those persecuted for righteousness -> kingdom of heaven; and "you" when insulted, persecuted, and falsely accused for Christ's sake -> a great reward in heaven, like the prophets before you.

Jesus calls his followers "the salt of the earth," and warns that salt which "loses its savor" becomes useless and cannot be made salty again. Since salt is only useful while it stays salty, what might Jesus be saying his followers must keep, to be useful as his "salt"?

Their saltiness itself -- the distinctive quality that makes them useful as salt. Jesus's warning is that salt is only 'salt' while it keeps that savor; if it loses it, it is fit only to be discarded. So disciples must keep whatever it is that makes them 'salt' -- their distinctive, preserving influence -- rather than let it go flat.
Trace, in order, what verse 13 says happens to salt that has lost its taste (what it is good for, then what is done with it).
In order: it is 'no longer good for anything' -- then it is 'thrown out and trampled by men.'

Jesus says, "You are the light of the world. A city on a hill cannot be hidden." What does the picture of a city on a hill show about light — is it meant to be seen or hidden? How does the lamp in verse 15 make the same point?

Light is meant to be seen, not hidden -- a city on a hill cannot be hidden; it's inescapably visible. The lamp in v.15 makes the same point: no one lights a lamp to hide it under a basket; it is set on a stand precisely so it can give light.

Put these in the order the passage gives them: (a) the salt statement, (b) the light and lamp statements, (c) the purpose — letting your light shine so others see your good deeds and glorify the Father. Which one does Jesus place last?

Order: (a) the salt statement (v.13), then (b) the light and lamp statements (vv.14-15), then (c) the purpose (v.16). Jesus places the purpose -- that others see good deeds and glorify the Father -- last.

Verse 15 says people do not light a lamp and put it under a basket, but set it on a stand instead. According to the passage, what does putting it on the stand accomplish that the basket would prevent?

The text states it plainly: on a stand, the lamp 'gives light to everyone in the house' -- under a basket, that light would be blocked from those it's meant to reach. Help the child find that phrase in v.15. Where you can leave it open is what this means for a disciple's life: don't hand them the application (e.g., 'so be seen'); let them sit with why Jesus assumes no one would deliberately hide a lit lamp.

Verse 16 says 'let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.' According to this verse, who is meant to receive the glory when good deeds are seen? Why does that matter for how a good deed should be done?

V.16 names the Father in heaven as the one meant to receive glory when good deeds are seen -- not the disciple who does them; that part is answerable straight from the text. Leave the second half open: let the family reason toward motive (done to point to God, not to draw admiration to oneself) rather than supplying that conclusion. Watch for the common misreading that visibility itself is the goal -- the verse's point is who gets the glory, not simply being seen.
Name one specific, doable thing you could do this week that others could see — a good deed like those in verse 16 — done not so people admire you, but so that those who see it would praise God.

Keep this open -- any concrete, observable act is fair game; the passage doesn't specify which deeds. Hold the line on motive from v.16: the deed is done 'that they may... glorify your Father,' not so the doer is admired. If an answer is framed around being noticed or praised personally, gently redirect toward whose glory the act should point to, without prescribing which act to choose.

On an index card, write one observable good deed you will do before the next family study (a specific chore, kind word, or help offered). Note who might see it — and, beside it, write that it is meant to point them to God, not to you (verse 16). Afterward, write what happened.

This extends the same aim onto paper: any specific, observable deed is acceptable content for the card. The leader's role is only to confirm the two pieces v.16 asks for are present -- something observable, and a stated aim that it points to God rather than to the child -- not to approve or steer toward a particular deed. Leave 'what happened' open too; it's a record of what occurred, not a grade.
"In the same way, let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven." (Matthew 5:16)
Finish the line from memory: 'Let your ___ shine before men, that they may see your ___ and glorify your Father in heaven.'
'Let your light shine before men, that they may see your good deeds and glorify your Father in heaven.'

1 Peter 2:12 tells believers to keep their conduct honorable among outsiders so that, seeing their good deeds, they glorify God. How does this connect to what Matthew 5:16 says should happen when others see your good deeds?

Both point to the same pattern: visible conduct leads observers to glorify God. Matthew 5:16 says to let your light shine so that people, seeing your good deeds, glorify the Father in heaven; 1 Peter 2:12 gives the same instruction -- keep conduct honorable among outsiders so that, seeing your good deeds, they glorify God. Peter's charge is essentially an application of what Jesus commands in v.16.
Philippians 2:15 tells believers to 'shine as lights' in the world. How does this compare with Jesus calling his hearers 'the light of the world' in Matthew 5:14?

Both describe believers as light in a dark setting. Matthew 5:14 calls Jesus's hearers 'the light of the world'; Philippians 2:15 tells believers to 'shine as lights' amid a crooked generation. Philippians makes explicit what Matthew states as fact -- disciples, identified as light, are to actively shine amid surrounding darkness.
What is one question you have about being 'salt' or 'light' after hearing this passage? Write it down to ask during family time.

Fully open -- there is no expected question here. Receive whatever the child raises without correcting or resolving it on the spot; the point is that they notice a real gap or wonder in their own understanding and hold onto it for family discussion, not that the leader supplies the missing answer now.

Draw two pictures: one of a lamp hidden under a basket (dark), and one of a lamp set up on a stand (bright, lighting the room). Ask a grown-up to read v.15 again and point to which picture matches what Jesus says people actually do.

The picture that matches v.15 is the lamp set on the stand, bright and lighting the room -- that's what the passage says people actually do. The hidden-under-a-basket picture is the example Jesus says does not happen ('neither do people... put it under a basket').

category.en: Listen for the names Jesus gives

text.en: As this is read aloud, listen for the two things Jesus tells his listeners they already ARE. Hold up a finger for each one you hear.
Two fingers: 'the salt of the earth' (v.13) and 'the light of the world' (v.14).
In your own words, retell Matthew 5:13-16: what Jesus says about salt, what he says about light, and what he says the point of it all is.

A complete retelling covers three parts, in order: salt -- you are salt, but salt that loses its savor is useless and gets thrown out (v.13); light -- you are light, and light (a city on a hill, a lamp) is not hidden but set out to be seen (vv.14-15); the point -- let that light/good deeds be seen so that people glorify the Father in heaven, not the disciple (v.16). Use it to check the retelling hits all three, not to require exact wording.
In verse 1, Jesus is introduced as the descendant of two forefathers in particular, before the long list even begins. Who are the two men named there?
David and Abraham. Verse 1 calls Jesus "the son of David, the son of Abraham" — the two great forefathers to whom the Messianic promise was given.
This genealogy lists mostly fathers, yet verses 3-6 pause to name four women in the line (besides Mary in verse 16). Who are the four women named?

Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Uriah's wife. Verse 3 says Judah was "the father of Perez and Zerah by Tamar"; verse 5 says "Salmon was the father of Boaz by Rahab, Boaz the father of Obed by Ruth"; and verse 6 says "David was the father of Solomon by Uriah's wife."
Of all the men in this list, only one is given a royal title. Which man receives it, and what title does verse 6 give him?
David. Verse 6 alone adds a royal title, calling him "David the king" — the line whose throne the Messiah inherits.
Verse 17 divides the whole genealogy into three equal spans. What are the three periods it marks off, named in order?

From Abraham to David; from David to the exile to Babylon; and from the exile to the Christ. Verse 17 counts "fourteen generations from Abraham to David, fourteen from David to the exile to Babylon, and fourteen from the exile to the Christ."
The list runs in order from the earliest forefather down to a birth. With whose name does the genealogy begin in verse 2, and with whose birth does it end in verse 16?
It begins with Abraham (v.2) and ends with the birth of Jesus. Verse 16 reads that Jacob was the father of Joseph, the husband of Mary, "of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ."
Verse 1 calls this whole list "the record of the genealogy" of Jesus. In your own words, what is a genealogy?

A genealogy is a record of a person's ancestors — the line of fathers and sons that shows whom someone is descended from. Here it traces Jesus back through David to Abraham to prove He belongs to the family from which the Messiah must come (v.1).
The passage names Jesus with the title "Christ" at its opening and its close (vv.1, 16, 17). What does the title "Christ" mean?

Christ means the Anointed One — the promised Messiah, consecrated to His office as king, priest, and prophet. The genealogy is built to prove that this Jesus is the Christ of David's line, ending "of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ" (v.16).

Three times the passage marks one national event as a turning point in the line: "the exile to Babylon" (vv.11, 12, 17). What event does this phrase refer to, and what role does it play in the genealogy?

It refers to the deportation of Judah to Babylon, when the people were carried away from their land. In the genealogy it is the great hinge: it closes the second set of fourteen generations and opens the third, dividing David's era from the era that leads to the Christ (v.17).
This is the record of the genealogy of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham: (Matthew 1:1)
In all, then, there were fourteen generations from Abraham to David, fourteen from David to the exile to Babylon, and fourteen from the exile to the Christ. (Matthew 1:17)
Fill in the number from verse 17: 'In all, then, there were ______ generations from Abraham to David.'
fourteen. Verse 17 reads, "fourteen generations from Abraham to David" — and the same count marks each of the three spans.
In your own words, retell this genealogy as the story of God keeping a promise — from Abraham, down through David the king, through the exile to Babylon, to the birth of Jesus who is called Christ.

A faithful retelling traces the line's main stages: it begins with Abraham (v.2), runs through David the king (v.6), passes the hinge of the exile to Babylon (vv.11-12), and arrives at Joseph, the husband of Mary, "of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ" (v.16) — the whole set out in three groups of fourteen generations (v.17). Note which stages the learner recalls; do not grade wording.
Verse 1 calls Jesus the son of Abraham. The brief points to Genesis 22:18. What did God promise Abraham there about his offspring?

In Genesis 22:18 God promised Abraham that "through your offspring all nations of the earth will be blessed." Matthew's genealogy shows that promised offspring arriving in Jesus, the son of Abraham (v.1).
The genealogy singles out David the king (v.6), and verse 1 calls Jesus the son of David. The brief points to Psalm 132:11. What did God swear to David there?
In Psalm 132:11 the LORD swore an oath to David: "One of your descendants I will place on your throne." The royal line traced here leads to Jesus, the promised heir of David's throne (vv.1, 6).
The line passes through Jesse, the father of David, in verse 5. The brief points to Isaiah 11:1. What does Isaiah 11:1 promise concerning Jesse?

Isaiah 11:1 foretold that "a shoot will spring up from the stump of Jesse, and a Branch from his roots will bear fruit" — the Messiah rising from Jesse's line, the very line this genealogy traces down to Jesus (v.5).
This genealogy of fathers pauses to name four women — Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, and Uriah's wife (vv.3-6). What is one question you would like to ask about why Matthew included these women?

Let the learner voice their own wondering; do not supply the answer. The passage simply states the fact — these women stand named in the line (vv.3-6). Fruitful wonderings may touch that some were outsiders or had troubled histories, yet God wove them into the Messiah's line (the brief notes these as marks of grace, not gaps). Record the question the learner actually asks; do not evaluate anyone's worthiness or the learner's own standing.
Verse 17 counts the names in three groups of fourteen generations. What is one thing you wonder about why Matthew arranged all the names into three equal groups?

Invite the learner's own wondering; do not hand them a conclusion. The passage states the pattern plainly (v.17) as an aid to memory marking three eras that meet in Jesus. A child may wonder about the repeated number, or why history is grouped this way. Record the question the child raises without judging it; the counting is the passage's own, not a puzzle to be solved against the text.

Before a single name in the list appears, verse 1 introduces Jesus with exactly two forefather-titles: the son of David and the son of Abraham. What does the passage claim about Jesus by naming these two men first, and why might Matthew open his whole Gospel this way?

Anchor to what the text states, then ask why. The text: verse 1 titles Jesus "the son of David, the son of Abraham," and the list that follows (vv.2-16) documents the claim generation by generation. The why: Abraham and David are the two great forefathers to whom the Messianic promise was given — of blessing to the nations and of an everlasting throne — so Matthew opens by asserting that Jesus is the promised heir of both, then proves it by pedigree. Meaning is drawn from the text and the brief, not from speculation.

The whole passage is a chain of who was the father of whom, ending "of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ" (v.16). What does this long, unbroken list of fathers and sons say about who Jesus is?

Start from what the text states: an unbroken descent from Abraham, through David the king, down to Joseph and the birth of Jesus (vv.2-16). The point of such a list is legal and titular — it establishes Jesus' pedigree, proving He truly belongs to the family and nation from which the Messiah must arise (the brief calls it a pedigree given in evidence to prove a title). Keep the reading tied to the passage's own claim, not to added speculation.

Matthew traces God's promise down a long line of named people. On an index card, write (or draw) the people who have taught you about God — a parent, a grandparent, a teacher — making your own short line of the people God used to bring His Word to you. (Pre-reader variant: draw them, and a helper writes each name beside your picture.)

Observable action only: the child produces a card that lists or draws named people who taught them about God, echoing the passage's line of generations through whom God kept His promise. Note that a card was made and whom it names; do not assess anyone's faith, nor the depth or sincerity of the child's own belief.
This is a list mostly of fathers — but listen for the women. Which women are named in the genealogy?
Four women are named in the line — Tamar (v.3), Rahab and Ruth (v.5), and Uriah's wife (v.6) — besides Mary, the mother of Jesus (v.16).
Listen for a number that Matthew repeats at the very end. How many generations does he count in each of the three parts?
Fourteen. Verse 17 counts "fourteen generations from Abraham to David," fourteen more to the exile to Babylon, and fourteen from the exile to the Christ.
Listen for the one man in the whole list who is given a royal title. Which man is called a king?
David. Verse 6 alone attaches a royal title, naming him "David the king" — the throne the Messiah inherits.
This part of the story is about a man and a woman who were pledged to be married. What are their two names?
Mary and Joseph (v18).
Who appeared to Joseph, and how did he appear, according to verse 20?
“An angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream” (v20).
Before the angel spoke to him, what had Joseph already resolved to do about Mary?
He “resolved to divorce her quietly” (v19) — unwilling to disgrace her publicly. This was his plan before the angel redirected him in v20.

On four slips of paper, write these events, then put them in the order they happen in verses 19-24: (a) Joseph resolves to divorce Mary quietly; (b) the angel tells Joseph not to be afraid to take Mary as his wife; (c) Joseph wakes up; (d) Joseph embraces Mary as his wife.
Correct order: (a) v19, (b) v20, (c) v24, (d) v24. Joseph plans to divorce quietly, the angel redirects him in the dream, he wakes, and then he does as commanded and embraces Mary.
The angel tells Joseph to give the child the name Jesus. What reason does verse 21 give for that name?
“Because He will save His people from their sins” (v21). The name itself carries the reason: He saves His people from their sins.
Verse 23 gives the name Immanuel and then tells you what it means. What does Immanuel mean?
“God with us” (v23) — the passage supplies the meaning itself: “which means, ‘God with us’.”
As the passage is read aloud, listen for the two names the child is given. Hold up one finger for the first name and two fingers for the second when you hear them.
The two names are Jesus (v21, v25) and Immanuel (v23).
“She will give birth to a Son, and you are to give Him the name Jesus, because He will save His people from their sins.” (Matthew 1:21)
“Behold, the virgin will be with child and will give birth to a son, and they will call Him Immanuel” (which means, “God with us”). (Matthew 1:23)
Verse 22 says all this took place to fulfill what the Lord had said through the prophet. Turn to Isaiah 7:14 — how closely does the prophet's words there match the sign quoted in verse 23?

Named cross-reference: Isaiah 7:14 — “Behold, the virgin will be with child and will give birth to a son, and will call Him Immanuel.” Matthew 1:23 quotes it almost word for word, presenting this birth as the one fulfillment of that single prophesied sign (WCF 1.9: Scripture interprets Scripture).
Verse 21 says Jesus will save His people from their sins. Turn to Acts 4:12 — what does Peter say there about where salvation is found and about His name?

Named cross-reference: Acts 4:12 — “Salvation exists in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given to men by which we must be saved.” It confirms that the saving named in Matthew 1:21 is found in this name alone.
The passage says Joseph pondered these things before the angel came. What is one question you would have wanted to ask if you had been Joseph, waiting to understand what was happening?

Keep this open — the aim is the learner forming their own honest question, not receiving a model answer. Let the questions stand rather than resolving them on the spot; you may write them on a card to revisit.

Verse 22 says “all this took place to fulfill what the Lord had said through the prophet,” then verse 23 gives the prophecy. Using only verses 22-23, explain what the text says was fulfilled and why Matthew points it out.

Anchor first to what the text states: the prophecy of the virgin bearing a son called Immanuel (v23) is what “took place to fulfill what the Lord had said through the prophet” (v22). Matthew names it to show this birth is the announced fulfillment of that prophecy, not a coincidence — prophecy and fulfillment held together in one determinate sense. Keep learners in vv22-23; don't reach for doctrine the passage itself doesn't state.
In your own words, retell the whole passage: what Joseph first planned to do, what the angel told him in the dream, and what Joseph did when he woke up.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the passage rather than grading it against a fixed script. Look back together to see whether it touched: the child conceived through the Holy Spirit (v18); Joseph's plan to divorce Mary quietly (v19); the angel's words not to be afraid and the two names (vv20-21, 23); and Joseph waking and doing as commanded (vv24-25). If a part was missing, let the family re-read it and add it; don't supply a model retelling.

Verse 24 says that when Joseph woke up, “he did as the angel of the Lord had commanded him” — right away. Name one thing you have been asked to do and have been putting off. Write it on a card and do it today, without delay.

Observable action only: did they name one delayed task, write it down, and carry it out today? Do not evaluate willingness, attitude, or spiritual maturity — only whether the concrete step was taken. Model this on Joseph's prompt obedience in v24, not on measuring anyone's heart.
Who came from far away to look for the newborn King?
The Magi from the east. "Magi from the east arrived in Jerusalem," asking where the King of the Jews had been born.
In what town was Jesus born, according to this passage?
Bethlehem in Judea. The passage opens after Jesus "was born in Bethlehem in Judea," and the priests confirm it from the prophet: "In Bethlehem in Judea."
Listen for who became upset when they heard the Magi's question about a newborn King.
King Herod, and Jerusalem along with him: "he was disturbed, and all Jerusalem with him."
After the Magi left Herod, what did the star do?
It went ahead of them and stopped over the Child: "the star they had seen in the east went ahead of them until it stood over the place where the Child was."

Put these four events in the order they happen in the passage: the star stops over the Child; the Magi arrive in Jerusalem; Herod gathers the chief priests and scribes; the Magi return home by another route.

1) The Magi arrive in Jerusalem (v.1-2); 2) Herod gathers the chief priests and scribes to ask where the Christ was to be born (v.4); 3) the star stops over the place where the Child was (v.9); 4) warned in a dream, the Magi return home by another route (v.12).
In your own words, retell the visit of the Magi: where they came from and whom they sought, what Herod did when he heard, how the star led them, and what they did when they found the Child.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Magi from the east come to Jerusalem seeking the newborn King of the Jews, having seen His star (v.1-2); Herod and all Jerusalem are disturbed, and Herod learns from the chief priests and scribes that the Christ is to be born in Bethlehem (v.3-6); Herod secretly sends the Magi to search and report back (v.7-8); the star goes ahead and stops over the place where the Child was, and they rejoice (v.9-10); they find the Child with His mother Mary, fall down and worship, and give gold, frankincense, and myrrh (v.11); warned in a dream, they return home another way (v.12).
The Magi said they had come "to worship Him," and at the house "they fell down and worshiped Him." From what the Magi actually do at the house, what does worship mean here?

More than greeting a ruler politely. Worship here is the homage owed to God: the Magi fall down before the Child and present costly gifts. The word marks reverent, self-giving honor directed to Him as King, not mere civil courtesy.
The prophecy the priests quote calls the coming ruler one who "will be the shepherd of My people Israel." What does calling a ruler a shepherd suggest about the kind of king he will be?

The word joins ruling and caring. A shepherd-king does not merely command; he guides, feeds, and protects the flock. The prophecy pictures the promised ruler's kingship as pastoral care over God's people, not domination like Herod's.
“Where is the One who has been born King of the Jews? We saw His star in the east and have come to worship Him.” (Matthew 2:2)
The chief priests and scribes answer Herod by quoting a prophet who foretold that a ruler would come out of Bethlehem. Which Old Testament prophet did they quote?
Micah. The priests quote Micah 5:2, which foretells that out of Bethlehem will come the ruler of God's people. Scripture interprets Scripture: the prophet's words explain the event unfolding here.
The Magi followed a star to the King. Which prophecy in the book of Numbers speaks of a star rising out of Jacob and a scepter from Israel?

Numbers 24:17, Balaam's oracle of a star out of Jacob and a scepter out of Israel. It is a fitting Old Testament backdrop to the star that leads the Magi to Israel's King, though the passage names only the star itself.
The Magi present gold, frankincense, and myrrh to the King. Which psalm foretells distant kings bringing gifts and tribute to God's King?

Psalm 72:10, where kings of distant lands bring gifts and tribute to the King (Isaiah 60:6 likewise pictures gold and frankincense brought to Him). The Magi's gifts in verse 11 echo this pattern of far-off worshipers honoring the King.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you have — about the star, about the Magi's long journey, or about why Herod was so troubled?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder how the star moved, how far the Magi traveled, why Herod pretended he wanted to worship, or why the priests knew the prophecy yet did not go. Receive whatever question they bring rather than steering them to one you prefer; it is fine to say some of it stays a mystery.

The chief priests and scribes could say exactly where the Christ would be born, yet they did not travel the few miles to Bethlehem with the Magi. What does the passage show by setting the seeking Magi beside the informed priests?

Anchor first in what the text states: the priests answer correctly from the prophet (v.4-6), yet only the Magi go and worship (v.9-11). The passage contrasts knowing about the King with going to Him. Let learners notice that right information is not the same as coming to worship; avoid turning this into a verdict on anyone's heart — the text shows behavior, not a judgment of the priests' souls.
When Herod asks where the Christ was to be born, the priests answer straight from the prophet (v.5-6). How does the passage use that prophecy to explain the event that is taking place?

Stay with what the text does: the priests read Micah's words about Bethlehem, and the narrative shows the birth happening exactly there. The passage lets the prophecy interpret the event — Scripture explaining Scripture — so the reader sees the birth of Jesus as the ruler the prophet foretold. Keep the discussion on how the text ties prophecy to fulfillment, not on speculation beyond what is written.
The Magi traveled far, searched carefully, and worshiped the King when they found Him. What is one concrete thing you can do this week to seek Jesus and honor Him?

Look for a specific, doable action, not a feeling: a set time to read the passage again, memorizing verse 2, giving something of value to serve someone, or bowing in prayer the way the Magi bowed. Help each person name one observable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not grade their devotion.
On paper, draw the Magi following the star to the house, and draw the Child with His mother Mary where the star stops. A grown-up can label who is who.

The drawing should show: the Magi, the star going ahead and stopping over the house, and the Child with His mother Mary inside. In the passage they "saw the Child with His mother Mary," and no other people are named at the house.
On an index card, list the three gifts the Magi gave the King (gold, frankincense, and myrrh). Then write or draw one gift of your own — something you will actually do this week to honor Jesus.

The three named gifts are gold, frankincense, and myrrh (v.11); confirm the child names all three. For the personal gift, look for a concrete, observable action they can carry out — a kind deed, a time of prayer, memorizing the verse. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or measuring their sincerity.
In verse 13, who appeared to Joseph, and how did he appear?
'An angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream' (v.13).

verse.en: When the Magi had gone, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream.

text.en: The angel names the group Joseph is to take with him. Which two people, besides Joseph, does the passage say he took?
'The Child and His mother' (vv.13-14). Joseph 'took the Child and His mother by night, and withdrew to Egypt' (v.14).

verse.en: So he got up, took the Child and His mother by night, and withdrew to Egypt,

text.en: As the passage is read, listen for the one country the angel tells Joseph to take the Child to. What place is named?
Egypt. The angel says, 'flee to Egypt' (v.13), and Joseph 'withdrew to Egypt' (v.14).
According to verse 13, whom does the angel name as the danger, and what does that person intend to do?
Herod. The angel warns, 'Herod is going to search for the Child to kill Him' (v.13).

verse.en: for Herod is going to search for the Child to kill Him.

text.en: Put these events in the order the passage gives them: Joseph withdrew to Egypt; the angel appeared to Joseph in a dream; Joseph stayed until the death of Herod; Joseph got up and took the Child and His mother by night.

Order: (1) the angel appeared to Joseph in a dream (v.13); (2) he got up and took the Child and His mother by night (v.14); (3) he withdrew to Egypt (v.14); (4) he stayed until the death of Herod (v.15).
Listen for the time of day when Joseph set out for Egypt. What words tell you when he left?
'By night.' The text says he 'took the Child and His mother by night, and withdrew to Egypt' (v.14).
The angel told Joseph to stay 'until I tell you' (v.13). What event does verse 15 give as the actual end of the stay in Egypt?
The death of Herod. Joseph stayed in Egypt 'until the death of Herod' (v.15).

verse.en: where he stayed until the death of Herod.

text.en: The angel's command is to 'flee to Egypt' (v.13). Within this passage, what role does Egypt serve for the Child and His family?

Egypt is a place of refuge from Herod's threat. The command to 'flee to Egypt' (v.13) sends the family out of Herod's reach so that they are kept safe there 'until the death of Herod' (v.15). Egypt, once a house of bondage, is here made a house of refuge.
The angel says, 'Stay there until I tell you' (v.13). What do the words 'until I tell you' show about how Joseph was to wait?
Joseph is not told how long to stay; he must remain 'until I tell you' (v.13), waiting on further word from God and kept in continual dependence on Him rather than acting on his own timing.
where he stayed until the death of Herod. This fulfilled what the Lord had spoken through the prophet: “Out of Egypt I called My Son.” (Matthew 2:15, BSB)

When the Magi had gone, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream. “Get up!” he said. “Take the Child and His mother and flee to Egypt. Stay there until I tell you, for Herod is going to search for the Child to kill Him.” (Matthew 2:13, BSB)
Finish the prophet's words quoted in verse 15 from memory: 'Out of Egypt I ___.'
'...called My Son' (v.15). The full line is 'Out of Egypt I called My Son.'

verse.en: This fulfilled what the Lord had spoken through the prophet: “Out of Egypt I called My Son.”

text.en: Retell the whole passage in your own words, from the angel's warning in the dream to the prophet's word that it fulfilled.

A full retelling covers: after the Magi had gone, an angel of the Lord appeared to Joseph in a dream and told him to take the Child and His mother and flee to Egypt, staying until told, because Herod meant to kill the Child (v.13); Joseph got up and left by night for Egypt (v.14); he stayed there until Herod died, and this fulfilled the prophet's word, 'Out of Egypt I called My Son' (v.15).
Verse 15 says the flight fulfilled the prophet's word, 'Out of Egypt I called My Son.' Hosea 11:1 first spoke those words of Israel. What does Matthew show by applying them to the Child?

Hosea 11:1 (named in the brief) says, 'When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called My son.' Matthew reads this word of Israel's youth as finding a fuller accomplishment in Christ, by way of analogy: the pattern of God calling His son out of Egypt recurs in the Child. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
The passage calls the Child God's 'Son' brought out of Egypt (v.15). In Exodus 4:22 God calls another His firstborn son. Who is that, and how does it connect to this fulfillment?

In Exodus 4:22 (named in the brief) God says, 'Israel is My firstborn son.' Israel is the original pattern God once called out of Egypt; Matthew applies the 'Out of Egypt I called My Son' pattern by analogy to Christ (v.15), the greater Son. The deliverance-from-Egypt pattern recurs.
In verse 13 an angel of the Lord guides Joseph in a dream. Matthew 1:20 records an earlier time this happened. How are the two moments alike?

Matthew 1:20 (named in the brief) says, 'an angel of the Lord appeared to him in a dream,' guiding Joseph about Mary. As here in v.13, God directs Joseph through an angel in a dream — the same means of guidance recurring at another turn in the story.
When you hear that God sent the Child all the way to Egypt to keep Him safe, what is one thing you want to ask about it?

Receive the child's own question; do not supply an answer. Good wonderings might touch why Egypt, how long they stayed, or how it felt to leave at night. Write the question down so it can be carried into the reading.
God warns Joseph and sends the family away rather than stopping Herod some other way. What question does that choice raise for you that you would want to explore?
Invite the learner's own question; resist answering it for them. The aim is to surface genuine wondering about how God preserves the Child through an ordinary flight, not to test recall.

Verse 15 states plainly that the flight to Egypt 'fulfilled what the Lord had spoken through the prophet.' Why does Matthew attach this fulfillment note to what looks like an ordinary escape from danger?

First anchor to the text: v.15 says the stay in Egypt 'fulfilled what the Lord had spoken through the prophet: "Out of Egypt I called My Son."' Matthew reads the escape not as mere flight but as God's providence carrying forward the prophetic pattern — the Son called out of Egypt. The link to Hosea is by analogy, not correction (WCF 1.9). Keep the point to what the text states.
Verse 14 does not merely say Joseph left; it says he 'got up' and took the family 'by night.' What does the passage stress by telling us how and when he responded?

Anchor to the text: as soon as the angel spoke, 'he got up, took the Child and His mother by night, and withdrew to Egypt' (v.14). The detail 'by night' stresses prompt, unquestioning obedience — Joseph does not wait for morning but acts at once on God's word. Keep the point to what the text shows.
Joseph 'got up' and obeyed that same night (v.14). Name one thing you already know you should do but have been putting off, and one concrete step you will take on it this week without delay.

Look for a specific, doable step tied to a real duty the learner names (mirroring Joseph's prompt action in v.14). Record only the observable plan or deed, never a verdict on the learner's diligence, character, or spiritual state.
Draw a picture of Joseph taking the Child and His mother to Egypt at night to keep the Child safe. Then point to your picture and tell who is in it and where they are going.

Doable with paper and crayons. The drawing should show the family leaving for Egypt by night (vv.13-14). Affirm what the child names and drew as observable evidence of what they noticed; do not record any judgment about the child.
In this passage, in which town did Herod put the little boys to death?
Bethlehem. The passage says Herod "put to death all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under."
Which prophet does the passage name — the one whose words were fulfilled?
Jeremiah. The passage says, "what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah was fulfilled."

This passage names two places: Bethlehem, where the boys were, and Ramah, where a voice of weeping is heard. On paper, write each place name and draw a small picture beside it. Beside Ramah, draw Rachel weeping for her children.

Two places: Bethlehem (v.16) and Ramah (v.18). The passage also names Rachel, "weeping for her children, and refusing to be comforted." Check that both place names appear and are spelled from the text.
Draw Herod, the king in this passage. Then draw the little town of Bethlehem where the boys lived.
The passage names Herod (v.16) and the town of Bethlehem (v.16). There is no wrong way to draw them; use the drawing to check the child can name who and where.
What did Herod feel when he saw he had been outwitted by the Magi, and what did he do because of it?
He was "filled with rage," and because of it he "put to death all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under." The feeling comes first, then the action.
Listen for what Herod does right after he realizes the Magi have outwitted him.
He is "filled with rage" and gives orders to put to death all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity who were two years old and under, according to the time he had learned from the Magi (v.16).
In your own words, retell what happens in this passage: what Herod does when he learns the Magi have outwitted him, and how the passage says this fulfills what the prophet Jeremiah had spoken.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Herod sees he has been outwitted by the Magi and is filled with rage (v.16); he orders the death of all the boys in Bethlehem and its vicinity two years old and under, based on the time he learned from the Magi (v.16); the passage says this fulfilled what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah (v.17) — the voice heard in Ramah, Rachel weeping for her children and refusing to be comforted (v.18).
The passage says what the prophet Jeremiah spoke was "fulfilled." What does "fulfilled" mean here?

That the words the prophet had spoken were brought to pass — they came true, accomplished in this event. The passage points from what Herod did back to what Jeremiah had already said, so that Scripture's word is shown to hold even over this grief.
The passage describes Rachel "refusing to be comforted, because they are no more." What does this phrase tell us about the depth of the grief?

It is grief so deep that comfort is turned away — she will not be consoled. The reason given is "because they are no more": the children are gone. The words name real, inconsolable mourning, held here inside the fulfillment of Scripture rather than explained away.
Listen for the word the passage says twice when it describes the sound heard in Ramah.
"Weeping." Verse 18 says, "weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children." The word weeping is used twice.
“A voice is heard in Ramah, weeping and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children, and refusing to be comforted, because they are no more.” (Matthew 2:18)
Verse 18 quotes an earlier prophecy word for word. Which verse in the book of Jeremiah does it come from?

Jeremiah 31:15, which reads, "A voice is heard in Ramah, mourning and great weeping, Rachel weeping for her children, and refusing to be comforted, because they are no more." Matthew quotes it almost exactly; note that Jeremiah's wording is "mourning and great weeping" where Matthew has "weeping and great mourning." This is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
This is a very sad passage. After hearing it, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why Herod was so angry, why God let this happen, where Jesus was, or what it means that it was "fulfilled." Receive the question they bring rather than steering them to one you'd prefer; it is fine to say that some of this stays hard and that Scripture lets the grief be real.
The passage sets a tyrant's cruelty right beside the words "was fulfilled." What question does holding those two things together raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask how prophecy and human evil relate, why Scripture records such grief, or what comfort the fulfillment offers. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer. The brief notes the mourning is real and inconsolable, yet stands inside the sure fulfillment of prophecy.

The passage first tells what Herod did, then says, "what was spoken through the prophet Jeremiah was fulfilled." What two things is the passage connecting, and why might Matthew want the reader to see that connection?

Anchor first in the text: the event (Herod's slaughter of the boys of Bethlehem, v.16) is joined to the prophecy of Jeremiah quoted in v.18. Matthew shows that even this grief did not fall outside what Scripture had spoken — the Word foresaw and encompassed it. The brief notes a double horizon in v.18: the weeping was heard first in Jeremiah's day and now again at Bethlehem. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.

This passage names people "weeping" and "refusing to be comforted." This week, do one specific, kind thing for someone you know who is sad or grieving. Afterward, write down what you did and what happened.

No right answer to check. Look for a genuine, specific act toward a real person (not a vague intention), and record what was done as evidence. Do not judge whether the act "worked" or assess the learner's compassion — note only the observable action.
In this passage, who appeared to Joseph in a dream and told him to take the Child and His mother back to the land of Israel?
An angel of the Lord. The passage says, "an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph in Egypt."
In which town did Joseph go and live with the Child and His mother?
Nazareth. The passage says, "he went and lived in a town called Nazareth."
Who was reigning in Judea in place of his father Herod, so that Joseph was afraid to go there?
Archelaus. The passage says, "when he learned that Archelaus was reigning in Judea in place of his father Herod, he was afraid to go there."

This passage names several places on the family's journey: Egypt, the land of Israel, Judea, Galilee, and the town of Nazareth. On paper, write each place name and draw a simple arrow-path from where they started to where they ended.

The places named are Egypt (v.19), the land of Israel (vv.20-21), Judea (v.22), the district of Galilee (v.22), and the town of Nazareth (v.23). The journey runs from Egypt toward the land of Israel, away from Judea, and settles in Nazareth in Galilee. Check that the place names appear and are spelled from the text.
Draw the three people who travel together in this passage: Joseph, the Child, and His mother.
The passage names Joseph, the Child, and His mother traveling together (vv.20-21). There is no wrong way to draw them; use the drawing to check the child can name the three who go together.
What happened first in this passage: did the angel appear to Joseph, or did Herod die?
Herod died first. The passage opens, "After Herod died, an angel of the Lord appeared in a dream to Joseph." The death comes before the angel's appearing.
After Joseph came into the land of Israel, why did he not settle in Judea, and where did he go instead?

He learned that Archelaus was reigning in Judea and was afraid to go there; then, "having been warned in a dream, he withdrew to the district of Galilee" and lived in Nazareth (vv.22-23). The fear and the dream-warning come first, then the move to Galilee.
Listen for the two separate dreams in this passage and what Joseph does right after each one.

First dream: the angel tells Joseph to get up and take the Child and His mother to the land of Israel, and Joseph gets up and goes (vv.19-21). Second dream: warned about Judea, he withdraws to Galilee and lives in Nazareth (vv.22-23). Each command is followed at once by Joseph's obedience.
In your own words, retell what happens in this passage: how the angel tells Joseph to return, how Joseph obeys, why he turns aside from Judea, and where the family finally settles.

Checklist for a complete retelling: after Herod died, an angel tells Joseph in a dream to take the Child and His mother to the land of Israel because those seeking the Child's life are dead (vv.19-20); Joseph gets up and goes (v.21); learning Archelaus reigns in Judea, he is afraid, and warned in a dream he withdraws to Galilee (v.22); he settles in a town called Nazareth, fulfilling what was spoken through the prophets, "He will be called a Nazarene" (v.23).
The passage says Joseph "withdrew to the district of Galilee." What does the word "withdrew" tell you about how and why he went?

To withdraw is to draw back or pull away from something — here, away from Judea and from the danger of Archelaus. The word carries the sense of a careful, deliberate turning aside rather than a bold advance; Joseph moves out of harm's way toward Galilee under God's warning.
The passage ends, "He will be called a Nazarene." What does the word "Nazarene" mean here?

Most plainly, a Nazarene is one from Nazareth, the town where Jesus grew up (v.23). The brief notes the name carried a note of lowliness and even contempt (compare the later question whether anything good could come from Nazareth), and by allusion points to the lowly Branch, the shoot from Jesse's stump (Isaiah 11:1). The passage ties the humble settling in Nazareth to what "was spoken through the prophets."
Listen for the name of the town where the family goes to live at the end of the passage.
Nazareth. Verse 23 says, "he went and lived in a town called Nazareth."
“Get up!” he said. “Take the Child and His mother and go to the land of Israel, for those seeking the Child’s life are now dead.” (Matthew 2:20)
“So was fulfilled what was spoken through the prophets: “He will be called a Nazarene.”” (Matthew 2:23)

The angel tells Joseph that "those seeking the Child’s life are now dead," which echoes God's words to another fleeing servant long before. In which verse of Exodus does the LORD tell Moses to return to Egypt because those who sought his life are dead?

Exodus 4:19, where the LORD tells Moses in Midian, "Go back to Egypt, for all the men who sought to kill you are dead." As with Moses, so with the Child: the persecutor dies and God calls the family back. This is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Nazareth, where the family settles, was a lowly, little-regarded town. In which verse of John's Gospel does Nathanael ask whether anything good can come from Nazareth?

John 1:46, where Nathanael asks, "Can anything good come from Nazareth?" and Philip answers, "Come and see." It shows the low esteem attached to the name Nazarene, the very name this passage says was spoken through the prophets. This is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why the family had to move so many times, why Joseph was afraid, what a dream from God is like, or what Nazarene means. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
God guides Joseph one step at a time — first back to the land of Israel, then, by a further warning, aside to Galilee. What question does that step-by-step guiding raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why God did not give the whole route at once, how they discern his leading in stages, or how fear and obedience sit together in Joseph. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.

The passage turns from plain travel to the words, "So was fulfilled what was spoken through the prophets: ‘He will be called a Nazarene.’" What two things is the passage joining, and why might Matthew want the reader to see that even settling in Nazareth answers the prophets?

Anchor first in the text: the event (the family settling in the town of Nazareth, v.23) is joined to what "was spoken through the prophets." Matthew shows that even this humble, out-of-the-way resettlement did not fall outside God's word but fulfilled it. The brief notes the name Nazarene carries both lowliness (a despised town) and an allusion to the lowly Branch of Isaiah 11:1 — the whole family's journey is under God's guiding, prophesied word. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.

In this passage Joseph does what God tells him right away — twice. This week, the next time a parent or leader asks you to do something, do it right away. Afterward, write down what you were asked and what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, real instance the child recorded — what was asked and what was done — as observable evidence. Do not judge the child's attitude or obedience in general; note only the single observable action they wrote down.
In this passage, who came preaching in the wilderness?
John the Baptist. The passage opens, "In those days John the Baptist came, preaching in the wilderness of Judea."
Which two groups does the passage name as coming to John's place of baptism?
The Pharisees and Sadducees. The passage says, "when John saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees coming to his place of baptism."
The passage says people came out to John from three places or regions. On paper, list the three the passage names, then draw a simple map with a river running through it.

The passage says, "People went out to him from Jerusalem and all Judea and the whole region around the Jordan." The three: Jerusalem, all Judea, and the whole region around the Jordan. The river to draw is the Jordan River (v.6).
This passage tells us what John wore and what he ate. Draw John in his camel's hair coat and leather belt, and draw his food beside him.

The passage says, "John wore a garment of camel's hair, with a leather belt around his waist. His food was locusts and wild honey." Use the drawing to check the child can name the clothing (camel's hair garment, leather belt) and the food (locusts and wild honey).
Which prophet does the passage name as the one who spoke of John beforehand?
Isaiah. The passage says, "This is he who was spoken of through the prophet Isaiah."
According to the passage, what were the people doing as they were baptized by John in the Jordan?
They were confessing their sins. The passage says, "Confessing their sins, they were baptized by him in the Jordan River." The confessing and the baptizing go together.
Listen for what the people do first when they come out to John at the river, before he says anything to the Pharisees and Sadducees.

category.en: Listen for the order of events

text.en: The people come out to John from Jerusalem, all Judea, and the whole region around the Jordan (v.5), and confessing their sins they are baptized by him in the Jordan River (v.6). Only afterward does John speak to the Pharisees and Sadducees (v.7).

In your own words, retell what happens in this passage: John's preaching in the wilderness, the crowds who come to be baptized, his words to the Pharisees and Sadducees, and what he says about the One coming after him.

Checklist for a complete retelling: John the Baptist preaches in the wilderness of Judea, calling people to repent because the kingdom of heaven is near (v.1-2); he is the voice Isaiah spoke of, and he wears camel's hair and eats locusts and wild honey (v.3-4); crowds come from Jerusalem, Judea, and around the Jordan, confessing their sins and being baptized (v.5-6); John warns the Pharisees and Sadducees to produce fruit in keeping with repentance and not to trust in Abraham as their father (v.7-10); he says One more powerful is coming who will baptize with the Holy Spirit and with fire, gathering the wheat and burning the chaff (v.11-12).
John's first word is "Repent." Using the passage, what does John show that real repentance produces?

Repentance is a change of mind that shows itself in a changed life. The passage defines it by its evidence: "Produce fruit, then, in keeping with repentance" (v.8). It is not mere descent from Abraham (v.9) but fruit — the reformation that befits a true penitent.
John gives one reason people should repent. In the passage's own words, what is that reason?

Because the kingdom of heaven is near. John says, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near" (v.2). The nearness of the kingdom is the ground of the summons; repentance is the fitting preparation for it.
Listen for the word John uses for the changed living he demands, when he tells the crowd to produce something in keeping with repentance.

The word is "fruit." John says, "Produce fruit, then, in keeping with repentance" (v.8), and warns that "every tree that does not produce good fruit will be cut down" (v.10). Fruit is the visible evidence of a real change.
"Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." (Matthew 3:2)
"Produce fruit, then, in keeping with repentance." (Matthew 3:8)

"I baptize you with water for repentance, but after me will come One more powerful than I, whose sandals I am not worthy to carry. He will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire." (Matthew 3:11)
The passage says John is the one "spoken of through the prophet Isaiah" as a voice calling to prepare the Lord's way. Which passage in Isaiah does this point back to?

Isaiah 40:3, the prophesied voice preparing the way for the Lord. Matthew 3:3 applies that prophecy to John: "A voice of one calling in the wilderness, 'Prepare the way for the Lord, make straight paths for Him.'" Scripture interprets Scripture — the Gospel names John as the fulfillment of Isaiah's word.
John preaches, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." Later in Matthew, Jesus begins His own preaching with the very same summons. Where?

Matthew 4:17, where Jesus takes up John's same summons: from that time Jesus began to preach, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." The forerunner's message and the Lord's message are one call.
John lived in the wilderness, wore camel's hair, and ate locusts and wild honey. What is one thing you would like to ask John about the way he lived?

Invite the child's own wondering; do not supply an answer. Good questions might be about why John lived in the wilderness, what locusts taste like, or why he dressed so plainly. Receive the question itself as the fruit of this item; the passage gives the details (v.4) but leaves room to wonder.

John greets the Pharisees and Sadducees sharply: "You brood of vipers, who warned you to flee from the coming wrath?" What question does his warning raise for you about how a person prepares for the coming One?

Let the learner frame their own question; do not answer it for them. The passage itself presses that fleeing wrath is not enough — fruit must follow (v.8) and Abrahamic descent secures nothing (v.9). A strong question owns the discomfort of John's words rather than softening them. Keep this on the text; resist turning it into a verdict on anyone present.
John says, "The axe lies ready at the root of the trees." First, what does the passage say happens to a tree that does not produce good fruit? Then, why might John picture people as trees?

What the text says (anchor first): "every tree that does not produce good fruit will be cut down and thrown into the fire" (v.10). Then the why: the tree is a picture for a person, and its fruit is the visible evidence of real repentance (v.8). As a tree is known and judged by its fruit, so a life is; profession without fruit does not stand. Meaning is drawn from the passage's own fruit-language, not imported.

John pictures the coming One with a winnowing fork, gathering wheat into the barn but burning the chaff. What does the passage state the coming One will do, and what does the wheat-and-chaff image show about His work?

Anchor first in what the text says: "His winnowing fork is in His hand to clear His threshing floor and to gather His wheat into the barn; but He will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire" (v.12). The image: on a threshing floor the fork tosses the grain so that heavy wheat falls and light chaff blows off. It pictures the coming One separating and gathering His own from what is worthless — the same One who "will baptize you with the Holy Spirit and with fire" (v.11). Keep the reading inside the passage's own harvest imagery; do not speculate beyond it.

John says, "Produce fruit, then, in keeping with repentance." On an index card, write one specific, doable action this week that would be visible fruit of a changed mind — something another person could actually see you do.

Look for a concrete, observable action (for example, returning something borrowed, apologizing to a named person, doing a chore unasked), not a feeling or a resolve to 'be better.' Keep the response about a doable deed; do not turn it into an assessment of the learner's faith or heart. The passage grounds this: fruit is the visible keeping-company of repentance (v.8).
In this passage, who baptized Jesus?
John. The passage says Jesus came "to be baptized by John."
The passage names where Jesus came from and where He came to. From what region did He come, and to what river?
He came from Galilee to the Jordan. The passage says Jesus "came from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John."
Listen for the two people who meet at the river in this passage.
Jesus and John. Jesus comes to the Jordan to be baptized, and John is the one baptizing (v.13-14).
Draw the river Jordan with Jesus and John standing in it. Then draw the dove coming down from above.

The passage names the Jordan (v.13), Jesus and John (v.13-14), and the Spirit of God descending "like a dove" (v.16). There is no wrong way to draw them; use the drawing to check the child can name who is there and where.
When Jesus comes to be baptized, what does John try to do at first?
John tries to stop Him. The passage says, "But John tried to prevent Him," and John says, "I need to be baptized by You, and do You come to me?" (v.14).
After Jesus is baptized and comes up out of the water, the passage reports three things that happen. What are they, in the order the passage gives them?

In order: (1) "the heavens were opened"; (2) He saw "the Spirit of God descending like a dove and resting on Him"; (3) "a voice from heaven" said, "This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased!" (v.16-17).

On paper, write these five moments from the passage in the order they happen: the voice from heaven speaks; Jesus comes from Galilee to the Jordan; John tries to prevent Him; the Spirit descends like a dove; Jesus goes up out of the water.

Correct order: (1) Jesus comes from Galilee to the Jordan (v.13); (2) John tries to prevent Him (v.14); (3) Jesus goes up out of the water (v.16); (4) the Spirit descends like a dove (v.16); (5) the voice from heaven speaks (v.17).
Listen for what happens in the sky right after Jesus comes up out of the water.
The heavens were opened, and the Spirit of God came down like a dove and rested on Him (v.16), and then a voice from heaven spoke (v.17).
In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: how Jesus came to be baptized, the exchange between John and Jesus, and what happened when Jesus came up out of the water.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus comes from Galilee to the Jordan to be baptized by John (v.13); John tries to prevent Him, saying he needs to be baptized by Jesus (v.14); Jesus replies that it is fitting for them to fulfill all righteousness this way, and John permits Him (v.15); as soon as Jesus is baptized He goes up out of the water, the heavens open, and the Spirit of God descends like a dove and rests on Him (v.16); a voice from heaven says, "This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased!" (v.17).
Jesus says it is fitting "to fulfill all righteousness in this way." What does "fulfill all righteousness" mean here?

That Jesus takes up and completes everything righteousness requires — owning every divine institution and binding Himself to the whole obedience God asks. He does not need cleansing from sin; He submits to this baptism because it is right and proper for Him to do all that righteousness calls for. Carry the text's plain sense; do not sharpen it beyond what the passage says.
The voice from heaven calls Jesus "My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." What do the words "beloved" and "well pleased" tell us about how the Father regards the Son?

"Beloved" names the Father's dear, particular love for His Son; "well pleased" names a settled, complete delight resting on Him.
