Together they declare who Jesus is and the Father's full approval of Him as He begins His public work. Keep to the text's own words; do not add doctrine the passage does not state.
The passage says the Spirit of God was "descending like a dove and resting on Him." What does the word "resting" add beyond simply "descending"?

"Descending" is the coming down; "resting" is the staying. The Spirit does not merely touch Jesus and pass, but comes down and abides on Him. The word marks the Spirit settling and remaining on Him as He enters His work. Hold the text's sense without pressing it further than the passage does.
Listen for the bird that the passage says the Spirit of God came down like.
A dove. The passage says He saw "the Spirit of God descending like a dove and resting on Him" (v.16).
“And a voice from heaven said, ‘This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased!’” (Matthew 3:17)
“Let it be so now,” Jesus replied. “It is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness in this way.” (Matthew 3:15)
Later in Matthew, on a mountain, the Father speaks almost these same words over Jesus again. In which passage does that happen?

Matthew 17:5, at the Transfiguration, where a voice from the cloud says, "This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased. Listen to Him!" The Father's word at the baptism is spoken again at the Transfiguration — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
The Father's delight in His Son echoes an earlier prophecy of the Servant whom God upholds and in whom His soul delights. Which Old Testament passage names that Servant?

Isaiah 42:1, the first Servant song, where God speaks of His chosen Servant, upheld by Him, His elect in whom His soul delights, on whom He puts His Spirit. The voice at the baptism echoes this prophecy — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). (Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote Isaiah.)
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why John did not want to baptize Jesus, why the Spirit looked like a dove, who was speaking from heaven, or what "well pleased" means. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
John protests that he needs to be baptized by Jesus, yet Jesus insists on being baptized by John. What question does that reversal raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why the sinless one submits to a baptism of repentance, what "fulfill all righteousness" required, or how the Father, Son, and Spirit each appear here. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
The passage says John tried to prevent Jesus, saying, "I need to be baptized by You, and do You come to me?" What do John's own words show about why he held back?

Anchor first in the text: John's words (v.14) show he saw himself as the lesser one — he is the one who ought to be baptized by Jesus, not the reverse. He held back out of a sense that the order was upside down: the servant would sooner be baptized by the Master. Draw the meaning from what John says; do not add beyond the text.

First the passage tells what Jesus does — He humbly submits to baptism; then the heavens open, the Spirit descends, and the voice speaks. How do those events from heaven answer what Jesus has just done?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus insists on baptism to "fulfill all righteousness" (v.15) and is baptized (v.16); then heaven responds — the heavens open, the Spirit of God descends and rests on Him, and the Father's voice names Him "My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased" (v.16-17). The heavenly signs interpret the act: as the Son humbly submits, the Father and the Spirit publicly attest who He is. The brief notes Father, Son, and Spirit appear together here. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.

Jesus did what was "fitting" and right even when John thought it should be the other way. This week, name one thing you know is right to do but would rather skip — then do it. Afterward, write down what you did and what happened.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable action the learner actually carried out (not a vague intention), and record what was done as evidence. Do not judge the learner's obedience or heart — note only the observable action.
In this passage, who heard about John and then withdrew to Galilee?
Jesus. The passage says, "When Jesus heard that John had been imprisoned, He withdrew to Galilee."
After leaving Nazareth, in which town did Jesus go and live?
Capernaum. The passage says, "Leaving Nazareth, He went and lived in Capernaum, which is by the sea in the region of Zebulun and Naphtali."
Which prophet does the passage name — the one whose words are being fulfilled?
Isaiah. The passage says the move was "to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah."

This passage names several places Jesus moved through: Galilee, Nazareth, and Capernaum, which is by the sea in the region of Zebulun and Naphtali. On paper, write each place name, then draw an arrow from Nazareth to Capernaum to show where Jesus went to live.

Places named: Galilee (v.12), Nazareth and Capernaum (v.13), and the region of Zebulun and Naphtali (v.13); the quoted prophecy also names the Way of the Sea, beyond the Jordan, and Galilee of the Gentiles (v.15). For this activity check that the arrow runs from Nazareth to Capernaum, matching "Leaving Nazareth, He went and lived in Capernaum."
In this passage Jesus goes to live in a town by the sea called Capernaum. Draw the sea, and draw Jesus beside it.
The passage says Jesus "went and lived in Capernaum, which is by the sea" (v.13). There is no wrong way to draw it; use the picture to check the child can say Jesus went to live in a town by the sea.
What did Jesus hear that came first, and what did He do next because of it?
First Jesus heard "that John had been imprisoned"; then, because of it, "He withdrew to Galilee." The hearing comes first, the withdrawing next (v.12).
Listen for what Jesus begins to do at the very end of the passage, after He has settled in Capernaum.
He begins to preach. Verse 17 says, "From that time on Jesus began to preach, 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near.'" This is the last thing the passage reports.
In your own words, retell what happens in this passage, in order: what Jesus heard, where He went, where He settled, and what He began to do.

Checklist for a complete retelling, in order: Jesus hears that John had been imprisoned and withdraws to Galilee (v.12); leaving Nazareth, He goes and lives in Capernaum by the sea, in the region of Zebulun and Naphtali (v.13); this fulfills what the prophet Isaiah spoke about a great light dawning on the people in darkness (vv.14-16); from that time on Jesus begins to preach, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near" (v.17).
The passage says Jesus "withdrew to Galilee." What does the word withdrew tell us about the movement He made?

To withdraw is to draw back or move away from one place to another — here, away from where John's arrest had happened and back to Galilee. The word signals a deliberate change of location in response to what He had heard (v.12), not a flight of panic.
The quoted prophecy speaks of those living in "the land of the shadow of death." Reading it alongside "the people living in darkness" in the same verse, what condition is this phrase describing?

A settled condition of deep darkness and nearness to death — the verse pairs "the people living in darkness" with those "living in the land of the shadow of death" (v.16). The two phrases describe the same benighted, endangered people, upon whom "a light has dawned." The brief notes this is a settled, contented, endangered posture on the borders of death.
Listen for the first word Jesus preaches when He begins His preaching at the end of the passage.
"Repent." Verse 17 says, "Jesus began to preach, 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near.'" Repent is the first word of what He preaches.
“the people living in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of the shadow of death, a light has dawned.” (Matthew 4:16)
“From that time on Jesus began to preach, ‘Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near.’” (Matthew 4:17)
Verses 15-16 quote a prophecy about darkness turning to light in Zebulun and Naphtali. Which passage in the book of Isaiah is Matthew drawing from?

Isaiah 9:1-2. Isaiah 9:2 reads, "The people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of the shadow of death, a light has dawned," and Isaiah 9:1 names the land of Zebulun and Naphtali and "Galilee of the nations." Matthew applies this to Jesus' move to Galilee, writing "the people living in darkness" and "Galilee of the Gentiles." This is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Jesus begins to preach, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." Earlier in this same book, another preacher had said those exact words. Who was it, and where?

John the Baptist, in Matthew 3:2, which says, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." Jesus takes up the very summons John had preached (see MAT-010 v.17 and Matthew 3:2). Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
The passage says a great light dawned on people in darkness. After hearing it, what is one question you would like to ask about this passage?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what the light is, why the people were in darkness, why Jesus moved to a new town, or what happened to John. Receive the question they bring rather than steering them to one you'd prefer; write it down as their own wondering.
The passage sets John's imprisonment right beside the dawning of a great light and the start of Jesus' preaching. What question does holding those together raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask how John's arrest and Jesus' new beginning relate, why the light dawns in Galilee rather than Jerusalem, or what it means that Jesus takes up John's own message. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.

The passage reports that Jesus moved to Galilee "to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah." How does Matthew connect the region Jesus moved to with the images of darkness and light in the quotation?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus settles in Capernaum in "the region of Zebulun and Naphtali" (v.13), and the quoted prophecy names exactly "Land of Zebulun and land of Naphtali" and "Galilee of the Gentiles" as the place where "the people living in darkness have seen a great light" (vv.15-16). Matthew maps the geography of Jesus' move onto the geography of the prophecy: the very region Isaiah called dark is where Christ, the light, now comes to live and preach. The brief notes the reading move is image-to-referent — Isaiah's light/darkness applied to Galilee. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.

The passage shifts at verse 17: after reporting that prophecy is fulfilled, it turns to what Jesus began to preach. Why might Matthew place the summons "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near" right after the light has dawned?

Anchor in the text: vv.14-16 report the fulfillment (the light dawning on the people in darkness), and v.17 turns from report to Jesus' own preached word, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." The passage lets the dawning light give way to a spoken call: the light that has come is not only seen but summons a response. The brief notes the shift at v.17 from fulfillment-report to Jesus' preached summons. Keep the meaning within what the text says; do not press it past the passage's own words.

Jesus' first preached word in this passage is "Repent," which means to turn. On paper, name one specific thing you will turn away from this week and one specific thing you will turn toward instead. Afterward, write down what you actually did.

No right answer to check. Look for a specific, doable turn — one concrete thing to leave and one to take up — named as an action, not a feeling. Afterward, record what was actually done as observable evidence. Do not assess the learner's sincerity, faith, or spiritual state; note only the action they name and carry out.
Who was walking beside the sea and called the fishermen to follow Him?
Jesus. "As Jesus was walking beside the Sea of Galilee," He saw the brothers and called them, "Come, follow Me."
Beside what sea was Jesus walking when He called the first disciples?
The Sea of Galilee: "As Jesus was walking beside the Sea of Galilee, He saw two brothers."
Listen for the names of the four men Jesus calls, and notice which two are brothers with each other.
Two pairs of brothers: "Simon called Peter and his brother Andrew," then "James son of Zebedee and his brother John."
What was the name of the father of James and John, who was in the boat with them?
Zebedee. They were "in a boat with their father Zebedee, mending their nets."
Right after Jesus called Simon Peter and Andrew, what did they do?
They left their nets and went with Jesus: "And at once they left their nets and followed Him."

Put these events in the order the passage tells them: Jesus sees James and John mending nets; Simon Peter and Andrew leave their nets; Jesus sees Simon Peter and Andrew casting a net; James and John leave the boat and their father.

1) Jesus sees Simon Peter and Andrew casting a net (v.18); 2) at His call they leave their nets and follow (v.19-20); 3) going on, He sees James and John mending nets in the boat (v.21); 4) they leave the boat and their father and follow (v.22).
In your own words, retell this passage: where Jesus was walking, the two pairs of brothers He saw and what each pair was doing, what He said to them, and how they responded.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus walks beside the Sea of Galilee (v.18); He sees Simon Peter and Andrew casting a net, for they were fishermen (v.18); He says, "Come, follow Me," and "I will make you fishers of men" (v.19); at once they leave their nets and follow (v.20); going on, He sees James and John in a boat with their father Zebedee, mending their nets, and calls them (v.21); immediately they leave the boat and their father and follow Him (v.22).
Jesus tells the fishermen, "I will make you fishers of men." These men caught fish for a living. What do you think "fishers of men" means Jesus will now have them do?

Jesus takes the image of their old trade and lifts it to a new work: instead of catching fish, they will gather people to Him. "Fishers of men" describes bringing others to follow Christ, as they themselves have just done. Draw the sense from the passage's picture, not from ideas outside it.
Jesus' call is "Come, follow Me." In this passage, what does "follow Me" turn out to mean, judging by what the men actually do in verses 20 and 22?

"Follow Me" here means far more than agreeing with Jesus: the men leave their nets, their boat, and their father to go with Him. The passage shows following as leaving one's own work to be with Christ and go where He goes. Let the men's actions define the word.
“Come, follow Me,” Jesus said, “and I will make you fishers of men.” (Matthew 4:19)
Another Gospel tells this same calling of the first disciples beside the Sea of Galilee. Which Gospel records the parallel account of Jesus calling these fishermen?

Mark's Gospel: Mark 1:17-18 gives the parallel call, where Jesus likewise says, "Come, follow Me," and "I will make you fishers of men," and they leave their nets. Scripture interprets Scripture: the parallel account confirms and fills out the same event.

Later in Matthew, Jesus explains the cost of following Him: a disciple must deny himself and take up his cross. In which verse of Matthew does Jesus give that call to deny self, take up the cross, and follow Him?

Matthew 16:24, where Jesus says whoever would come after Him must deny himself, take up his cross, and follow Him. It deepens what following costs the fishermen here — leaving nets, boat, and father is the beginning of that self-denial.
These four fishermen are later listed by name among the twelve apostles Jesus sends out. In which verse of Matthew does that list of the twelve apostles begin?

Matthew 10:2, where the roll of the twelve apostles begins, naming Simon called Peter, Andrew, James son of Zebedee, and John among them. The men called at the sea reappear there as commissioned apostles.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about the fishermen, about why they went with Jesus so quickly, or about what happened next?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder whether the fishermen had met Jesus before, how they knew to trust Him, what their father Zebedee thought, or where they went. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not tell us.
The passage says the first pair left their nets "at once," and the second pair left "immediately." Why do you think the writer stresses how quickly they responded to Jesus' call?

Anchor first in the words on the page: "at once" (v.20) and "immediately" (v.22). The repeated speed underlines the authority of Jesus' word — He calls and they follow without delay. Let learners notice how the text presents His call as compelling; keep the discussion on what the passage shows, not on speculation about the men's inner feelings.
Both pairs of brothers follow the same pattern: Jesus sees them at their work, He calls them, and they leave and follow. What does the passage emphasize by telling the story twice in the same shape?

Stay with what the text does: call, leaving, following — repeated for Peter and Andrew (v.18-20) and for James and John (v.21-22). Telling it twice in one shape presses home that Jesus' call effectively draws men from their work to Himself; it is not a one-off but a pattern of His summoning and their obedient response. Keep the focus on the pattern the passage draws, not on doctrine it does not state.
The fishermen left what they were doing to follow Jesus. What is one concrete thing you could do this week to follow Jesus — a specific action you will actually take?

Look for a specific, doable action rather than a feeling: a set time to read a passage, obeying Jesus in a task they usually avoid, setting aside something that pulls them away, or helping a family member. Help each person name one observable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion or faith.
On paper, draw Jesus beside the sea, the two fishermen with their net, and the two brothers in the boat with their father. A grown-up can help you label who is who.

The drawing should show Jesus by the Sea of Galilee, Simon Peter and Andrew with a net, and James and John in a boat with their father Zebedee (v.18, 21). Those are the only people the passage names; no others belong in the picture.

On an index card, write or draw what the fishermen left behind to follow Jesus (their nets, their boat, their father). On the other side, write or draw one thing you will do this week to follow Jesus.

In the passage the men leave their nets, their boat, and their father (v.20, 22); confirm the child names what was left. For the personal side, look for a concrete, observable action they can carry out this week. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.
Through which land did Jesus go, teaching, preaching, and healing?
Galilee. "Jesus went throughout Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness among the people."
The passage names five places the large crowds came from. Name as many of them as you can.

"The large crowds that followed Him came from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan." (Syria is named separately as where His fame spread, not as a place the crowds came from.)
Listen for every place-name in this passage, and notice which land His fame spread through and which places the crowds travelled from.

His fame spread through Syria: "News about Him spread all over Syria." The crowds came "from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan." Galilee is also where He went about teaching and healing (v.23).
What three things does the passage say Jesus was doing as He went throughout Galilee?
"Teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness among the people." Three works: teaching, preaching, and healing.

Put these in the order the passage tells them: large crowds follow Jesus from many regions; Jesus teaches, preaches, and heals throughout Galilee; people bring Him all who were ill and He heals them; news about Him spreads all over Syria.

1) Jesus teaches, preaches, and heals throughout Galilee (v.23); 2) "News about Him spread all over Syria" (v.24); 3) people bring Him all who were ill and He heals them (v.24); 4) large crowds follow Him from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan (v.25).
In your own words, retell this passage: what Jesus was doing throughout Galilee, how word about Him spread, the kinds of sick people who were brought to Him, and where the large crowds came from.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus went throughout Galilee, teaching in synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness (v.23); news about Him spread all over Syria (v.24); people brought Him all who were ill — those with various diseases, acute pain, the demon-possessed, those having seizures, and the paralyzed — and He healed them (v.24); large crowds followed Him from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan (v.25).
The passage says Jesus was "preaching the gospel of the kingdom." The word gospel means good news. From this passage, what is the good news Jesus proclaims good news about?

It is the glad tidings of the kingdom — the good news that God's kingdom has come near, of which Christ Himself is the Preacher. In the passage this preaching goes together with His healing every disease, so the message is that the King has arrived and His reign brings blessing. Draw the sense from the passage's own phrase, not from outside ideas.
The passage says Jesus was "teaching in their synagogues." What were the synagogues, and why would they be a fitting place for Jesus to teach?

Synagogues were the local places of religious gathering where the Old Testament Scriptures were read — the natural ground for introducing the gospel of the kingdom to people already assembled around God's word. The passage pairs "teaching in their synagogues" with "preaching the gospel of the kingdom," so Jesus meets people where they gathered to hear Scripture. Keep the sense to what the term and passage supply.
Jesus went throughout Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness among the people. (Matthew 4:23)

Later in Matthew this same summary of Jesus teaching, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing appears again to frame His ministry. In which verse of Matthew does that matching summary reappear?

Matthew 9:35, which again says Jesus went through the towns "teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness." Scripture interprets Scripture: the repeated summary brackets His Galilean ministry from both sides.
Another Gospel gives a parallel summary of Jesus preaching in the synagogues throughout Galilee. Which Gospel records that parallel account?
Mark's Gospel: Mark 1:39 says He went throughout Galilee, "preaching in their synagogues and driving out demons." The parallel confirms and fills out the same season of ministry summarized here.

In Acts, Peter sums up Jesus' ministry as going about doing good and healing all who were oppressed. In which verse of Acts does Peter give that summary of Jesus healing all who were under the devil's power?

Acts 10:38, where Peter says God anointed Jesus, who "went around doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil." It looks back on the very ministry of teaching and healing this passage describes, confirmed by the apostles' later witness.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about the sick people who were brought to Jesus, about the crowds, or about where they all came from?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder how far the people travelled, what it was like to be healed, why such crowds came, or what Jesus taught them. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not tell us.
The passage says Jesus was both preaching the gospel of the kingdom and healing every disease. Why do you think the passage joins His preaching and His healing so closely together?

Anchor first in the words on the page: teaching and preaching go side by side with "healing every disease and sickness among the people" (v.23), and "and He healed them" (v.24). The healing accompanies and confirms the preaching — the works back up the message that the King has come. Keep the discussion on what the passage shows about word and work together; do not press it into doctrine the passage does not state.
The passage widens from Galilee, to all Syria, to crowds from five regions. What does the passage emphasize by tracing this ever-widening reach of Jesus' ministry?

Stay with what the text does: it moves from Jesus in Galilee (v.23), to news spreading "all over Syria" (v.24), to "large crowds" coming "from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan" (v.25). The repeated "all" and "every" and the growing map press home the total reach of His ministry — no disease and no region is beyond it. Keep the focus on the breadth the passage draws, not on speculation about the crowds' motives.
In the passage, people brought to Jesus those who were sick and suffering. Who is one person you could bring to Jesus in prayer this week, and when will you pray for them?

Look for a specific, doable action rather than a feeling: naming one real person and a set time to pray for them, or writing their name on a card as a reminder. In the passage people carry the sick and suffering to Jesus (v.24); the response mirrors that by bringing a real need to Him. Help each person name one observable step; affirm the plan without measuring their devotion or faith.
On paper, draw Jesus in the middle and draw crowds of people coming toward Him from many directions. A grown-up can help you write some of the place-names the people came from.

The drawing should show Jesus with crowds coming to Him from many places; the passage names them coming "from Galilee, the Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judea, and beyond the Jordan" (v.25), with His fame spreading through Syria (v.24). Those are the only places the passage names.
On an index card, write or draw the kinds of hurting people who were brought to Jesus in the passage. On the other side, write the name of one person you will bring to Jesus in prayer this week.

In the passage people bring "all who were ill with various diseases, those suffering acute pain, the demon-possessed, those having seizures, and the paralyzed" (v.24); confirm the child names some of these. For the personal side, look for one real name and, if useful, when they will pray. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.
Jesus names two groups of religious teachers whose righteousness must be surpassed. Who are they?
The scribes and Pharisees. Jesus says a person's "righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees" or he "will never enter the kingdom of heaven."
Listen for the two groups of people Jesus names at the end, whose righteousness He says yours must exceed.
The scribes and Pharisees (v.20). They are the only people-group Jesus names in this passage.
Jesus says He did NOT come to abolish the Law and the Prophets. What does He say He came to do instead?
He came to fulfill them: "I have not come to abolish them, but to fulfill them." To abolish would be to tear down or do away with; instead He fills the Law full and completes it.
Jesus sets "fulfill" against "abolish" in verse 17. Reading verses 17-19 together, what does "fulfill" mean here — and why is it the opposite of abolishing the Law?

"Abolish" would tear the Law down and cancel its claim; "fulfill" does the reverse — Jesus completes and confirms it, filling it full rather than emptying it. The following verses bear this out: not the smallest letter will pass "until everything is accomplished" (v.18), and He honors even "the least of these commandments" (v.19). Draw the sense from these verses, not from outside definitions.

Jesus says that "not a single jot, not a stroke of a pen, will disappear from the Law." A jot and a pen-stroke are the tiniest marks in writing. What is Jesus teaching about the Law by pointing to its smallest marks?

By naming the smallest possible marks of writing, Jesus teaches that the Law endures down to its least detail — nothing in it will fail or fall away "until everything is accomplished" (v.18). The point is the total permanence of God's written word, not merely its broad themes.
In verse 19 Jesus uses the words "least" and "great" for people in the kingdom of heaven. According to the verse, what makes someone "least" and what makes someone "great"?

Whoever "breaks one of the least of these commandments and teaches others to do likewise will be called least in the kingdom of heaven"; but whoever "practices and teaches them will be called great in the kingdom of heaven." How one treats even the smallest commandment — keeping and teaching it, or loosening and teaching against it — is what the verse ties to being least or great.
Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets. I have not come to abolish them, but to fulfill them. (Matthew 5:17)
For I tell you that unless your righteousness exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven. (Matthew 5:20)

Jesus says He came not to abolish the Law but to fulfill it. Paul makes a matching point when he writes that Christ is the goal the Law was aiming at. In which verse does Paul say, "Christ is the end of the law"?

Romans 10:4: "For Christ is the end of the law, to bring righteousness to everyone who believes." Scripture interprets Scripture — Paul confirms that Christ completes rather than cancels the Law, matching "but to fulfill them" (Matthew 5:17).

Verse 18 says not a single stroke of a pen will disappear from the Law until everything is accomplished. Luke's Gospel records Jesus saying nearly the same thing. In which verse of Luke does Jesus say it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one stroke of a pen to drop out of the Law?
Luke 16:17: "But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for a single stroke of a pen to drop out of the Law." The parallel confirms the permanence Jesus teaches here in Matthew 5:18.

Jesus grounds the Law's permanence in verse 18 — not a jot will pass until heaven and earth do. Isaiah proclaims the same enduring quality of God's word. In which verse of Isaiah does he say, "the word of our God stands forever"?

Isaiah 40:8: "The grass withers and the flowers fall, but the word of our God stands forever." It undergirds Matthew 5:18 — because God's word endures, not the smallest mark of the Law will fail. (1 Peter 1:25 quotes the same, "the word of the Lord stands forever.")
After hearing Jesus talk about the Law and the Prophets, what is one question you would like to ask about what He said?

Fully open — there is no target answer. A learner may wonder what the Law and the Prophets are, what it means to fulfill them, who the scribes and Pharisees were, or what the smallest commandment is. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not spell out.

Verse 19 says whoever breaks even the least commandment and teaches others to do so will be called least in the kingdom. Starting from what the verse says, why would teaching others to break a command matter, and not just breaking it oneself?

Anchor first in the words: the verse pairs "breaks one of the least of these commandments" with "and teaches others to do likewise" (v.19). The teaching spreads the breaking to others, so it is weightier than a private lapse. Conversely the great are those who both "practices and teaches them." Keep the discussion on what the verse joins together — doing and teaching — rather than on speculation beyond it.

Trace the flow of the passage: Jesus does not abolish the Law but fulfills it (v.17); it is permanent to its smallest mark (v.18); so how one treats its commandments matters (v.19). How does the demand of verse 20 — a righteousness exceeding the scribes and Pharisees — follow from what comes before it?

Follow the four steps on the page: not-abolish but fulfill (v.17); therefore the Law stands entire (v.18); therefore keeping and teaching it marks the least and the great (v.19); therefore ("For I tell you") entry demands a righteousness that "exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees" (v.20). If the Law stands in full, a merely outward keeping like theirs is not enough — the required righteousness must surpass it. Keep learners tracing the text's own logic; let the passage set the standard, not outside speculation about the Pharisees.

Verse 20 requires a righteousness that "exceeds that of the scribes and Pharisees." Reading it alongside verse 19's call to both practice and teach the commandments, what does the passage suggest this greater righteousness looks like?

Anchor in the text: the righteousness required must "exceed" theirs (v.20), and verse 19 has just praised the one who both "practices and teaches" the commandments, down to the least. The passage points to a righteousness that keeps the whole Law truly, not a thinner or merely outward version of it. Keep the focus on what these verses state; do not import a full doctrine of justification the passage does not here spell out.

In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage: what He came to do with the Law and the Prophets, how lasting the Law is, how the way we treat its commandments matters, and the kind of righteousness needed to enter the kingdom.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus came not to abolish but to fulfill the Law and the Prophets (v.17); the Law is permanent — not a jot or pen-stroke will disappear until everything is accomplished (v.18); whoever breaks and teaches against even the least commandment is called least, whoever practices and teaches it is called great (v.19); and entering the kingdom requires a righteousness exceeding that of the scribes and Pharisees (v.20). Affirm a faithful retelling of the flow; do not grade wording.

Jesus honors the one who both practices and teaches even the least of God's commandments. Name one commandment of God you already know, and one concrete thing you will do this week both to keep it yourself and to help someone in your family keep it.

The passage praises the one who "practices and teaches" the commandments (v.19), so look for both parts: a specific action to obey and a specific way to pass it on (reminding a sibling, explaining it to a younger child, doing it together). Help each person name one observable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; record the action, never a verdict on their character or faith.
On an index card, write or draw one of God's commandments that is easy to treat as small or unimportant. On the other side, write or draw one thing you will do this week to obey it carefully.

This works from verse 19, where Jesus warns against loosening even "the least of these commandments." Any real commandment of God the child names is fine; the aim is a concrete, doable action to keep it this week. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.
Draw two pictures on paper: one of a rule being torn up, and one of a job being finished and made complete. A grown-up can help you mark which picture shows what Jesus came to do to God's Law.

Jesus said He did not come to abolish (tear down) the Law but to fulfill (complete) it (v.17). The finished, completed picture is the one that shows what Jesus came to do; the torn-up picture is what He said He did NOT come to do. Help the child say the two words simply — not tearing down, but finishing. Affirm the drawing; do not assess the child.
In Jesus' example, where is the person standing when he remembers that his brother has something against him?
At the altar, offering a gift: "if you are offering your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you."
In the courtroom warning of verses 25-26, name the people the passage says you could be handed over to, and the place you could end up.

The adversary hands you to the judge, the judge to the officer, and you may be thrown into prison: "he may hand you over to the judge, and the judge may hand you over to the officer, and you may be thrown into prison."
Listen for the three consequences Jesus names in verse 22 for anger and contemptuous words, and to which of the three the harshest is attached.

Being angry brings one "subject to judgment"; saying "Raca" brings one "subject to the Sanhedrin"; saying "You fool!" brings one "subject to the fire of hell" — the harshest attached to the reviling word.
When you are at the altar and remember your brother has something against you, what does Jesus say to do first, and what to do only after that?
First leave the gift and go be reconciled, then come back and offer it: "leave your gift there before the altar. First go and be reconciled to your brother; then come and offer your gift."

On paper, write these five steps from Jesus' courtroom warning in the order the passage gives them: thrown into prison; handed to the officer; still on the way to court; handed to the judge; pay the last penny. (Pre-reader variant: draw arrows from a road, to a judge, to a jail.)

Order: (1) still on the way to court, (2) handed to the judge, (3) handed to the officer, (4) thrown into prison, (5) pay the last penny — "while you are still on the way to court. Otherwise, he may hand you over to the judge, and the judge may hand you over to the officer, and you may be thrown into prison." The final step: "you will not get out until you have paid the last penny."

In your own words, retell this passage: what the ancients had heard about murder, what Jesus says also brings judgment, what to do about the gift at the altar when a brother has something against you, and the warning about the adversary on the way to court.

Checklist for a complete retelling: the ancients heard "Do not murder" and that a murderer is subject to judgment (v.21); Jesus says anger, saying "Raca," and saying "You fool!" also bring judgment, the Sanhedrin, and the fire of hell (v.22); if at the altar you remember a brother has something against you, leave the gift, first go be reconciled, then offer it (vv.23-24); reconcile quickly with your adversary on the way to court, or be handed to judge, officer, and prison until the last penny is paid (vv.25-26).
Jesus says to "be reconciled to your brother." In this passage, what does it mean to be reconciled with someone?

To make peace and set things right with the brother — to go and mend the broken relationship so there is no longer "something against you." In the passage it is the action that must come before the gift is offered.
Jesus warns against saying "Raca" and saying "You fool!" to a brother. What kind of speech are these two words — what is a person doing to his brother when he says them?

Both are contemptuous, reviling words spoken against a brother. "Raca" is a scornful term of disdain, treating a brother as empty and worthless; "You fool!" is a spiteful term of hatred, condemning him as vile. Jesus places such words under the same judgment as murder because they spring from the same angry, contemptuous heart.
Jesus says "anyone who is angry with his brother will be subject to judgment." What does the passage show about how far the command "Do not murder" actually reaches — to the hand only, or further?

It reaches further than the killing act. By setting "Do not murder" beside "anyone who is angry with his brother will be subject to judgment," Jesus exposes the true spiritual extent of the sixth commandment: it condemns the sinful anger and contemptuous words of the heart and tongue, not merely the murdering hand. Such anger is, in God's account, the beginning of murder.
“leave your gift there before the altar. First go and be reconciled to your brother; then come and offer your gift.” (Matthew 5:24)
“Reconcile quickly with your adversary, while you are still on the way to court.” (Matthew 5:25)

Jesus here ties anger against a brother to the judgment on murder. One of John's letters states plainly that hating a brother is the same as murder. In which verse does 1 John say that everyone who hates his brother is a murderer?

1 John 3:15: "Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer, and you know that eternal life does not reside in a murderer." Scripture interprets Scripture: John states plainly what Jesus teaches here — the anger and hatred of the heart is murder in God's sight.

This passage warns against sinful anger. In Ephesians, Paul commands the same urgency in dealing with anger before it festers. In which verse of Ephesians does Paul say, "Be angry, yet do not sin," and warn against letting the sun set on your anger?

Ephesians 4:26: "Be angry, yet do not sin. Do not let the sun set upon your anger." Scripture interprets Scripture: Paul, like Jesus here, treats anger as urgent — to be settled quickly rather than nursed, just as Jesus urges reconciliation "quickly" and before offering the gift.

Jesus says here that reconciliation with a brother should even interrupt worship at the altar. In Mark's Gospel, He says something similar about forgiving when you pray. In which verse of Mark does Jesus say to forgive whatever you hold against another when you stand to pray?

Mark 11:25: "And when you stand to pray, if you hold anything against another, forgive it, so that your Father in heaven will forgive your trespasses as well." Scripture interprets Scripture: as the altar-gift must wait for reconciliation here, so prayer there is joined to forgiving a brother.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about being angry, about leaving the gift at the altar, or about being reconciled with a brother?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder why anger is put next to murder, why the gift must wait, who the brother is, or what "Raca" means. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not spell out.
Jesus says to leave your gift at the altar and go be reconciled first. What question does that raise for you about the connection between how we treat other people and how we come to worship God?

Open — invite the learner's own wondering, not a settled answer. Some may ask why God would have worship wait, whether reconciliation is always possible, or what "something against you" includes. Draw out the question itself; you need not resolve it here. Guard only against turning it into a judgment of anyone present.

The passage moves from "You have heard that it was said to the ancients" to "But I tell you." What does Jesus add to the old command against murder, and why does that make the command harder, not easier, to keep?

The text states the contrast: the ancients heard "Do not murder," but Jesus says even anger and contemptuous words against a brother bring judgment (vv.21-22). He is not loosening the command but showing its full spiritual reach — it binds the heart and tongue, not only the hand. That makes it harder, because no one can claim to keep it merely by never killing; the angry, scornful heart already stands condemned. Let learners anchor the answer in the "but I tell you" wording before drawing the why.

The passage says to leave the gift at the altar, first be reconciled, then return and offer it. What does the order Jesus commands — reconciliation before offering — show about how He weighs a broken relationship against an act of worship?

The text itself sets the order: "First go and be reconciled to your brother; then come and offer your gift" (v.24). Jesus makes reconciliation so urgent that it interrupts worship — the gift waits while the wrong is set right. The why, drawn from the passage: God is not honored by an offering brought over an unrepaired breach with a brother; a heart at odds with a brother is not yet fit to approach the altar. Keep this to what the text says; do not extend it into judging anyone's standing before God.

On an index card, write the name of one person you have something unsettled with, and one concrete step you could take this week to "go and be reconciled" — a word to say, a wrong to admit, a visit to make. Keep the card to yourself; it is between you and that person. (Pre-reader variant: draw yourself saying "I'm sorry" to someone.)

Aim for a concrete, doable step, not a feeling. The passage's model is action: leave the gift, go, be reconciled. Note only what the person chooses to do — the observable step — never whether their heart is right or how sincere they seem. Some may have no one to name; that is a fine and honest answer, not a shortfall.
Jesus says to "reconcile quickly." Think of one person you are upset with right now. What is one kind thing you could do or say to them today to make peace?

Look for a specific, doable action a child can actually take today — say sorry, share a toy, offer to help. The passage stresses doing it "quickly." Affirm the concrete step named; record only the action, never a verdict on the child's attitude or heart.

In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage from beginning to end: the old command He names, the deeper thing He then says about the heart, and what He says about the right eye and the right hand.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus names the command they had heard, "Do not commit adultery" (v.27); He then says that anyone who "looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart" (v.28) — the command reaches the heart, not only the outward act; He then presses how far one should go to resist sin: if the right eye "causes you to sin, gouge it out and throw it away" (v.29) and if the right hand causes sin, "cut it off and throw it away" (v.30), for it is better to lose one part than for the whole body to be thrown into hell.
Jesus speaks of a man who "looks at a woman to lust after her." In this passage, what does "to lust after her" mean — is it the same as simply seeing her?

No — it is more than seeing. "To lust after her" is the looking that gives itself over to desire, the settled wanting of the will, not an involuntary glance. The passage marks this out because such looking "has already committed adultery with her in his heart" (v.28); it is the desire, not merely the sight, that Jesus names as sin.
Jesus says a person can commit this sin "in his heart." According to the passage, where does Jesus say this sin can happen?

In the heart. The passage says the one who looks to lust "has already committed adultery with her in his heart" (v.28). Jesus teaches that this sin can begin inside a person, in what they want and think, not only in what they do with their body.
Twice Jesus uses the phrase "causes you to sin" — of the right eye and the right hand. What is that phrase pointing to: the body part itself, or its use?

It points to the occasion of sin — the eye or hand as it is used, not the innocent organ in itself. Jesus says, "If your right eye causes you to sin" and "if your right hand causes you to sin" (v.29-30); the phrase names whatever leads a person into the sin He has just described. That is why the remedy is aimed at cutting off the occasion, not literally at the flesh (see the D7 items). Carry the text's plain sense; do not press it further than the passage does.
Jesus twice weighs losing a part of the body against "your whole body" being "thrown into hell." What weight does the word "hell" carry in His argument here?

It carries the weight of final judgment — the most serious loss there is. Jesus sets the temporary loss of "one part of your body" against the whole body being "thrown into hell" (v.29-30). "Hell" is what makes the drastic remedy reasonable: because the stake is so grave, it is "better" to lose the lesser thing than to keep it and perish. Keep to the text's own words; do not add detail the passage does not state.
Listen for the word Jesus uses for the place inside a person where He says this sin can happen.
Heart. The passage says the sin can be "committed adultery with her in his heart" (v.28) — Jesus teaches that the command reaches inside a person, to the heart.
Draw a big heart on your paper. Inside it, draw one thing that only God can see. In this passage Jesus teaches that God cares about what is inside our hearts, not only what our hands do.

There is no wrong way to draw it. The passage says the sin happens "in his heart" (v.28), so Jesus teaches that God sees what is inside us. Use the drawing to check the child can say that God cares about the heart, the inside part, not only the outside.
“You have heard that it was said, ‘Do not commit adultery.’ But I tell you that anyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart.” (Matthew 5:27-28)
“If your right eye causes you to sin, gouge it out and throw it away. It is better for you to lose one part of your body than for your whole body to be thrown into hell.” (Matthew 5:29)
Jesus begins by quoting a command they had already heard: "Do not commit adultery." Where in the Old Testament was that command first given as one of the Ten Commandments?

Exodus 20:14 — the seventh of the Ten Commandments, "Do not commit adultery." Jesus is not setting the command aside but reaching deeper into it, from the outward act to the heart — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). (Name the connection; the child need not quote Exodus.)

Jesus warns against looking at a woman "to lust after her." Proverbs already gave a father's warning against this very thing — desiring a woman's beauty in the heart. Which passage in Proverbs says that?

Proverbs 6:25, which warns, "Do not lust in your heart for her beauty." It names the same inward desire Jesus points to in Matthew 5:28 — the heart's lust, not only the outward deed — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). (Name the connection; the learner need not quote Proverbs.)

Jesus' saying about cutting off the hand rather than being thrown into hell appears again elsewhere in the Gospels, where He repeats it about hand, foot, and eye. In which passage in Mark does that parallel saying occur?

Mark 9:43-48, where Jesus says the same of the hand, the foot, and the eye — better to enter life maimed than to be thrown into hell. The repeated saying confirms the sense here: the point is drastic mortification of whatever leads to sin, not literal self-harm — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). (Name the connection; the learner need not quote Mark.)

The lusting look Jesus names belongs to a wider pattern the New Testament calls the desire of the eyes. Which passage in 1 John lists the lust of the flesh and the lust of the eyes as being not from the Father but from the world?

1 John 2:16, which names "the desires of the flesh, the desires of the eyes, and the pride of life" as being from the world, not from the Father. It sets the lusting look of Matthew 5:28 within the broader disorder of desire Scripture warns against — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). (Name the connection; the learner need not quote 1 John.)
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what "adultery" means, why Jesus talks about the heart, or why He speaks about an eye or a hand. Answer honestly and simply at the child's level, and receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
Jesus moves the command from the outward act to the heart, and then speaks of the eye and hand in the strongest possible terms. What question does the severity of His language raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why Jesus uses such extreme images, whether He means them literally, how the heart's sin relates to the outward act, or what resisting this sin actually requires. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer. (The D7 items address the figure of eye and hand if it is asked.)
Jesus sets "You have heard that it was said" against "But I tell you." First, what had they heard? Then, what does Jesus add to it, and why does that go further than the outward command?

Anchor first in the text: they had heard "Do not commit adultery" (v.27), understood as the outward act. With "But I tell you" (v.28) Jesus adds that the man who "looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart." It goes further because it reaches the desire itself, not only the deed — the command binds the heart, so a person can break it inwardly before ever acting. Draw the meaning from what the passage states; do not add beyond the text.

Jesus says of the right eye, "gouge it out and throw it away," and of the right hand, "cut it off and throw it away." The passage itself calls these the parts that cause a person to sin. Does the passage require literal self-harm, or is it pressing something else? Show your reasoning from the text.

Anchor first in the text: Jesus qualifies each member by its use — "if your right eye causes you to sin" and "if your right hand causes you to sin" (v.29-30). It is the offending use, the occasion of sin, that must go; the eye and hand stand for whatever leads a person into the lust He has just named. So the command presses radical, decisive self-denial — cutting off the occasion of sin whatever it costs — rather than literal maiming; the parallel in Mark 9:43-48 confirms the sense (Scripture interpreting Scripture, WCF 1.9). Note also that a blind man may still lust, which shows the target is the sinning use, not the organ. Draw the meaning from the text and its Scripture parallels; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus twice says, "It is better for you to lose one part of your body" than the alternative. What is the alternative He names, and why does that comparison make the drastic step "better"?

Anchor first in the text: the alternative is "for your whole body to be thrown into hell" (v.29) — the whole body "to depart into hell" (v.30). The comparison makes losing "one part" the better choice because the loss is small next to that final ruin; the greater danger justifies the greater measure. Jesus is weighing a temporary, partial loss against final judgment, and calling the first the wiser trade. Draw the meaning from what the passage states; do not add beyond the text.

Jesus teaches cutting off whatever "causes you to sin." On paper, name one specific thing (a screen, an app, a habit, a place) that draws you toward this kind of sin, and write one concrete step you will take this week to cut off its access to you. At the end of the week, write down what you actually did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, doable step the learner actually carried out (not a vague resolve), and record the observable action as evidence. Keep the sharing appropriate and private — the point is naming an occasion of sin and removing it, per v.29-30, not confession of the sin itself. Do not judge the learner's heart or purity; note only what was done.

Because Jesus locates this sin in the heart and calls for cutting off its occasions, name one concrete guard you can put in place this week — a change to what you look at, where you go, or what you allow near you. What is the one step, and when will you take it?

No right answer to check. Look for a single, concrete, time-bound step that removes an occasion of sin (per v.29-30), not a general intention. Record the observable action the person commits to and later reports doing. Do not assess the person's faith, character, or purity — note only the stated, doable action.
This passage speaks about people in relation to marriage. Which people or roles does it name?

The passage names a man who divorces his wife, his wife, a divorced woman, and the man who marries a divorced woman. It also has a speaker who says, "But I tell you." Look for the learner to name the husband, the wife, and the divorced woman from the text itself.
This passage is about a husband and his wife. On paper, draw a husband and a wife standing together.
There is no wrong way to draw them. The passage speaks about a man and "his wife" (v.31). Use the drawing to check the child can say the passage is about a husband and a wife who belong together.
In your own words, retell this passage: first the old saying it repeats about divorce, and then what Jesus says in answer to it.

Checklist for a complete retelling: first the passage repeats an old saying, that whoever divorces his wife must give her a certificate of divorce (v.31); then comes the answer, "But I tell you" (v.32), that anyone who divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, brings adultery upon her, and he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery. The order matters: the old permission first, then the correction.
The old saying in this passage speaks of a "certificate of divorce." What kind of thing is a certificate — and what does its being written say about how divorce was meant to happen?

A certificate is a written, legal document — not a word spoken in anger. The saying is, "Whoever divorces his wife must give her a certificate of divorce" (v.31). That it had to be written and handed over made divorce a deliberate, formal act, a check against a man simply sending his wife away in the heat of the moment.
In verse 32 Jesus names one exception with the words "except for sexual immorality." What does naming a single exception tell us about how binding He treats the marriage bond?

By allowing only one exception, "except for sexual immorality," Jesus treats the marriage bond as otherwise unbreakable: apart from that one ground, to divorce "brings adultery upon her," and "he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery" (v.32). The exception is narrow, so the rule it stands against is strong.
Listen for the same serious word that verse 32 uses two times.
"Adultery." Verse 32 uses it twice: a man who wrongly divorces his wife "brings adultery upon her," and "he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery."
“But I tell you that anyone who divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, brings adultery upon her. And he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.” (Matthew 5:32)
The old saying in verse 31 about a certificate of divorce comes from the Law of Moses. Which passage in Deuteronomy first gives this permission?

Deuteronomy 24:1-4, where the man "may write her a certificate of divorce, hand it to her, and send her away from his house." Jesus repeats this concession in verse 31, then answers it in verse 32. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9): Christ, who gave the Law, gives its true intent.
Jesus gives a very similar teaching about divorce in Luke's Gospel. Which verse in Luke records it?

Luke 16:18: "Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery, and he who marries a divorced woman commits adultery." It echoes Matthew 5:32 closely, so the two Gospels bear the same witness to Christ's teaching (WCF 1.9).
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what divorce is, why Jesus speaks so seriously, or what a certificate is. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you'd prefer. It is fine to keep the answer simple and honest and to say some of this is grown-up and hard.
The passage moves from a permission that was allowed to a standard Jesus now presses. What question does that move raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why a permission was ever given, how the exception should be understood, or what the passage means for a marriage under strain. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer. Note that this passage speaks of the bond and its one exception, not of every pastoral case.
The passage first says, "It has also been said," and then, "But I tell you." What is the old saying, what is Jesus' answer to it, and why does putting them side by side matter?

Anchor first in the text: the old saying is that a man who divorces his wife must give her a certificate of divorce (v.31); Jesus' answer is that to divorce her, except for sexual immorality, brings adultery upon her, and marrying a divorced woman commits adultery (v.32). Setting them side by side shows Jesus is not loosening the command but exposing how a permission had been stretched to excuse what God did not intend. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus says a wrongful divorce "brings adultery upon her." Looking only at what the passage states, why would He connect divorce with adultery at all?

Anchor in the text: the passage twice links divorce to adultery (v.32) — wrongly divorcing his wife "brings adultery upon her," and marrying a divorced woman "commits adultery." The connection assumes the marriage bond is still real: because the tie is not truly dissolved by a certificate, a further union treads on it. So Jesus guards the bond by naming what breaking it lightly actually is. Stay with what the verse states; do not press it beyond its words.

This passage shows that Jesus cares about a promise being kept between a husband and wife. On paper, write one promise you can make and keep this week to someone in your family. Then keep it, and write down afterward what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, doable promise to a real person and a note of what was actually done as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not judge the child's faithfulness or heart.

This passage treats a commitment as something to be kept, not lightly broken. This week, pick one commitment you have already made — to a family member, a friend, or a team — and do the specific thing needed to follow through on it. Afterward, write down what the commitment was and what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for a real, already-made commitment and a specific follow-through action, recorded as evidence. Note only the observable action; do not assess the learner's character or measure how faithful they are.
In this passage Jesus names some things people used to swear by. Which places or things does He name?

Jesus names four things people swore by: heaven, "for it is God’s throne" (v.34); the earth, "for it is His footstool"; Jerusalem, "the city of the great King" (v.35); and one's own head (v.36). Look for the child to name heaven, earth, Jerusalem, and the head from the text itself.
This passage talks about heaven above and the earth below. On paper, draw the sky and the ground, and put a person standing on the ground.

There is no wrong way to draw it. The passage calls heaven "God’s throne" (v.34) and the earth "His footstool" (v.35). Use the drawing to check the child can point to heaven and to the earth as two things the passage names.
In your own words, retell this passage in order: first the old command it repeats, then what Jesus tells the disciples not to do, and last the plain rule He gives instead.

Checklist for a complete retelling, in order: (1) the old command repeated, "Do not break your oath, but fulfill your vows to the Lord" (v.33); (2) Jesus' answer, "But I tell you not to swear at all" (v.34), with the run of oaths He forbids — by heaven, earth, Jerusalem, and one's head (vv.34-36); (3) the positive rule that replaces them, to simply let your Yes be Yes and your No, No, since "Anything more comes from the evil one" (v.37). The order matters: old command, then Jesus' correction, then the plain rule.
The key word in this passage is "swear." What does it mean to swear by something, and why would swearing "by heaven" or "by Jerusalem" still involve God?

To swear by something is to call it as a witness or backing to make your word more believable. The passage shows every such oath still reaches God: heaven is "God’s throne" (v.34), the earth "His footstool," and Jerusalem "the city of the great King" (v.35). So swearing "by heaven" or "by Jerusalem" is not a way around God at all — each one is bound up with Him. (The point is rash, evasive swearing in common speech; a solemn, lawful oath is not in view.)
Verse 37 ends, "Anything more comes from the evil one." In the flow of the passage, what is the "anything more" that Jesus warns against?

The "anything more" is everything piled on top of a plain Yes or No — the oaths and swearing Jesus has just forbidden. The rule is to "simply let your ‘Yes’ be ‘Yes,’ and your ‘No,’ ‘No’" (v.37); whatever is added beyond that plain word, to prop it up or evade it, "comes from the evil one." The passage sets simple truthful speech against the need to swear at all.
Listen for the two small words Jesus says our answer should simply be.
"Yes" and "No." Verse 37 says to "simply let your ‘Yes’ be ‘Yes,’ and your ‘No,’ ‘No.’" Jesus wants our plain word to be true, without needing to swear.
“Simply let your ‘Yes’ be ‘Yes,’ and your ‘No,’ ‘No.’ Anything more comes from the evil one.” (Matthew 5:37)
The plain rule of verse 37 — let your Yes be Yes and your No, No — is repeated almost word for word in one of the New Testament letters. Which verse in James says the same thing?

James 5:12: "do not swear, not by heaven or earth or by any other oath. Simply let your ‘Yes’ be yes, and your ‘No,’ no, so that you will not fall under judgment." James restates Jesus' teaching in Matthew 5:37 nearly word for word, so the two bear one witness (Scripture interprets Scripture, WCF 1.9).
Jesus says heaven is God’s throne and the earth is His footstool. Which verse in Isaiah does this teaching draw its words from?

Isaiah 66:1, where the LORD says, "Heaven is My throne, and earth is My footstool." Jesus uses these very words in verses 34-35 to show that swearing by heaven or earth still points to God. The Gospel rests on the prophet's words (Scripture interprets Scripture, WCF 1.9).
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what it means to swear, why you cannot make a hair white or black, or who the evil one is. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you'd prefer. It is fine to keep the answer simple and honest.
Jesus moves from the old command about keeping oaths to a call for plain, truthful speech. What question does that shift raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask whether every oath is forbidden, how this squares with taking a solemn oath in court, or what plain speech asks of them day to day. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer. Note the passage targets rash, evasive swearing in common speech, not the lawful, weighty oath.
The passage first repeats what "was said to the ancients" and then Jesus says, "But I tell you." What is the old command, what does Jesus tell them instead, and why put the two side by side?

Anchor first in the text: the old command is "Do not break your oath, but fulfill your vows to the Lord" (v.33); Jesus' answer is "not to swear at all" (v.34), then simply to let your Yes be Yes and your No, No (v.37). Setting them side by side shows Jesus is not loosening the command but going deeper: the old rule guarded against breaking oaths, while Jesus aims at a heart so truthful that no oath is needed. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus says not even to swear by your own head, "for you cannot make a single hair white or black." Looking only at what the passage states, why does that reason undercut swearing by your head?

Anchor in the text: verse 36 gives the reason — "you cannot make a single hair white or black." To swear by something is to stake it as security for your word, but your own head is not yours to command; you cannot change even the color of one hair. So swearing by it is empty: you pledge what you do not control. The passage exposes the pretense in such oaths. Stay with what the verse states; do not press it beyond its words.
This passage says to let your Yes be Yes and your No be No. Pick one thing you will say Yes to this week and then really do. Write it on paper, do it, and afterward write down what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific Yes the child can actually keep and a note of what was really done as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not judge the child's honesty or heart.

This passage calls for plain, truthful speech instead of swearing to be believed. This week, notice one time you are tempted to over-promise or exaggerate to be believed, and instead give a plain Yes or No and stand by it. Afterward, write down the situation and what you actually said and did.

No right answer to check. Look for one real situation and a specific, plain-spoken response the learner followed through on, recorded as evidence. Note only the observable action; do not assess the learner's character or how truthful they are as a person.
Jesus says that when we love our enemies we may be sons of someone. Whose sons does Jesus say we may be?
Sons of your Father in heaven: loving enemies is done "that you may be sons of your Father in heaven," the one Jesus calls "your heavenly Father."
Listen for the two kinds of people Jesus says already love only their own — the ones He asks whether we are doing anything more than.
Tax collectors and Gentiles. "Do not even tax collectors do the same?" (v.46) and "Do not even Gentiles do the same?" (v.47) — both love only those who love them or greet only their own.
The passage says the Father in heaven gives two good things to everyone, both the evil and the good. What two things does He send?
Sun and rain: "He causes His sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous."

In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage: the two old sayings He quotes ("You have heard that it was said..."), what He tells us to do instead each time, the reason He gives about the Father in heaven, and how He sums it all up at the end.

A complete retelling follows the passage's flow: (1) "Eye for eye and tooth for tooth" — but instead of getting even, turn the other cheek, give the cloak too, go the second mile, and give or lend to whoever asks (vv.38-42); (2) "Love your neighbor" and "Hate your enemy" — but instead, "love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you" (vv.43-44); (3) the reason: "that you may be sons of your Father in heaven," who sends sun and rain on evil and good alike, whereas even tax collectors and Gentiles love their own (vv.45-47); (4) the summary: "Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect" (v.48).

Jesus says "not to resist an evil person," and the examples that follow are being slapped, being sued for your tunic, and being forced to go a mile. Judging by those examples, what kind of "resisting" is Jesus forbidding here?

The examples all involve a personal wrong met without striking back, so the "resisting" Jesus forbids is rendering evil for evil — private revenge or paying back an injury. He is not repealing self-preservation or the magistrate's duty of justice (WCF 19.4, general equity of the judicial law); he forbids the personal vengeance the abused "eye for eye" saying was used to justify. Read the sense from Jesus' own examples of turning the cheek, yielding the cloak, and going the second mile.
Against the saying "Hate your enemy," Jesus gives His command in verse 44. What two things does He tell us to do toward our enemies?

Love them and pray for them: "love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you." The love commanded is not liking their character but seeking their good, shown here in the concrete act of praying for those who persecute.

The passage ends, "Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect." The word "therefore" ties it to the verses just before, about the Father sending sun and rain on evil and good alike. In that light, what does being "perfect" like the Father mean here?

Because of the "therefore," "perfect" means the completeness of love the Father shows — doing good even to the evil and unthankful, loving beyond one's own circle as He gives sun and rain to all (vv.45-47). It is the God-imitating fullness of kind, not a claim to sinless degree; the whole passage defines it as loving enemies as the Father does, not merely those who love us.
“But I tell you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you,” (Matthew 5:44)
“Be perfect, therefore, as your heavenly Father is perfect.” (Matthew 5:48)
Luke's Gospel records Jesus giving this same command to love enemies in His Sermon on the Plain. In which verses of Luke does Jesus say to love your enemies and do good to those who hate you?

Luke 6:27-28, the parallel where Jesus says to love your enemies, do good to those who hate you, bless those who curse you, and pray for those who mistreat you. Scripture interprets Scripture: Luke's fuller wording confirms and expands Matthew's "love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you."
Paul echoes this teaching when he tells the Roman church to bless those who persecute them rather than repay evil for evil. In which verse of Romans does Paul say to bless those who persecute you?

Romans 12:14, where Paul writes to bless those who persecute you — bless and do not curse. It applies the same command as Matthew 5:44, showing the apostolic church living out Jesus' word to bless and pray for persecutors rather than retaliate.

Jesus tells us to pray for those who persecute us, and He Himself did this from the cross, asking the Father to forgive those crucifying Him. In which verse of Luke does Jesus, from the cross, pray for the Father to forgive those putting Him to death?

Luke 23:34, where Jesus prays for those crucifying Him. It shows the Lord living out His own command of Matthew 5:44 to pray for persecutors; Stephen prays likewise for those stoning him in Acts 7:60. The teaching is embodied in Christ before it is asked of us.

After hearing Jesus tell us to love our enemies, what is one question you would like to ask — about turning the other cheek, about praying for someone who is unkind, or about anything in the passage that puzzles you?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child might wonder how you can love someone who hurts you, what it means to turn the other cheek, whether this means never saying stop, or why God gives sun and rain to bad people too. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say the passage does not answer everything.

Twice in this passage Jesus says, "You have heard that it was said," and then answers, "But I tell you." Name the old saying and Jesus' "But I tell you" answer in each case. Why do you think Jesus sets His own word against what they had heard?

Anchor first in the text. First pair: "Eye for eye and tooth for tooth" (v.38) — but Jesus says "not to resist an evil person," turn the cheek, give the cloak, go the second mile (vv.39-42). Second pair: "Love your neighbor" and "Hate your enemy" (v.43) — but "love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you" (v.44). By His "But I tell you," Jesus speaks with His own authority, pressing the law past the flattened, minimal reading to its full inward reach. Keep learners on what the passage states; the antithesis is against the abuse of the sayings, not against Scripture itself.

Jesus says to love enemies "that you may be sons of your Father in heaven," and then points to how the Father "causes His sun to rise on the evil and the good." Starting from those words, why does Jesus ground the command to love enemies in what the Father does?

Stay with the text: the ground is the Father's own impartial kindness — sun and rain on evil and good, righteous and unrighteous alike (v.45). Children resemble their Father; so those who share His love toward the undeserving show themselves His sons. Loving only those who love us is no more than tax collectors and Gentiles do (vv.46-47); Father-like love reaches enemies. The command is not arbitrary but flows from the character of the God whose children we are called to resemble. Keep to what the passage draws, not to speculation beyond it.

Jesus asks, "If you love those who love you, what reward will you get?" and "if you greet only your brothers, what are you doing more than others?" What point is Jesus making by asking these questions about tax collectors and Gentiles?

Anchor in the text: even tax collectors love those who love them, and even Gentiles greet their own (vv.46-47). By the questions, Jesus shows that love limited to our own kind is nothing special — it is the ordinary way of everyone. His disciples are to do "more than others," the Father-like love that reaches enemies. The rhetorical questions expose the smallness of selective love and press toward the wider love just commanded. Keep the discussion on what the passage states.

Jesus says to love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you. Think of one person who is hard for you to love right now. What is one concrete thing you could do this week to show love to that person, and will you pray for them by name?

Look for a specific, doable action rather than a feeling: praying for the person by name each day, a kind word, returning good for an unkindness, sharing something, or refusing to retaliate the next time. Help each person name one observable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion, faith, or heart — only the action they choose.

On an index card, write or draw one of Jesus' pictures from the passage — turning the other cheek, giving your cloak too, or going the second mile. On the other side, write or draw one kind thing you will do this week for someone who has been unkind to you.

The passage side should match one of Jesus' concrete images: turning the other cheek (v.39), letting them have your cloak as well (v.40), or going with him two miles (v.41). For the personal side, look for a concrete, observable kindness the child can actually carry out. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.

On paper, draw the sun and the rain coming down on all kinds of people — some smiling and kind, some frowning and unkind. A grown-up can help you talk about how God is good to everyone, and how you can be kind even to someone who is not kind to you.
