The drawing should show the sun and rain reaching every kind of person, matching the passage's picture of the Father who "causes His sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous" (v.45). Use it to help the child name one kind thing they could do for someone unkind. Keep it to a doable action; do not assess the child's character.
Who does this passage say sees what is done in secret and will reward you?
Your Father in heaven. "And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you."
In this passage, in what two public places do the hypocrites make a show of their giving?
In the synagogues and on the streets. The hypocrites give "in the synagogues and on the streets, to be honored by men."
Listen for the two places where the hypocrites give, so that they will be honored by men.
Two places are named: "the synagogues and on the streets." There the hypocrites give so as "to be honored by men."

In your own words, retell this passage: the warning Jesus gives about doing righteous acts to be seen, what the hypocrites do when they give and the reward they get, what the disciple should do instead, and what the Father does.

Checklist for a complete retelling: (1) beware doing your righteous acts before men to be seen, or you have no reward from your Father in heaven (v.1); (2) the hypocrites sound a trumpet when they give, in the synagogues and streets, to be honored by men, and they already have their full reward (v.2); (3) instead, give so secretly that your left hand does not know what your right hand is doing (v.3); (4) your giving is in secret, and your Father, who sees in secret, will reward you (v.4).
The passage says the show-offs give "as the hypocrites do." Judging by what these people do here, what does "hypocrites" mean in this passage?

In this passage the hypocrites are those who make their giving public "to be honored by men" rather than to please God. They put on a show of righteousness for an audience. Draw the sense from what the passage shows them doing, not from ideas outside it.
Of the hypocrites Jesus says, "they already have their full reward." What does the passage mean that they already have their full reward, and what do they not receive?

They sought to be honored by men, and that honor is all they get: their reward is paid in full, now, in the applause they wanted. Nothing remains for them from God, for the passage warns, "you will have no reward from your Father in heaven." Keep the meaning to what the text states.
Jesus says our giving should be in secret. What does giving in secret mean in this passage?

It means giving so quietly that others do not see it or praise you for it. Jesus pictures it this way: "do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing." The giving is hidden, and only the Father sees it.
“Be careful not to perform your righteous acts before men to be seen by them. If you do, you will have no reward from your Father in heaven.” (Matthew 6:1)
“And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.” (Matthew 6:4)

Just after this, Jesus teaches about prayer and repeats the very same promise about giving in secret. In which verse of Matthew chapter 6 does He say the same thing about praying to your Father who is unseen?

Matthew 6:6, on prayer, closes with the identical words used here of giving: "And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you." Scripture interprets Scripture: the same secret-and-reward pattern governs both giving and prayer.

The hypocrites here give to be honored by men. John's Gospel names the same disordered love in some who would not confess Christ. In which verse of John chapter 12 is that love of human praise stated?
John 12:43: "For they loved praise from men more than praise from God." It exposes the same root the almsgiving hypocrites show — valuing men's honor above God's. Scripture interprets Scripture.

Later Jesus condemns the Pharisees for the same fault as these hypocrites: doing their deeds so that men will see. In which verse of Matthew chapter 23 does He say their deeds are done for men to see?
Matthew 23:5: "All their deeds are done for men to see." It is the same motive judged here in almsgiving — acting for a human audience rather than for God. Scripture interprets Scripture.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about giving to the needy, or about doing it in secret?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder why the hypocrites blew a trumpet, how you can give without anyone knowing, or what reward the Father gives. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not tell us.
What question does this passage raise for you about why God would reward giving that no other person ever sees?

Open — invite the learner's own wondering without supplying the answer. Questions may run toward what secret giving says about who we are trying to please, or how God can reward what is hidden. Draw out their question and let the passage's own words stay in view; do not hurry to resolve it for them.

The passage contrasts two kinds of giving: the hypocrites give "to be honored by men," but you are to give so that your giving "may be in secret." Why do you think Jesus links each way of giving to a different reward?

Anchor first in the words on the page. The hypocrites seek men's honor and get it now: "they already have their full reward." The secret giver looks instead to "your Father, who sees what is done in secret." The reward each receives matches whose approval he was seeking. Keep the discussion on the contrast the text draws, not on speculation about motives it does not state.
Jesus says, "do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing." What point is this vivid picture making about how the disciple should give?

Stay with what the image does. It presses secrecy so far that the giver himself is not to dwell on the deed — the gift is hidden from others and not paraded even in one's own thoughts. The stated aim follows: "so that your giving may be in secret," done for the Father's eyes, not for applause. Keep the focus on the picture the passage draws, not on doctrine it does not state.
Jesus tells the disciple to give in secret. What is one concrete way you could give to someone in need this week without being seen or praised for it?

Look for a specific, doable action rather than a feeling: leaving something for a neighbor without a note, giving anonymously, doing a hidden chore for a family member, sharing from an allowance quietly. Help each person name one observable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion or heart.
On an index card, write or draw one way you can help someone in need this week so that only God sees — not so people will praise you. Keep it secret, then do it.

In the passage the disciple gives in secret, so the Father who sees in secret will reward (v.3-4); confirm the child's idea is genuinely hidden and doable. Look for a concrete, observable action they can carry out this week. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.
Draw a picture of yourself quietly helping someone who needs help, with no one watching but God. A grown-up can help you keep your kind deed a secret.

The passage teaches giving in secret, seen by the Father alone (v.3-4). The drawing should show the child helping someone in need, done quietly and unseen by others. Affirm the picture and, if the child wishes, help them carry out one hidden kind deed. Do not assess the child's heart, only celebrate the doable action.
When you pray in secret, whom does Jesus say you are praying to?
Your Father: Jesus says to “pray to your Father, who is unseen. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.”
Where does Jesus say the hypocrites love to stand and pray?
In public: they love “to pray standing in the synagogues and on the street corners to be seen by men.”
Listen for the private place Jesus tells you to go to when you pray, and what He says to do once you are there.
The inner room: “go into your inner room, shut your door, and pray to your Father, who is unseen.”

On separate slips write these five petitions: God's name be hallowed, God's kingdom come, God's will be done, give us daily bread, forgive us our debts. Without looking, arrange them in the order Jesus gives them in the prayer, then check against verses 9-12.

The order in verses 9-12: (1) “hallowed be Your name,” (2) “Your kingdom come,” (3) “Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven,” (4) “Give us this day our daily bread,” (5) “forgive us our debts.” God's name, kingdom, and will come first, then our own needs.
In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: how Jesus says NOT to pray, then how He says to pray, and how He ends by talking about forgiving others.

The flow: don't pray to be seen by men like the hypocrites (vv. 5-6), and don't babble many words like the pagans, for the Father already knows your needs (vv. 7-8); instead pray this way, honoring God's name, kingdom, and will first, then asking for bread, forgiveness, and deliverance (vv. 9-13); and finally, forgive others their trespasses, for the Father forgives you as you forgive (vv. 14-15).
In “forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors,” what are the “debts” we ask God to forgive?

Our sins. “Debts” pictures sin as something we owe to God's justice; the very next lines pair it with forgiving those who wrong us, and verses 14-15 name the same thing “trespasses.” It is not money owed but sin needing pardon.
The prayer says “hallowed be Your name.” What are we asking for when we ask that God's name be hallowed?
That God's name be treated as holy and honored above all. To hallow means to set apart as holy; the petition asks that everything by which God makes Himself known be held in reverence.
Jesus warns, “do not babble on like pagans, for they think that by their many words they will be heard.” What fault in praying is He naming, and what does the next verse say makes it needless?

The fault is heaping up empty, showy words as though the sheer quantity earns a hearing (“by their many words”). It is needless because “your Father knows what you need before you ask Him” (v. 8); the warning is against senseless multiplying of words, not against praying earnestly or more than once.
“Our Father in heaven, hallowed be Your name.” “Your kingdom come, Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven.” (Matthew 6:9-10)
“For if you forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.” (Matthew 6:14)
Jesus teaches this same model prayer again in another Gospel, when a disciple asks Him to teach them to pray. Which passage records that, and what did the disciple ask?
Luke 11:1-4 gives the parallel form of the prayer. There a disciple asks, “Lord, teach us to pray,” and Jesus answers with the shorter version: “Father, hallowed be Your name. Your kingdom come.”
This passage ties our forgiving others to God's forgiving us. Where does Paul give the same command and ground it in Christ? Name the verse.

Ephesians 4:32: “Be kind and tenderhearted to one another, forgiving each other just as in Christ God forgave you.” Paul roots our forgiving others in the forgiveness God has already given us in Christ, the same bond Jesus draws in Matthew 6:14-15.
After hearing Jesus warn against praying to be seen by others, what is one honest question you would want to ask Him about your own praying?

Invite the learner's own wondering; do not supply an answer. Welcome any sincere question raised by the text (for example, how to keep from wanting others to notice, or whether praying aloud with others is wrong). Note the question for later study rather than resolving it on the spot.
What is one thing in this prayer that you do not understand yet and would like to ask about?
Let the child name their own puzzle (for example, what debts are, or who the evil one is). Record the question and affirm the asking; the aim is the child's curiosity, not a tidy answer here.
The prayer asks first that God's name be hallowed, His kingdom come, and His will be done, and only then for bread and forgiveness. Why might Jesus teach us to put God's honor before our own needs?

Anchor first in what the text shows: the petitions in verses 9-13 move from God's name, kingdom, and will (“on earth as it is in heaven”) to our needs for bread, pardon, and deliverance. The order itself trains us to seek God's glory before our own comfort and to bring our needs as children to a Father who already “knows what you need before you ask Him” (v. 8). Draw the reasoning from the passage; avoid speculation beyond it.
Jesus says to “go into your inner room, shut your door” rather than pray on the street corner. Based on verses 5-6, why does the private place matter to Him?

Start from the text: the hypocrites pray in public “to be seen by men” and so “already have their full reward” (v. 5), while the one who prays in secret prays “to your Father, who is unseen” and is rewarded by the Father “who sees what is done in secret” (v. 6). The shut door removes the human audience so the prayer is truly aimed at God, not at onlookers. The point is the aim of the heart, not a rule against ever praying with others.
After the whole prayer, verses 14-15 return to just one of its petitions and explain it further. Which petition, and what does the passage say hangs on it?

It returns to forgiveness (the petition of v. 12). The text states the bond plainly: “if you forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you” (v. 14), but “if you do not forgive men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive yours” (v. 15). Singling it out shows how seriously Jesus binds receiving God's forgiveness to extending forgiveness to others. Keep the discussion to what the passage says; the forgiveness in view is the Father's fatherly dealing with His children, not the ground of justification itself.

On an index card, write one specific place and one time this week when you will go somewhere quiet, shut the door, and pray by yourself, the way Jesus describes in verse 6. Keep the card where you will see it.

Look for a concrete, doable plan (a real place and time), not a promise about feelings. The action mirrors verse 6: “go into your inner room, shut your door, and pray to your Father, who is unseen.” Record only the observable plan; do not assess the child's sincerity or spiritual state.
Draw a picture of the quiet place with a shut door where you can go to pray by yourself this week. Point to your picture and tell a grown-up when you will go there.

The drawing and the child naming a place and time is the observable response, echoing verse 6 (“go into your inner room, shut your door”). Affirm the plan; record what the child chose, not any judgment about their heart.
Verse 12 ties forgiving others to being forgiven. Name one person you can choose to forgive this week, and one concrete thing you will do or say toward them.

Seek a specific, observable step (a word said, a note written, a grudge set down), grounded in verse 12: “forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors.” Record the intended action; do not evaluate the learner's character or the depth of their forgiveness.
This passage names more than one kind of person. Which people or roles does it speak about?

The passage names the "hypocrites" who fast to be seen, the "men" they want to notice them, and "your Father, who is unseen" (vv.16-18). Look for the learner to name the hypocrites, the people (men) whose notice they seek, and the Father, all from the text itself.
Listen for who finally sees the fast, even when no other person does.
The Father. The fast is meant to be hidden from men, "but only to your Father, who is unseen" (v.18), the One "who sees what is done in secret" (v.18).
In your own words, retell this passage: first the wrong way the hypocrites fast, then the way Jesus tells His disciples to fast, and what comes of each.

A complete retelling has three parts in order. First, the hypocrites "disfigure their faces to show men they are fasting," and so "they already have their full reward" (v.16). Then Jesus says to "anoint your head and wash your face" so the fast is hidden (v.17). Last, because it is seen "only to your Father, who is unseen" who "sees what is done in secret," the Father "will reward you" (v.18). The order matters: warning first, then instruction, then promise.
Verse 16 says the hypocrites "disfigure their faces" when they fast. What does that phrase mean here, and why do they do it?

To "disfigure their faces" is to spoil the look of the face on purpose — a downcast, unwashed, gloomy appearance put on so the fasting shows. The text itself gives the reason: they do it "to show men they are fasting" (v.16), that is, to be seen and admired by people rather than by God.
The passage describes the Father with the words "who is unseen" and "who sees what is done in secret." What do these phrases say about the audience Jesus wants for a fast?

They set the whole act before God alone. The fast is to be hidden from men and directed to "your Father, who is unseen," the One "who sees what is done in secret" (v.18). The intended audience is God, not people; and because He truly sees, the hidden fast is not lost — He "will reward you."
Jesus says, "do not be somber like the hypocrites" when you fast. What does the word somber mean here?

Somber means sad-looking and gloomy on the outside. The hypocrites put on a gloomy, unhappy face on purpose so other people would see they were fasting. Jesus says, "do not be somber like the hypocrites" (v.16) — do not make a sad show for people.
Verse 17 says to "wash your face" and get ready like on any ordinary day. On paper, draw a clean, happy face getting ready in the morning.

There is no wrong drawing. The point is that Jesus tells the one who fasts to look ordinary — to "anoint your head and wash your face" (v.17) — not gloomy. Use the drawing to check the child can say we can love God quietly, without making a sad show for others.

“But when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, so that your fasting will not be obvious to men, but only to your Father, who is unseen. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.” (Matthew 6:17-18)
Listen for the two things Jesus tells you to do to your head and to your face when you fast.
"Anoint your head and wash your face" (v.17). Jesus names two actions — anoint the head and wash the face — so the one fasting looks like on any ordinary day, not gloomy.
The same promise — the Father who "sees what is done in secret" and rewards — appears twice more nearby in Matthew 6, once about giving and once about praying. Which two verses are they?

Matthew 6:4 (giving) and Matthew 6:6 (praying). Matthew 6:4 closes with the same line, "your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you," and 6:6 tells us to pray "to your Father, who is unseen." Giving, prayer, and fasting share one pattern: done in secret before God, rewarded by Him. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Long before, God warned a people that He rejects a fast that is only outward show without a humbled heart. In which Old Testament chapter does God ask, "Why have we fasted, and You have not seen?"

Isaiah 58 (see vv.3-5). There the people complain, "Why have we fasted, and You have not seen?" yet God exposes a fast that is mere outward affliction — "a day for a man to deny himself, to bow his head like a reed" — while they oppress their workers. The same lesson stands behind Matthew 6:16-18: the fast God honors is humble and directed to Him, not a show for men. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
In one of Jesus' parables, a Pharisee prays by boasting, "I fast twice a week." Which verse records that boast?

Luke 18:12, in the parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector. The Pharisee prays, "I fast twice a week and pay tithes of all that I acquire," treating his fast as a thing to display and be admired for — exactly the fault Jesus warns against here, a fast aimed at men rather than the Father who sees in secret. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing this passage about fasting in secret, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may ask what fasting is, why someone would hide it, or what it means that the Father is unseen. Receive the child's own question rather than steering to a preferred one. It is fine to answer simply and honestly, including that fasting is a grown-up practice they will grow into.
Jesus draws a sharp line between doing a devotion to be seen by people and doing it before the Father who is unseen. What question does that raise for you about your own hidden and visible acts?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask how to keep a good work hidden without false modesty, where the line falls between setting an example and putting on a display, or how to weigh their own motives. Let the question stand; the aim is honest self-examination, not a tidy answer. Keep to what the text raises — the audience of the act — rather than ranging into every possible motive question.

The passage sets two audiences against each other: the hypocrites fast to be seen by men, and the disciple fasts to be seen "only to your Father, who is unseen." What does the passage say each audience gives back in return?

Anchor first in the text: those who fast for men "already have their full reward" (v.16) — the notice they wanted, and nothing more. The one who fasts for the Father, "who sees what is done in secret," is told the Father "will reward you" (v.18). So the contrast is not only who watches but what each gives back: men give passing praise that is the whole payment; God gives a reward that is still to come. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add what it does not say.
Of the hypocrites Jesus says, "they already have their full reward." Looking only at what the passage states, why would being seen by men be the whole of their reward?

Anchor in the text: the hypocrites "disfigure their faces to show men they are fasting" (v.16); being seen is the whole aim of the act. When men see and admire, the act has done exactly what it was for — so the reward is complete, paid in full: "they already have their full reward." Nothing is left for God to add, because nothing was done toward Him. The disciple's hidden fast, by contrast, still waits on the Father who "will reward you" (v.18). Stay with what the verse states; do not press beyond its words.

This passage shows that we can do good things quietly, just for God to see. This week, do one kind thing in secret for someone in your family without telling anyone you did it. Afterward, write down what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, doable, hidden kindness and a note of what was actually done as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not judge the child's heart or how well hidden their motives were.

Jesus tells His disciples to keep a fast hidden, seen only by the Father. This week, choose one small act of devotion — a set time of prayer, a gift given quietly, or going without something — and do it without letting anyone know. Afterward, write down what you chose and that you did it.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable practice carried out privately and a note recorded as evidence. Note only the observable action; do not assess the learner's sincerity or spiritual state. If a youth is uneasy about fasting itself, a hidden act of prayer or giving fits the passage's point equally well.
Jesus speaks of storing treasure in two different places. What are the two places the passage names?

The two places are earth and heaven. Jesus says, "Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth" (v.19), but instead "store up for yourselves treasures in heaven" (v.20). Look for the learner to name both places from the text itself.
In your own words, retell this passage in order: first what Jesus says not to do, then what He says to do instead, and last the reason He gives.

A full retelling has three parts in order. First, do not store treasure on earth, "where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal" (v.19). Then, instead store treasure in heaven, where "moth and rust do not destroy, and where thieves do not break in and steal" (v.20). Last comes the reason: "For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also" (v.21). The order matters: the warning, then the command, then the reason that explains both.
The passage keeps using the word "treasure." From verse 21 — "where your treasure is, there your heart will be also" — what does a person's treasure mean here?

A treasure is more than possessions kept in a box; it is what a person values most and rests their hope on. Verse 21 shows this by tying the treasure to the heart: "where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." So one's treasure is the thing the heart is set on and follows. Jesus does not forbid having treasure but directs which treasure to choose — earthly and perishable, or heavenly and secure (vv.19-20).
Verse 19 says that on earth "moth and rust destroy" treasures. What does the word destroy mean here?

To destroy means to ruin or wear something away until it is spoiled and gone. A moth eats holes in clothes, and rust eats into metal, so treasures kept on earth do not last. That is why Jesus says on earth "moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal" (v.19).
Listen for the three things that can harm treasure kept on earth but cannot touch treasure kept in heaven.

Moth, rust, and thieves. On earth "moth and rust destroy" and "thieves break in and steal" (v.19); but in heaven "moth and rust do not destroy" and "thieves do not break in and steal" (v.20). The same three are named on both sides, once as a threat and once as no threat at all.
“For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.” (Matthew 6:21)

“Do not store up for yourselves treasures on earth, where moth and rust destroy, and where thieves break in and steal. But store up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where moth and rust do not destroy, and where thieves do not break in and steal. For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also.” (Matthew 6:19-21)
Listen for the last sentence Jesus says about your treasure and your heart, and see if you can say it back.
"For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also" (v.21). This is the closing line of the passage, tying the treasure a person chooses to where the heart will go.
Elsewhere Jesus says almost these exact words about treasure and the heart. In which verse of Luke does He say, "For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also"?

Luke 12:34. There Jesus says the same line word for word: "For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." The two Gospels record the same teaching, showing the heart follows whatever it treasures. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Paul takes up the same aim — treasure above rather than below — when he writes, "Set your minds on things above, not on earthly things." In which passage does he say this?

Colossians 3:1-3, especially v.2: "Set your minds on things above, not on earthly things." Paul urges believers, raised with Christ, to fix their affections above rather than on earthly things — the same direction Jesus gives here in storing treasure in heaven, not on earth (vv.19-20). Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Jesus contrasts perishable earthly treasure with lasting heavenly treasure. Where does Paul draw the same contrast, writing, "For what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal"?

2 Corinthians 4:18: "For what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal." Paul sets the passing, visible world against the lasting, unseen one — the same contrast Jesus makes between treasure on earth that moth, rust, and thieves can reach and treasure in heaven that they cannot (vv.19-20). Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing this passage about treasure on earth and treasure in heaven, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what treasure in heaven is, how someone stores it, or what moth and rust are. Receive the child's own question instead of steering to a preferred one. It is fine to answer simply and honestly.
Jesus says, "where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." What question does that line raise for you about where your own heart is drawn?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask how to tell where their treasure actually is, how to loosen their grip on earthly things, or what storing treasure in heaven looks like in ordinary life. Let the question stand; the aim is honest self-examination, not a tidy answer. Keep to what the text raises — the tie between treasure and heart — rather than ranging off the passage.
The passage pictures earthly treasure with "moth and rust" and with "thieves break in and steal." Read as pictures, what do these show about treasure kept on earth?

Anchor first in the text: on earth "moth and rust destroy" and "thieves break in and steal" (v.19). Read as pictures, moth and rust show that earthly treasure wears out and decays from within, while thieves show it can be taken away from without. Together they say earthly treasure is perishable and unsafe — it cannot be held on to. That is why heaven, where these "do not" reach (v.20), is the safe place to store treasure. Draw the meaning from what the text states; do not add what it does not say.

Verse 21 begins with "For": "For where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." Looking only at what the passage states, why does Jesus give this as the reason behind His command about treasure?

Anchor in the text: the word "For" makes v.21 the reason for the command in vv.19-20. Jesus says the heart follows the treasure — "where your treasure is, there your heart will be also." So the choice of treasure is not a small money matter; it decides where the heart itself goes. Store treasure on earth and the heart is bound to what perishes; store it in heaven and the heart is set on what lasts. The command is urgent because the treasure a person chooses draws the whole inner life after it. Stay with what the verse states; do not press beyond its words.
Jesus says to store treasure in heaven. On paper, draw one kind thing you could do this week that pleases God and lasts — like helping, sharing, or praying for someone.

There is no wrong drawing. The point is that a good deed done for God is a kind of treasure that lasts, unlike toys or coins that wear out. Use the drawing to check the child can name one kind, doable thing they could actually do. Note only what the child draws or says they will do; do not judge the child's heart.
This week, do one thing that stores treasure in heaven rather than on earth — such as giving something away, helping quietly, or spending time in prayer. Afterward, write down what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, doable action and a note of what was actually done as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not assess the child's motives or spiritual state.
On paper, name one earthly treasure your time or money tends to go toward, then write one concrete step you will take this week to store treasure in heaven instead. Afterward, note that you did it.

No right answer to check. Look for one honestly named earthly treasure, one concrete and doable step, and a note recorded afterward as evidence. Record only the observable action the learner names and carries out; do not assess their sincerity, character, or spiritual state.
At the end of this passage Jesus speaks of two masters a person might serve. Who are the two masters He names?
God and money. Jesus says "No one can serve two masters" and then names them: "You cannot serve both God and money" (v.24). The two masters are God and money.
Verse 22 says, "The eye is the lamp of the body." What does calling the eye a "lamp" mean here?

A lamp is what lets light into a place. Jesus calls the eye the body's lamp because what the eye takes in fills the whole person: "If your eyes are good, your whole body will be full of light," but "if your eyes are bad, your whole body will be full of darkness" (vv.22-23). The eye pictures where a person looks and fixes their aim; a single, clear aim lets light into the whole life, while a divided one leaves it dark.
Jesus says, "No one can serve two masters." What does it mean to serve a master?

To serve a master is to belong to him and do what he says — to obey and work for one owner. Jesus says "No one can serve two masters" (v.24): a person cannot fully obey two owners at once, because he will "hate the one and love the other." So he must belong to just one.
The passage ends, "You cannot serve both God and money." What does money stand for here, set as a master against God?

Money here is riches viewed as a rival master — wealth trusted in and served as though it were a god. The verse frames it as one of "two masters" (v.24) a heart may belong to. To serve money is to give it the devotion owed to God, holding to it and loving it. The brief glosses the term (mammon) as what one trusts in, riches treated as an idol master. Keep to the text's own point: God and money are set as masters, and only one can truly be served.

This passage uses two words that are opposites: light and darkness. On paper, draw one side bright and full of light, and the other side dark. Then point to the bright side as the grown-up reads, "full of light."

There is no wrong drawing. The point is the passage's own picture words: a good eye leaves the body "full of light," a bad eye "full of darkness" (vv.22-23). Use the drawing to check the child can name light and darkness as the two opposite words Jesus uses here.
“No one can serve two masters: Either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and money.” (Matthew 6:24)

“The eye is the lamp of the body. If your eyes are good, your whole body will be full of light. But if your eyes are bad, your whole body will be full of darkness. If then the light within you is darkness, how great is that darkness!” (Matthew 6:22-23)
Listen for the one thing Jesus says no one is able to do when it comes to two masters.
"No one can serve two masters" (v.24). No one is able to serve two masters at once; a person must belong to one. Jesus applies it plainly: "You cannot serve both God and money."
Jesus speaks this same either/or — that no one can serve both God and money — again in another Gospel. In which verse of Luke does it appear?

Luke 16:13. There Jesus says almost word for word: "No servant can serve two masters." He again concludes, "You cannot serve both God and money." The repeated saying confirms the single point — undivided allegiance. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
The lamp-and-eye picture of these verses appears again in Luke, where Jesus opens it as the interpretive key. In which verse of Luke does He say, "Your eye is the lamp of your body"?

Luke 11:34 (see vv.34-36). There Jesus says, "Your eye is the lamp of your body," and, as here, a good eye fills the body with light and a bad eye with darkness. Luke's fuller form is the interpretive key to Matthew's briefer saying, reading the eye as the aim of the whole person toward light or darkness. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
This passage warns that money can become a master rivaling God. Which verse in 1 Timothy 6 says the love of money is the root of all kinds of evil?

1 Timothy 6:10: "the love of money is the root of all kinds of evil." Paul adds that by craving it, some "have wandered away from the faith" — the very danger Jesus names when He warns that a person cannot serve both God and money. Money mastered becomes a snare that draws the heart from God. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing this passage about the eye, light and darkness, and serving two masters, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may ask how an eye can be a lamp, what money has to do with a master, or how someone could be full of darkness. Receive the child's own question rather than steering to a preferred one. It is fine to answer simply and honestly.

Jesus moves from an image (the eye as the body's lamp) straight to a flat either/or about serving God and money. What question does that movement raise for you about how the two halves of the passage fit together?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why a saying about the eye leads to one about money, what a single or divided eye has to do with a divided heart, or where in their own lives allegiance is split. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering that stays with the text's own images, not a tidy answer supplied for them.

The passage says a good eye fills the body with light and a bad eye fills it with darkness, then turns at once to serving God or money. Looking at what the passage states, how does the picture of the eye connect to the choice of a master?

Anchor first in the text. Jesus says "If your eyes are good, your whole body will be full of light," and "if your eyes are bad, your whole body will be full of darkness" (vv.22-23); then, "No one can serve two masters" and "You cannot serve both God and money" (v.24). The eye stands for where the whole person is aimed: a single aim floods the life with light, a divided one leaves it dark. That is why the choice of a master follows — an eye fixed on God is the body full of light, while an eye divided between God and money is the darkness of v.23. Draw the meaning from what the text states; do not add what it does not say.
Jesus says, "If then the light within you is darkness, how great is that darkness!" Looking only at what the passage states, why would that darkness be so great?

Anchor in the text. The eye is called "the lamp of the body" (v.22) — the very thing meant to give light. If the lamp itself has failed, "if then the light within you is darkness" (v.23), then what should have lit the whole person is instead the source of its darkness, and nothing is left to correct it — so the darkness is total, "how great is that darkness!" Read against v.24, a heart that thinks it serves God but is in fact bound to money is not half-lit but wholly dark, mistaking its darkness for light. Stay with what the passage states; do not press beyond its words.

This passage says we cannot serve both God and money. This week, choose one thing you own and like, and give it or share it with someone, to practice not letting your things be your master. Afterward, draw or write what you gave.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, doable act of giving or sharing and a note or drawing of what was actually done as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not judge the child's heart or how willingly it was done.

Jesus says no one can serve two masters — God and money. This week, name one place where money or getting things competes for your attention, and take one concrete step that puts serving God first: give a set amount away, or spend time you would have spent earning or shopping on prayer or helping someone. Afterward, write down what you chose and that you did it.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable step and a note recorded as evidence of what was actually done. Note only the observable action; do not assess the learner's motives or spiritual state. If naming a place of competition is hard, a single fixed act of giving or of time spent serving fits the passage's point equally well.
Jesus tells us to look at and consider things God cares for. Which living things from nature does this passage name?

The passage names the birds of the air, the lilies of the field, and the grass of the field. Jesus says "Look at the birds of the air" whom "your heavenly Father feeds" (v.26), "Consider how the lilies of the field grow" (v.28), and speaks of how "God clothes the grass of the field" (v.30). Look for the learner to name the birds, the lilies, and the grass, all from the text.
This passage names one famous king of Israel. Which king is he, and what does Jesus say about him next to the lilies?

Solomon. Jesus says that "not even Solomon in all his glory was adorned like one of these" (v.29) — that is, the wild lilies God clothes are more beautifully arrayed than Solomon at the height of his royal splendor. The point is God's lavish care for small things, not the king himself.
Listen for the birds and the flowers Jesus tells us to look at, and who takes care of them.

category.en: Listen for what to look at

text.en: The birds of the air and the lilies of the field. Jesus says the birds "do not sow or reap or gather into barns" and "yet your heavenly Father feeds them" (v.26), and He tells us to "Consider how the lilies of the field grow" (v.28), which God clothes (v.30). The One who cares for them is the heavenly Father.

In your own words, retell this passage in order: the command Jesus gives, the examples from nature He uses to back it up, the one thing He tells us to seek instead, and His closing word about tomorrow.

A full retelling follows the passage's flow. First the command, repeated: "do not worry about your life" (v.25). Then the examples that support it — the birds, whom "your heavenly Father feeds" (v.26), and the lilies and grass, which God clothes better than Solomon (vv.28-30) — argued from the lesser to the greater: if God feeds and clothes these, He will much more provide for you. Then the positive turn: "seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added unto you" (v.33). Last, the closing word: "do not worry about tomorrow" (v.34). The order matters: command, then reasons, then what to seek instead, then tomorrow.
Jesus says "do not worry" three times in this passage. What does it mean to worry?

To worry is to be afraid and full of anxious care that God will not take care of you — fretting about whether you will have food or clothes. Jesus says "do not worry about your life, what you will eat" (v.25) and "do not worry about tomorrow" (v.34). It is trusting-fear, not planning: Jesus points to the birds God feeds so we will trust the Father instead of being afraid.
The repeated phrase "do not worry" is the heart of this passage. From how Jesus uses it here, what kind of worry is He forbidding — is it the same as making sensible plans?

The worry Jesus forbids is anxious, distrustful care — fretting over food, drink, and clothing as if the Father did not know or would not provide. He grounds it against unbelief: "your heavenly Father knows that you need them" (v.32), and He calls the worrier "O you of little faith" (v.30). It is not the same as sensible planning; the birds are fed by the Father, yet the passage does not condemn work — it condemns the distrustful anxiety that adds nothing ("Who of you by worrying can add a single hour to his life?", v.27) and forgets the Father who provides.
The passage's positive command is to "seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness." Within this passage, what does the word "first" mean here?

"First" means first in place and priority — the supreme pursuit, sought ahead of food, drink, and clothing. The whole passage sets the Gentiles, who "strive after all these things" (v.32), against the disciple, who is to "seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness" (v.33). The needful things are not denied but reordered: seek the kingdom first, "and all these things will be added unto you" (v.33). The kingdom and God's righteousness come first; provision follows.
“But seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added unto you.” (Matthew 6:33)
“Therefore do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will worry about itself. Today has enough trouble of its own.” (Matthew 6:34)
Listen for the one thing Jesus says to seek first, and what He promises will be added.

"Seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added unto you" (v.33). The one thing to seek first is the kingdom of God and His righteousness; what is promised to be added is "all these things" — the food, drink, and clothing Jesus has just been speaking about.
Luke records Jesus giving almost this same teaching, ending with a near-identical call to seek the kingdom. In which chapter of Luke does that parallel appear?

Luke 12 (see v.31). There Jesus likewise tells the disciples not to be anxious about food and clothing and concludes, "seek His kingdom, and these things will be added unto you" — the close parallel to "seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness, and all these things will be added unto you" (Matthew 6:33). Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9); the two accounts confirm one teaching of the Lord.

This passage promises the Father provides for His children who seek Him first. A psalm of David makes the same promise, saying its writer has never seen the righteous forsaken or their children begging bread. Which psalm is that?

Psalm 37 (see v.25). David testifies that he has not seen the righteous forsaken or their descendants begging bread, and the psalm urges, "Trust in the LORD and do good" (v.3). This is the same confidence Jesus teaches: the Father "knows that you need them" (Matthew 6:32) and provides for those who seek Him first. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).

Jesus links the kingdom of God with righteousness rather than with food and drink (v.33). Paul makes the very same link in a verse in Romans, saying the kingdom of God is not eating and drinking but righteousness. Where does Paul say it?

Romans 14:17, where Paul writes that the kingdom of God is not a matter of eating and drinking, but of righteousness, peace, and joy in the Holy Spirit. This echoes Jesus' pairing of "the kingdom of God and His righteousness" over against striving for food and drink (Matthew 6:31-33). Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing Jesus talk about the birds, the flowers, and not worrying, what is one question you would like to ask about this passage?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder how God feeds the birds, why grown-ups worry, or what the kingdom of God is. Receive the child's own question rather than steering to a preferred one. It is fine to answer simply and honestly.

Jesus says worry adds nothing and that the Father already knows what we need, then commands us to seek first the kingdom. What question does this passage raise for you about the things you tend to worry over?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask where the line falls between sinful anxiety and responsible provision, how to actually "seek first the kingdom" amid real needs, or why worry persists even when they believe the Father provides. Let the question stand; the aim is honest reflection, not a tidy answer. Keep to what the text raises — worry, provision, and the kingdom — rather than ranging afield.
Jesus points to the birds that "do not sow or reap or gather into barns" and yet are fed. Starting from what the passage actually says about the birds, what greater point is He making about you?

Anchor first in the text: the birds "do not sow or reap or gather into barns—and yet your heavenly Father feeds them" (v.26). Then the passage draws the greater point in its own words: "Are you not much more valuable than they?" (v.26). The argument runs from lesser to greater — if the Father feeds the birds, who cannot store food, He will surely provide for His children, who are of far greater value. Keep the learner drawing meaning from what the verse states; do not add what it does not say.

Verse 30 reasons that if God so clothes the grass, "will He not much more clothe you, O you of little faith?" Looking only at what the passage states, how does the grass argument expose worry as "little faith"?

Stay with the text's own reasoning. The grass "is here today and tomorrow is thrown into the furnace" (v.30) — short-lived and of little account — yet God clothes it more gloriously than Solomon (v.29). If God so adorns what perishes tomorrow, "will He not much more clothe you" (v.30), who are His children and outlast the grass? So to worry about clothing is to doubt a provision the created world already displays — hence "O you of little faith." Worry here is not weak nerves but weak trust in the Father who plainly provides. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not press beyond its words or turn it into a judgment of the learner's own faith.
The passage turns from repeated warnings against worry to a single positive command in verse 33. What does it tell us to do instead of worrying, and what does it promise will follow?

Anchor in the text: instead of worrying and striving like the Gentiles (vv.31-32), Jesus commands, "seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness" (v.33). The promise attached is "and all these things will be added unto you" (v.33) — the food, drink, and clothing just named. So the cure for worry the passage gives is not to secure the needful things directly but to reorder our seeking: put the kingdom first, and the Father who knows our needs supplies the rest. Let the meaning rest on what the verse states; do not add doctrine the passage does not raise.
This passage says the heavenly Father feeds the birds. On paper, draw a bird eating the food God gives it, and one food God gives your family to eat.

There is no wrong drawing. The point is that the child can say, from the passage, that God feeds the birds and gives our food too (v.26). Use the drawing to check the child can name God as the giver. Record only what the child drew and said; do not assess the child's faith.

Jesus tells us to bring our needs to the Father instead of worrying. On an index card, write or draw one thing you sometimes worry about, then write a short prayer asking the Father, who "knows that you need them," to help. Keep the card to use this week.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete worry named and a short prayer written or drawn, following the passage's move from worry to trusting the Father who "knows that you need them" (v.32). Record only the observable action — that the card was made and used — not any judgment of the child's heart or how worried they are.

Jesus says to "seek first the kingdom of God and His righteousness." This week, when you notice yourself worrying about tomorrow, write the worry on a card, and beside it write one specific thing you will do that day to seek God's kingdom first — a set time to pray, read Scripture, or serve someone. Afterward, note that you did it.

No right answer to check. Look for a specific worry paired with one concrete, doable act of seeking the kingdom first (v.33), and a note that it was done as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not assess the learner's sincerity, faith, or how much they worry. Any of the listed practices — prayer, Scripture, service — fits the passage's point equally.
Jesus keeps speaking about one person whose eye you might want to fix. What does He call that person?

"Your brother." Jesus speaks of "the speck in your brother's eye" (v.3) and of helping "remove the speck from your brother's eye" (v.5). Look for the learner to say the other person is called your brother — the one you would want to correct.
In verse 5 Jesus gives two steps in order. Which must you do first, and which comes after?

First take the beam out of your own eye; then help with your brother's speck. The text states the order plainly: "First take the beam out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother's eye" (v.5). The self-cleansing comes first, the helping comes after.
In your own words, retell this whole passage in order: the command about judging, the picture of the speck and the beam, and the last warning about what is holy.

A full retelling follows the passage's order. First the command and its reason: "Do not judge, or you will be judged," for "with the measure you use, it will be measured to you" (vv.1-2). Then the picture: you notice the speck in your brother's eye but miss the beam in your own, and Jesus calls this hypocrisy — first take the beam out of your own eye, then you can see to help your brother (vv.3-5). Last, a turn to the opposite error: "Do not give dogs what is holy; do not throw your pearls before swine" (v.6). The order moves from judging, to self-examination, to a warning against forcing holy things on those who only trample them.
Jesus talks about a "speck" and a "beam." Which one is tiny and which one is huge?

The speck is tiny; the beam is huge. A speck is a little splinter or bit of dust — a small fault. A beam is a big wooden plank or log — a far greater fault. Jesus makes the picture funny on purpose: the person fusses over the speck in someone else's eye while a whole beam sticks out of his own (vv.3-5).
In verse 5 Jesus says, "You hypocrite!" Reading the whole picture of the speck and the beam, what does He mean by calling this person a hypocrite?

A hypocrite here is someone who acts as the caring eye-doctor for a brother's small fault while ignoring a far greater fault of his own. He pretends to a careful, compassionate concern he has not first applied to himself. The text shows why the name fits: he would "take the speck out of your eye, while there is still a beam in your own eye" (v.4). The remedy is not to stop caring but to deal with his own fault first (v.5).
In verse 6, what do the pictures of "dogs," "swine," and "pearls" stand for, and how does the verse itself tell you these are not to be taken literally?

They are figures, not literal animals or jewels. "What is holy" and "your pearls" stand for holy, precious things — the sacred truth or reproof one might offer. The "dogs" and "swine" stand for those who are openly, obstinately hostile to it. The verse shows the figures by what follows: such ones "trample them under their feet, and then turn and tear you to pieces" — they do not receive the holy thing but despise it and attack the giver. So the point is discrimination: holy things are not to be forced on those who only rend them.
Listen for the two eye words Jesus sets against each other — one small, one large.

"Speck" and "beam." Jesus asks why you look at "the speck in your brother's eye, but fail to notice the beam in your own eye" (v.3). The speck is the small fault, the beam the far larger one — the two words carry the whole lesson.
Jesus talks about a tiny speck and a great big beam. On paper, draw a teeny-tiny dot for the speck, and next to it a great big log for the beam.

There is no wrong drawing. The point is the size difference — a tiny speck next to a huge beam (log). Use the two pictures to help the child say the speck is the little problem in someone else's eye and the beam is the big one Jesus says to take out of your own eye first (vv.3-5).
“Do not judge, or you will be judged.” (Matthew 7:1)
“You hypocrite! First take the beam out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.” (Matthew 7:5)
Listen for the very first short command Jesus gives at the start of this passage.
"Do not judge, or you will be judged" (v.1). The passage opens with this short command. Look for the child to catch that first line about not judging.
Luke records the same command not to judge in his account of Jesus' teaching. Which verse in Luke gives this parallel to "Do not judge, or you will be judged"?

Luke 6:37. There Jesus gives the same prohibition against judging that opens our passage in Matthew 7:1. Luke also carries the matching speck-and-beam picture a few verses later (Luke 6:41). Comparing the two Gospels lets Scripture interpret Scripture (WCF 1.9).

Jesus warns that the standard you use to judge will be turned back on you. Where does Paul make the same point — that whoever judges another condemns himself, because he practices the very same things?

Romans 2:1-2. Paul argues that the one who passes judgment on another condemns himself, for the judge does the same things — the very measure-for-measure principle Jesus states in Matthew 7:1-2. The two passages illumine each other (WCF 1.9).

This passage says to remove the beam from your own eye before helping your brother — self-examination before correction. Where does Paul teach that those who are spiritual should restore a fallen brother gently, keeping watch on themselves?

Galatians 6:1. Paul tells the spiritual to restore a brother caught in sin gently, each watching himself lest he too be tempted. This matches the order Jesus gives here: deal with your own eye first, then help your brother's (Matthew 7:5). Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing about the speck and the beam, what is one question you would like to ask about this passage?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder how a whole beam could be in someone's eye, what judging means, or who the dogs and swine are. Receive the child's own question rather than steering to a preferred one. It is fine to answer simply and honestly.
The passage forbids hypocritical judging (vv.1-5) yet also warns against giving what is holy to those who trample it (v.6). What question does holding these two together raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults often ask how the ban on judging squares with the discernment v.6 clearly requires, or where the line falls between rash censure and needful judgment. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wrestling with the text, not a tidy formula. Keep the wondering anchored to what the passage sets side by side — over-judging and undiscriminating reproof — rather than ranging into unrelated debates.
The passage pictures someone trying to remove a speck from a brother's eye while a beam is in his own. Starting from what the text says, why is that both absurd and useless?

Anchor first in the text. The person offers, "Let me take the speck out of your eye," "while there is still a beam in your own eye" (v.4). It is absurd because the fault he ignores in himself is far greater than the one he fusses over in his brother. It is useless because he cannot even see to do the delicate work — only after he takes the beam out will he "see clearly to remove the speck" (v.5). The point is order, not silence: self-examination makes the helping possible. Draw the meaning from what the text states; do not add motives the passage does not give.

Verses 1-5 warn against judging a brother too harshly; verse 6 warns against giving what is holy to dogs and swine. Reading only what the passage says, how does verse 6 guard against the opposite error?

Anchor in the text. Verses 1-5 correct the sin of over-judging — condemning a brother while blind to your own greater fault. Verse 6 turns to the opposite danger: an undiscriminating openness that hands "what is holy" and "your pearls" to those who will only "trample them under their feet, and then turn and tear you to pieces." So the passage forbids rash, censorious judging without forbidding all discernment — holy things are still not to be forced on the obstinately hostile. The one text holds both: do not judge harshly, yet do not cast the holy before those who despise it. Keep to what the verses state; do not press v.6 into questions of formal church discipline it does not raise here.

Jesus says to take the beam out of your own eye before helping with your brother's speck. Think of one fault you are tempted to point out in someone else. On paper, write first one honest thing you can work on in yourself before you say anything to them. Then do that first step this week.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable self-examination step written down and carried out, in the order the passage teaches — own eye first (v.5). Record only the observable action the learner names and did; do not assess their heart, sincerity, or spiritual state, and do not require them to name the other person.

Jesus says to check your own eye before you fix someone else's. This week, before you tell a brother or sister in your family what they did wrong, first stop and fix one thing about how you were acting. Afterward, draw or write what you fixed about yourself.

No right answer to check. Look for one small, doable step where the child worked on their own behavior first, and a drawing or note of what they did as evidence. Record only the observable action; do not judge the child's heart or how well they did it.
In this passage, who does Jesus say will give good things to those who ask Him?
Your Father in heaven. Jesus says, "how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask Him" (v.11).
In the little picture Jesus tells, who asks his father for bread and for a fish?
A son — the father's own child. Jesus asks, "Which of you, if his son asks for bread, will give him a stone?" (v.9), and then about a fish (v.10).
Jesus gives three commands in a row about prayer. What are the three things He tells people to do, in order?
Ask, then seek, then knock. Jesus says, "Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and the door will be opened to you" (v.7).

This passage pairs each action with a promise. On paper, write the three actions (ask, seek, knock) in one column and the three promises (given, find, opened) in another, then draw a line joining each action to the promise Jesus gives it.
Ask joins to "it will be given to you"; seek joins to "you will find"; knock joins to "the door will be opened to you" (v.7). Verse 8 repeats the same three pairings.
In verse 12, after telling people to do to others as they would have others do to them, what phrase does Jesus use to describe this rule's place in Scripture?
He calls it "the essence of the Law and the Prophets" — that is, the sum of what the Law and the Prophets require (v.12).
Jesus says the Father gives "good things" to those who ask Him. Within this passage, what does the comparison with bread and fish — not a stone or a snake — show about the kind of gifts He means?

The gifts are good and fitting, not harmful. A father gives his son bread, not a stone, and a fish, not a snake (vv.9-10); so the Father in heaven gives "good things," what genuinely helps, to those who ask Him (v.11).
Which word does Jesus say again and again in verses 7 and 8 as He teaches about prayer?
"Ask." Jesus repeats it: "Ask, and it will be given to you" (v.7) and "everyone who asks receives" (v.8).
As the passage is read, listen for the phrase "how much more" and for who Jesus says will give good things to those who ask.
"how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask Him" (v.11). The phrase marks the step from earthly fathers up to the Father in heaven.
Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and the door will be opened to you. (Matthew 7:7)
In everything, then, do to others as you would have them do to you. For this is the essence of the Law and the Prophets. (Matthew 7:12)
Finish verse 7 from memory: "Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and ..."?
"...the door will be opened to you" (v.7).
Luke records this same teaching of Jesus about asking, seeking, and knocking. Which passage in Luke gives the parallel?
Luke 11:9-13, the parallel to this passage. There Jesus makes the same lesser-to-greater argument, naming the good gift the Father gives to those who ask as the Holy Spirit (Luke 11:13).
The rule in verse 12 — do to others as you would have them do to you — is restated by Jesus elsewhere. Which verse in Luke gives the same command?
Luke 6:31, where Jesus gives the same command to do to others as you would have them do to you.
This passage invites us to ask God for good things. After hearing Jesus' promise here, what is one thing you would like to ask God about?

Any sincere, on-passage request is welcome; the aim is that the child voices a real thing to ask God, connecting Jesus' "Ask, and it will be given to you" (v.7) to their own asking. Do not correct or rank the content of the request; note only that the child formed and shared a question of their own.
Jesus says "everyone who asks receives." What questions does this raise for you when you think about prayers that do not seem to be answered the way we hoped?

Let the youth voice honest questions without rushing to resolve them. The passage supplies its own frame: the Father gives "good things" (v.11), and, like a wise father, gives bread not a stone and fish not a snake (vv.9-10) — what is truly good, not always what was asked. Point back to the text; do not force a conclusion. Record that they raised a genuine question.

In verses 9-11 Jesus argues from earthly fathers to the Father in heaven. The text says even fathers who are evil still give their children bread, not stones. What "how much more" point does Jesus draw from this about the Father in heaven?

Trace the lesser-to-greater move: if flawed human fathers still "know how to give good gifts to your children," then "how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask Him" (v.11). The certainty of the promise rests on the Father's goodness, which is far greater than any earthly parent's. Meaning is drawn from the text's own comparison, not added to it.

Verse 12 opens with "In everything, then" — a word joining it to what came just before. How does the rule to do to others as you would have them do to you follow from the teaching on prayer and the Father's giving in verses 7-11?

The connective "then" ties the golden rule to what precedes: the Father who freely gives good things to His children (vv.7-11) is both the ground and the pattern for how they are to treat others. Having received good from the Father, they extend good to others — and this, Jesus says, is "the essence of the Law and the Prophets" (v.12). Keep the reasoning tethered to the text's own "then"; avoid speculation beyond it.

In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage: His three commands about prayer, the little picture of a father giving bread and fish to his son, and the rule about doing to others at the end.

A good retelling moves through the flow: ask, seek, knock and their matching promises (vv.7-8); the earthly-father comparison and the "how much more" of the Father in heaven who gives good things (vv.9-11); and the golden rule as the essence of the Law and the Prophets (v.12). This is not a word-for-word memory test — look for grasp of the passage's shape and its move from prayer Godward to treating others.

Verse 12 says to do to others as you would have them do to you. On paper, write one kind thing you would want someone to do for you this week — then write when you will do that same kind thing for someone else.

Look for a concrete, doable pairing: a wish the child names, and a matching deed they plan to do for another person, following verse 12. Record the observable plan (what and when). Assess only that a specific action was written down — never the child's character or motives.
Jesus' words ask, seek, and knock describe growing effort in prayer. Name one specific matter you will bring to God this week, and how you will keep asking about it.

Aim for a concrete prayer plan that shows persistence — a named matter and a way to keep bringing it to God (ask, seek, knock, vv.7-8). Record the observable commitment (what and how often). Do not evaluate the youth's faith or spiritual state, only that a specific plan was stated.
As the passage is read, listen for the three things Jesus tells us to do about prayer. What are they, in order?
Ask, seek, knock, in that order (v.7).
Which word does Jesus use to describe the gate He tells us to enter?
"Narrow." Jesus says, "Enter through the narrow gate." (v.13).
This passage sets two ways against each other. According to the passage, where does the broad way lead, and where does the narrow way lead?
The broad way "leads to destruction" (v.13); the narrow way "leads to life" (v.14). The wide gate and broad way end in destruction, the small gate and narrow way in life.

The passage weighs the two ways not only by their gates but by their crowds. What words does Jesus use for how many travel the broad way and how many find the narrow one, and what is striking about which way is crowded?

Of the broad way "many enter through it" (v.13); of the narrow way "only a few find it" (v.14). The reversal is that the crowded, easily entered way is the one that leads to destruction, while the sparsely traveled, hard way is the one that leads to life.
As the passage is read, listen for two words that describe the two gates. Which gate are we told to enter?
One gate is "wide" and one is "narrow" (and "small"). We are told to enter the narrow gate: "Enter through the narrow gate." (v.13).
As the passage is read, listen for the opening command Jesus gives. What is the very first thing He tells the hearer to do?
"Enter through the narrow gate." (v.13) — the command that opens the passage.

Enter through the narrow gate. For wide is the gate and broad is the way that leads to destruction, and many enter through it. But small is the gate and narrow the way that leads to life, and only a few find it. (Matthew 7:13-14)
But small is the gate and narrow the way that leads to life, and only a few find it. (Matthew 7:14)
Finish verse 13 from memory: "Enter through the narrow gate. For wide is the gate and broad is the way that leads to ..."?
"...destruction, and many enter through it." (v.13).
Luke records Jesus giving a similar call about a narrow entrance, using a stronger word than simply "enter." Which passage in Luke gives this parallel, and what does it urge?
Luke 13:24, the parallel to this passage: "Make every effort to enter through the narrow door." It presses the same narrow way, with a stronger verb of striving than Matthew's "Enter."
This passage says the narrow way "leads to life" but does not name who the way is. Which verse in John's Gospel names Jesus Himself as the way to life and to the Father?
John 14:6, where Jesus says, "I am the way and the truth and the life. No one comes to the Father except through Me." John names the one the narrow way of Matthew 7:14 leads to and by.
This passage says the narrow way is found by "only a few." Later in Matthew, Jesus ends a parable with a saying that also contrasts many with few. Which verse in Matthew states it?
Matthew 22:14: "For many are called, but few are chosen." It echoes the many/few contrast of the two ways, where "many enter" the broad way and "only a few find" the narrow one.
Jesus talks about two gates and two ways. After hearing this passage, what is one thing you would like to ask about the narrow gate?

The aim is that the child forms and voices a real question of their own about the passage (the gate, the way, why so few find it). Welcome any sincere, on-passage wondering; do not correct or answer it for them. Record only that the child asked a genuine question.
Jesus says of the narrow way that "only a few find it." What questions does that line raise for you?

Let the youth voice honest questions the line stirs without rushing to settle them. Keep them anchored to what the passage actually says — the narrow way is hard and lightly traveled, yet it "leads to life" (v.14). Point back to the text rather than supplying a tidy answer. Record that they raised a genuine question.
Jesus pictures two gates and two ways. The passage does not describe real doors and roads. What do the two gates and two ways stand for, and how does the text tell us so?

The gate-and-way pair is a picture of two whole courses of life and where each one ends. The text itself gives the meaning by naming the destinations: one way "leads to destruction" and the other "leads to life" (vv.13-14). Hold the wide/narrow, many/few contrast across both verses rather than allegorizing separate details; the meaning is drawn from the passage's own words.
The passage calls one gate wide and its way broad, and the other gate small and its way narrow. Working only from what the text states, why does the easy, crowded way not turn out to be the good one?

Anchor first to what the text plainly says: the wide gate and broad way "leads to destruction, and many enter through it" (v.13), while the small gate and narrow way "leads to life, and only a few find it" (v.14). The passage measures a way by where it ends, not by how easy or crowded it is; ease and numbers are no proof of safety, since the broad way's very end is destruction. Keep the reasoning tethered to the two destinations the text names; add no doctrine beyond it.
In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage about the two gates and the two ways, and where each one leads.

A good retelling holds the paired contrast: the command to enter the narrow gate; the wide gate and broad way that many take, which leads to destruction; and the small gate and narrow way that few find, which leads to life (vv.13-14). This is not a word-for-word memory test — look for grasp of the two-ways picture and its two opposite endings.

This passage describes two ways. On paper, draw the two gates and the two ways from the passage. Label one wide and one narrow, and write where each way leads. Then write one thing you will do this week that follows the narrow way Jesus points to.

Look for the passage's own labels drawn correctly (wide way to destruction, narrow way to life, vv.13-14) and one concrete, doable action the child names for the coming week. Record the observable plan (what and when). Assess only that a specific action was written down — never the child's character or spiritual state.
Jesus tells about a wide gate and a narrow gate. On paper, draw a wide gate and a narrow gate. Then point to the narrow one, the gate Jesus says to go through.

For pre-readers, the aim is simply that the child can tell the two gates apart and point to the narrow one Jesus says to enter ("Enter through the narrow gate," v.13). Record only the observable action — that the child drew the two gates and chose the narrow one. Do not assess understanding beyond the pointing.

This passage says the narrow way is harder and less traveled than the broad way. Name one specific choice this week where following Jesus would mean taking the narrower, less popular way, and what you will do.

Aim for a concrete, doable choice the youth names — a real situation and the specific action they will take, matching the passage's narrow-way picture (v.14). Record the observable commitment (what and when). Do not evaluate the youth's faith or character, only that a specific plan was stated.
In this passage, who does Jesus tell His hearers to beware of?
False prophets. Jesus says, "Beware of false prophets" (v.15).
Jesus says the false prophets look like gentle sheep on the outside. What are they really like on the inside?
They are really like fierce wolves. They "come to you in sheep's clothing, but inwardly they are ravenous wolves" (v.15).
As the passage is read, listen for who Jesus warns about at the very start, and for the disguise they wear.
False prophets, who come "in sheep's clothing, but inwardly they are ravenous wolves" (v.15).
What two things does Jesus say happen, in order, to a tree that does not bear good fruit?
First it is cut down, then it is thrown into the fire. "Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire" (v.19).
In verses 22-23, many people speak to Jesus on that day and He answers. What do they say first, and what does He then tell them?

First they appeal to their works: "Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in Your name, and in Your name drive out demons and perform many miracles?" (v.22). Then Jesus answers, "I never knew you; depart from Me, you workers of lawlessness!" (v.23).
Jesus says twice, "by their fruit you will recognize them." Within this passage, what stands for the "fruit" that reveals whether a prophet is good or bad?

The fruit is what a tree produces, which shows what kind of tree it is: "every good tree bears good fruit, but a bad tree bears bad fruit" (v.17), and "a good tree cannot bear bad fruit" (v.18). Applied to prophets, their fruit is the good or bad that comes out of their teaching and lives, by which they are known (vv.16, 20).
To those who prophesied and worked miracles in His name, Jesus says, "I never knew you." Read against verse 21, what does this phrase declare about their true relationship to Him?

"I never knew you" means He never owned them as His own; there never was a true relationship, despite their works. Verse 21 supplies the standard: not the one who says "Lord, Lord," but "he who does the will of My Father in heaven" enters the kingdom. Their profession and mighty works were not the doing of the Father's will, so Jesus calls them "workers of lawlessness" (v.23).
What title do the people call out twice to Jesus in verses 21 and 22?
"Lord, Lord." They say to Him, "Lord, Lord," in both verse 21 and verse 22.
As the passage is read, listen for the one line Jesus repeats about how false prophets are known. How many times do you hear it?
"By their fruit you will recognize them" — said twice, at the start (v.16) and again as the conclusion, "So then, by their fruit you will recognize them" (v.20).
So then, by their fruit you will recognize them. (Matthew 7:20)
Not everyone who says to Me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only he who does the will of My Father in heaven. (Matthew 7:21)
Finish the line Jesus repeats: "So then, by their fruit you will ..."?
"...recognize them" (v.20).
Verse 21 says saying "Lord, Lord" is not enough without doing the Father's will. Elsewhere Jesus asks why people call Him Lord yet do not obey. Which verse in Luke records that question?
Luke 6:46, where Jesus asks, "Why do you call Me ‘Lord, Lord,’ but not do what I say?" — the same point as verse 21, that calling Him Lord without doing His will is empty.
This passage warns that saying the right words without doing the Father's will saves no one. Which verse in James presses the same call to do the word, not merely hear it?
James 1:22: "Be doers of the word, and not hearers only. Otherwise, you are deceiving yourselves." It matches this passage's demand that profession be joined to doing the Father's will (v.21).
In verses 22-23, people cry "Lord, Lord" but are told "I never knew you." Later in Matthew, another parable ends with the same kind of refusal. Which passage in Matthew 25 gives it?

Matthew 25:11-12, the parable of the ten virgins, where the shut-out ones cry, "Lord, lord, open the door for us!" and the reply comes, "Truly I tell you, I do not know you." It echoes this passage's "I never knew you" (v.23).
Jesus says you can know a tree by the fruit it grows. What is one question you would like to ask about how good fruit and bad fruit are different?

The aim is that the child forms and voices a real question of their own about the tree-and-fruit picture (vv.16-20), not that they arrive at a set answer. Welcome any on-passage wondering; do not supply the answer or rank the question. Record only that the child asked a genuine question.
