Jesus warns that false prophets look like sheep but are inwardly wolves. What questions does this raise for you about how their disguise could be spotted?

Let the youth voice honest questions raised by the warning in verse 15 without rushing to settle them. The passage offers its own frame — the test of fruit (vv.16, 20) — but the task here is the learner's own wondering, not the leader's answer. Point back to the text if asked; record that a genuine question was formed.
The passage says a good tree cannot bear bad fruit and a bad tree cannot bear good. Reading the tree as a picture of a person, why does Jesus say fruit is a reliable way to recognize a false prophet?

Anchor first to what the text states: "A good tree cannot bear bad fruit, and a bad tree cannot bear good fruit" (v.18). Because the kind of tree fixes the kind of fruit, the visible fruit truthfully discloses the hidden nature. So a disguise (sheep's clothing, v.15) cannot finally hide what a prophet is — their fruit, the effect of their teaching and life, shows it (vv.16, 20). Keep the reasoning tethered to the passage's own logic; do not add beyond it.

Verses 15-20 give a test for recognizing others (false prophets), but verses 21-23 turn to people who called Jesus "Lord, Lord." How does the passage move from testing others to testing one's own profession?

Trace the shift the text itself makes. The fruit test first exposes false prophets on the outside (vv.15-20). Then the same standard turns inward: it is "not everyone who says to Me, ‘Lord, Lord,’" but "he who does the will of My Father in heaven" who enters (v.21). Even prophecy, exorcisms, and miracles in His name are no substitute (v.22); what counts is doing the Father's will, or one is a worker of lawlessness (v.23). The reader who tests others is now measured by the same fruit — obedience, not words. Draw this only from the text's own move from verse 20 to verse 21.
In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage: the warning about false prophets, the picture of trees and their fruit, and His words about those who say "Lord, Lord."

A good retelling follows the flow: the warning to beware false prophets who look like sheep but are inwardly wolves (v.15); the test of fruit, good trees and bad trees and what happens to a fruitless tree (vv.16-20); and the turn to those who say "Lord, Lord" yet do not do the Father's will, whom Jesus never knew (vv.21-23). This is not a word-for-word memory test — look for grasp of the passage's shape and its move from recognizing others to doing the Father's will.

Jesus says a good tree bears good fruit. On paper, draw a tree and write on its fruit one good thing you can do this week that shows doing the will of your Father in heaven — then write the day you will do it.

Look for a concrete, doable deed tied to verses 17 and 21 — a specific good action and a day named for it. Record the observable plan (what and when). Assess only that a specific action was written down; never the child's character or spiritual state. A pre-reader can simply draw one good thing they will do and tell it aloud.

Verse 21 says it is the one who does the will of the Father, not the one who only says "Lord, Lord," who enters the kingdom. Name one specific thing you already know God calls you to do, and when this week you will do it rather than only speak about it.

Aim for a concrete, observable plan that moves from words to doing, following verse 21 — a named action the youth already knows to be right, with a time to carry it out. Record the observable commitment (what and when). Do not evaluate the youth's faith or spiritual state, only that a specific plan was stated.
Jesus tells about two men who each build a house. What kind of man is each one — one is called wise, and the other is called what?

The two men are a wise man and a foolish man. Jesus compares the one who hears and acts to "a wise man who built his house on the rock" (v.24), and the one who hears and does not act to "a foolish man who built his house on sand" (v.26). Look for the learner to name both from the passage.
Listen for the two different places the two men build their houses on.
The rock and the sand. The wise man "built his house on the rock" (v.24), and the foolish man "built his house on sand" (v.26). The two houses differ only in what they are built on.
When the storm comes, the passage lists what happens in order. What three things does it say happen to the house — first the rain, then what, and then what?

In order: "The rain fell, the torrents raged, and the winds blew and beat against that house" (vv.25, 27). Rain first, then the torrents, then the winds that blow and beat. The passage names the same three, in the same order, for both houses — only the ending differs.
The same storm beats on both houses, but they end differently. What happens to the house on the rock, and what happens to the house on sand?

The house on the rock stands: "yet it did not fall, because its foundation was on the rock" (v.25). The house on sand falls: "and it fell—and great was its collapse!" (v.27). Same rain, torrents, and winds strike both; the outcome turns on the foundation, not the storm.
In your own words, retell this passage in order: the wise man and where he builds, the foolish man and where he builds, the storm that comes on both houses, and how each house ends.

A full retelling keeps the passage's order and its exact pairing. The one who hears Jesus' words and acts on them is like a wise man who builds on the rock (v.24); the one who hears and does not act is like a foolish man who builds on sand (v.26). The same storm — rain, torrents, and winds — beats on both houses (vv.25, 27). The house on the rock does not fall, "because its foundation was on the rock" (v.25); the house on sand falls, and "great was its collapse" (v.27). The two accounts match word for word except the one thing that differs: the foundation.

Both men in the passage hear Jesus' words. The wise man "hears these words of Mine and acts on them," while the foolish man "hears these words of Mine and does not act on them." What is the one difference between them?

The one difference is doing. Both hear the same words of Jesus; the wise man "acts on them" (v.24) and the foolish man "does not act on them" (v.26). Hearing is not the dividing line — acting on what is heard is. To hear Christ's words rightly is to obey them, and that alone is the wise foundation.
The passage calls one builder "wise" and the other "foolish." In this passage, what makes the one man wise and the other foolish?

In this passage, wise and foolish are not about being clever or slow. The wise man is the one who hears Jesus' words and acts on them, building on the rock (v.24); the foolish man hears the same words but does not act on them, building on sand (v.26). Wisdom here is obeying what Jesus says; folly is hearing and not doing.
Listen for the two words Jesus uses to describe the two builders — one is wise, and the other is a word that means the opposite.
Wise and foolish. Jesus calls the builder on the rock "a wise man" (v.24) and the builder on sand "a foolish man" (v.26). Listen for both words as the two builders are described.
“Therefore everyone who hears these words of Mine and acts on them is like a wise man who built his house on the rock.” (Matthew 7:24)

“Therefore everyone who hears these words of Mine and acts on them is like a wise man who built his house on the rock. The rain fell, the torrents raged, and the winds blew and beat against that house; yet it did not fall, because its foundation was on the rock.” (Matthew 7:24-25)
Listen for the very last thing the passage says about the house on the sand, and see if you can say it back.
"and it fell—and great was its collapse!" (v.27). This is the closing line, describing the total ruin of the house built on sand.

Jesus tells this same two-builders story in another Gospel. In which passage of Luke does He say the one "who hears My words and does not act on them is like a man who built his house on ground without a foundation"?

Luke 6:47-49, the parallel account of the two builders. There Jesus says the one who hears His words and does not act on them "is like a man who built his house on ground without a foundation," and "immediately it fell—and great was its destruction" (Luke 6:49). The same teaching in a second Gospel confirms the point: hearing must issue in doing. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
James presses the same point Jesus makes here — that hearing is not enough without doing. Where does James write, "Be doers of the word, and not hearers only"?

James 1:22: "Be doers of the word, and not hearers only. Otherwise, you are deceiving yourselves." James says plainly what Jesus pictures in the two builders — that hearing without doing is self-deceiving folly, while the wise hearer acts on the word. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
In the upper room, Jesus ties blessing not to knowing but to doing. In which verse of John does He say, "If you know these things, you will be blessed if you do them"?

John 13:17: "If you know these things, you will be blessed if you do them." The blessing rests on doing, not merely knowing — the same distinction that separates the wise builder who acts from the foolish builder who only hears. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
After hearing about the two houses in the storm, what is one question you would like to ask about this passage?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why the foolish man built on sand, what the storm stands for, or whether the men knew a storm was coming. Receive the child's own question rather than steering to a preferred one; answer simply and honestly where you can.
The passage says the same storm beat on both houses, yet only the foundation decided which stood. What question does that raise for you about your own hearing and doing of Jesus' words?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask how to tell whether they are acting on what they hear, what the storm represents in their own life, or why hearing alone feels safe until testing comes. Let the question stand; the aim is honest self-examination, kept to what the text raises — hearing versus doing, and the foundation — rather than ranging off the passage.
This is a picture-story. The passage says the wise man builds "on the rock" and the foolish man "on sand." Read as a picture, what do the rock and the sand stand for in a person's life?

Anchor first in the text: the builders differ only in that one "hears these words of Mine and acts on them" (v.24) and the other "hears these words of Mine and does not act on them" (v.26). So the rock stands for hearing Jesus' words and doing them, the firm foundation that holds when the storm comes (v.25); the sand stands for hearing without doing, which gives way and lets the house fall (v.27). The passage itself supplies the meaning — acting or not acting on Christ's words — so draw the picture from the text, not from outside ideas.

The passage describes the rain, torrents, and winds striking both houses in exactly the same words, yet only one falls. Looking only at what the text states, why does the storm fall on both alike but ruin only one?

Anchor in the text: the storm is described identically for both — "The rain fell, the torrents raged, and the winds blew and beat against that house" (vv.25, 27). The text gives one reason for the different outcomes: the house on the rock "did not fall, because its foundation was on the rock" (v.25). So the storm tests both houses equally; what decides is not the strength of the storm but what each house was built on. The passage lets the trial come to wise and foolish alike, and only the foundation of obedient hearing holds. Stay with what the verses state; do not press beyond them.
The wise man built his house on the rock. On paper, draw a house standing firm on a big rock, and draw one thing Jesus asks you to do that you will do this week.

There is no wrong drawing. The point is that hearing Jesus' words and doing them is like building on the rock that stands (v.24). Use the drawing to check the child can name one specific, doable thing they will actually do. Note only what the child draws or says they will do; do not judge the child's heart.
The wise builder hears Jesus' words and acts on them. Pick one thing Jesus teaches — like telling the truth, sharing, or forgiving — and do it this week. Afterward, write down what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one specific, doable action drawn from Jesus' teaching and a note of what was actually done — this is building on the rock, hearing and acting (v.24). Record only the observable action; do not assess the child's motives or spiritual state.
On paper, write down one word of Jesus you have heard recently but have not yet acted on, then write one concrete step you will take this week to act on it. Afterward, note that you did it.

No right answer to check. Look for one honestly named teaching of Jesus, one concrete and doable step, and a note recorded afterward — moving from hearing to doing, the difference between the two builders (vv.24, 26). Record only the observable action the learner names and carries out; do not assess their sincerity, character, or spiritual state.
Who were amazed at the way Jesus taught?
The crowds. The passage says the crowds "were astonished at His teaching."
The passage sets Jesus' teaching over against one other group of teachers in Israel. What are those other teachers called?
The scribes. Jesus taught "as one who had authority, and not as their scribes."
On paper, draw Jesus teaching, and draw a crowd of people with surprised faces listening to Him, to show they were amazed at how He taught.

The drawing should show Jesus teaching and a crowd who "were astonished at His teaching" (v.28). The people the passage names are Jesus and the crowds; the scribes are mentioned only by comparison, not as present.
What had Jesus just finished doing right before the crowds reacted?
He had finished teaching. The passage opens, "When Jesus had finished saying these things," and then tells the crowds' reaction.
The passage gives the reason the crowds were astonished. According to verse 29, why were they astonished at Jesus' teaching?
Because of the way He taught: "He taught as one who had authority, and not as their scribes." His teaching (v.28) is the cause; their astonishment is the effect.
In your own words, retell this passage: what Jesus had just finished, how the crowds reacted, and the reason the passage gives for their reaction.

A complete retelling has three parts: Jesus had finished saying these things (v.28); the crowds were astonished at His teaching (v.28); and the reason given is that He taught as one who had authority, and not as their scribes (v.29).
The passage says Jesus taught "as one who had authority." Judging by the contrast the verse draws with the scribes, what does the word "authority" say about the manner of His teaching here?

"Authority" points to the manner and weight of His teaching, not merely its content: He taught as one whose word carried its own right to be obeyed, unlike the scribes, who taught by citing others. The passage marks the difference the crowd itself felt. Draw the sense from the verse's own contrast, not from outside definitions.
The passage says the crowds "were astonished" at Jesus' teaching. What does "astonished" mean about how the crowds felt as they listened?

"Astonished" means filled with wonder or amazement — the crowds were struck by what they heard, reacting far beyond ordinary interest. The passage reports their wonder at His teaching; it does not yet say they believed. Keep the sense to what the word carries here.
Listen for the word the passage uses to describe how Jesus taught — the thing His teaching had that the scribes' teaching did not.
The word is "authority." Jesus taught "as one who had authority, and not as their scribes" (v.29).
When Jesus had finished saying these things, the crowds were astonished at His teaching, because He taught as one who had authority, and not as their scribes. (Matthew 7:28-29)
Another Gospel reports the same reaction — people astonished that Jesus taught as one who had authority, and not as the scribes. Which Gospel records that parallel?

Mark's Gospel, at Mark 1:22: "The people were astonished at His teaching, because He taught as one who had authority, and not as the scribes." Scripture interprets Scripture — the parallel confirms the same point about His authority.
Later, temple officers sent to arrest Jesus come back marveling at the way He spoke. In which verse of John's Gospel do the officers say no one ever spoke like this man?

John 7:46: "Never has anyone spoken like this man!" the officers answered. It echoes the same wonder at the manner of Jesus' speech that the crowds show here, where He taught as one who had authority.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about why the crowds were so amazed, about the scribes, or about the way Jesus taught?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A learner might wonder what the scribes' teaching was like, what Jesus had just been saying, or why authority made such a difference. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not tell us.
The passage says Jesus taught "as one who had authority, and not as their scribes." What does this contrast tell you about why the crowds were astonished?

Anchor first in the verse: the astonishment is tied directly to authority set against the scribes. The crowds were used to teachers who leaned on other authorities; Jesus taught with a weight and directness the scribes lacked, and that difference is what struck them. Keep the discussion on the contrast the text draws, not on speculation about the scribes.

These two verses are the narrator's closing note on the whole Sermon on the Mount. What does the passage emphasize about Jesus by ending on the crowds' astonishment and the reason for it, rather than adding more of His words?

Stay with what the text does: after all the teaching, the narrator turns to how it landed — astonishment, and its cause, the authority not found in the scribes. The closing note frames the whole Sermon by the character and source of the One who taught it, pressing the reader to reckon with His authority, not merely His sayings. Keep the focus on what the passage states, not on doctrine it does not spell out.

The crowds were astonished that Jesus taught with authority. Choose one thing Jesus taught in the Sermon on the Mount that you have heard, and name one concrete, specific action you will take this week to do what He said.

The passage presents Jesus' word as carrying authority; a fitting response is to obey a specific thing He taught. Look for a concrete, observable action tied to a command they can name — making peace with someone, giving quietly, praying at a set time. Help each person name one doable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion or faith.
In this passage, who came and knelt before Jesus to ask Him for help?
A leper — a man with leprosy. The passage says a leper came and "knelt before Him" (v.2).
Where had Jesus just come down from when the large crowds followed Him?
The mountain. "When Jesus came down from the mountain, large crowds followed Him" (v.1).
After healing the man, Jesus sends him to show himself to one person and to offer a gift commanded by another. Which two people does Jesus name?
The priest and Moses. Jesus tells him to "show yourself to the priest and offer the gift prescribed by Moses, as a testimony to them" (v.4).
As the passage is read, listen for who was following Jesus, and for the one man who came up close to kneel before Him.
Large crowds followed Jesus (v.1), and a leper came and knelt before Him (v.2).
When the leper asked to be made clean, what did Jesus do first, before He said "I am willing"?
He reached out and touched the man. "Jesus reached out His hand and touched the man" (v.3), and then said, "I am willing," and "Be clean!"

On paper, put these four moments of the passage in the right order by numbering them 1 to 4: Jesus touched the man; the leper knelt and asked to be made clean; Jesus told him to show himself to the priest; the leprosy was cleansed.
1) the leper knelt and asked to be made clean (v.2); 2) Jesus touched the man (v.3); 3) the leprosy was cleansed (v.3); 4) Jesus told him to show himself to the priest (v.4).
As the passage is read, listen for how quickly the man was healed once Jesus spoke. What word tells you the timing?
"immediately." The text says, "immediately his leprosy was cleansed" (v.3) — the cure follows Jesus' word at once.

The words "clean" and "cleansed" run through this passage. The man does not simply ask to be healed but to be made clean. What does being "clean" mean here that goes beyond just being cured of a sickness?

In the law of Moses leprosy made a person ceremonially unclean — shut out from worship and the community, not merely ill. So "You can make me clean" (v.2) and "his leprosy was cleansed" (v.3) mean more than a cure: they mean being made fit to return, which is why Jesus sends him to the priest (v.4). The term is drawn from the passage's own use, not imported from outside.
The leper asks Jesus to make him "clean." What one word does Jesus say back that means the very same thing has happened?
"clean" — Jesus says, "Be clean!" (v.3). The leper asked to be made clean (v.2), and Jesus answers with that same word.
Suddenly a leper came and knelt before Him, saying, "Lord, if You are willing, You can make me clean." (Matthew 8:2)
Jesus reached out His hand and touched the man. "I am willing," He said. "Be clean!" And immediately his leprosy was cleansed. (Matthew 8:3)
Finish the leper's words from memory: "Lord, if You are willing, You can ..."?
"...make me clean" (v.2). The whole line is, "Lord, if You are willing, You can make me clean."
Luke records this same healing, with the man saying almost the very same words. Which verse in Luke gives the parallel account of Jesus touching the leper and making him clean?
Luke 5:13, the parallel to this passage. There too Jesus "reached out His hand and touched the man," said "I am willing" and "Be clean!", and the leprosy left him at once.
Jesus sends the cleansed man to "offer the gift prescribed by Moses." Which passage in Leviticus lays out the very cleansing rite for a healed leper that the man is told to fulfill?

Leviticus 14:2-32, the law of Moses for the cleansing of a healed leper — the priest's inspection and the prescribed offerings. This is the "gift prescribed by Moses" Jesus directs him to bring (v.4).
The Old Testament also tells of a leper cleansed — Naaman, healed after washing in the Jordan. Which verse in 2 Kings records Naaman's flesh being restored and made clean?

2 Kings 5:14, where Naaman dips in the Jordan and his flesh is restored and clean. Set beside this passage it points up the contrast: Naaman is cleansed through a prophet's directions, while Jesus cleanses the leper directly, by His own word and touch (v.3).
The leper knelt down and spoke to Jesus. What is one thing you wonder about this man or about what happened to him?

Welcome any genuine wondering that stays with the passage — about the man, his kneeling, the touch, or the healing. The aim is that the child forms and voices a question of their own; do not supply an answer or rank the questions. Record only that the child asked a real question.
The man was sure Jesus could heal him — "You can make me clean" — but he was not sure Jesus would want to. What questions does that raise for you about how he came to Jesus?

Let the youth voice honest questions about the leper's certainty of Christ's power and his uncertainty of Christ's will ("if You are willing, You can make me clean," v.2). Do not resolve the questions for them; the aim is that they raise their own genuine wondering. Record that a real question was formed, not its content.

The leper says, "if You are willing, You can make me clean." He is sure of one thing about Jesus and unsure of another. Which one is he sure of, and how do Jesus' own words answer the thing he was unsure of?

The man is sure of Christ's power ("You can make me clean") but unsure of His will ("if You are willing") — v.2. Jesus answers the exact point in doubt: "I am willing," He says, "Be clean!" (v.3), and the healing follows. Keep the reasoning tied to the text's matching of the man's "if You are willing" with Jesus' "I am willing"; do not add beyond what the passage states.

Jesus could have healed the man with a word alone, yet the text says He "reached out His hand and touched the man" — someone others would not go near. What does the passage show by joining the touch to the word "Be clean!"?

The touch shows compassion for one whom leprosy had made an outcast, while the word "Be clean!" shows authority — the cure comes "immediately" upon His speaking (v.3). The passage holds the two together: Christ draws near to the untouchable and heals him by His own willing word. Anchor the point in what the text states (the touch, the word, the immediate cleansing); the deeper symbolism of leprosy is a secondary application, not the passage's stated claim.
In your own words, retell what happens in this passage: how the leper came to Jesus and what he asked, what Jesus did and said, and what Jesus told the man to do afterward.

A good retelling follows the flow: the crowds and the leper kneeling with "Lord, if You are willing, You can make me clean" (vv.1-2); Jesus touching him, saying "I am willing" and "Be clean!", and the immediate cleansing (v.3); and the charge to tell no one but show himself to the priest and offer Moses' gift as a testimony (v.4). This is not a word-for-word test — look for grasp of the passage's shape and its move from the man's plea to Christ's willing word.
The leper knelt and brought his need straight to Jesus. On paper or on an index card, write one thing you would like to bring to Jesus in prayer this week, the way the leper brought his.

Look for a concrete need the child names and writes down, following the leper's example of bringing his request to Jesus (v.2). Record only the observable action — that a specific thing was written to pray about. Do not evaluate the child's faith or the worthiness of the request.
After healing him, Jesus gave the man specific instructions to carry out. Name one clear instruction someone has given you that you could obey this week, and say when you will do it.

The passage models obedience to Christ's direction — the man is told to show himself to the priest and offer the gift (v.4). Look for a concrete, doable action the youth names, with a stated time. Record the observable plan (what and when) only; do not assess the youth's character or willingness.
In this passage, whose house did Jesus go to, and who did He find there sick in bed?
Jesus went to Peter's house, and He saw Peter's mother-in-law "sick in bed with a fever."
Which prophet does this passage name — the one whose words the healings fulfilled?
Isaiah. The passage says this was to fulfill "what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah."
On paper, draw Jesus at Peter's house. Then draw Peter's mother-in-law, first lying sick in bed, and then standing up well again.

The passage names Jesus, Peter's house, and Peter's mother-in-law (v.14). There is no wrong way to draw them; use the drawing to check the child can name who is there and where, and that the woman is sick first and then well.
After Jesus touched the woman's hand, what three things does the passage say happened, in order?
"He touched her hand, and the fever left her, and she got up and began to serve them" (v.15). In order: the fever left her, she got up, and she began to serve them.
Listen for what happens when evening comes, and how many people Jesus healed then.
"When evening came, many who were demon-possessed were brought to Jesus, and He drove out the spirits with a word and healed all the sick" (v.16). He healed all the sick, not only some.
In your own words, retell what happens in this passage: what Jesus does at Peter's house, what happens when evening comes, and what the passage says all of it fulfilled.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus arrives at Peter's house and sees Peter's mother-in-law sick in bed with a fever (v.14); He touches her hand, the fever leaves, and she gets up and begins to serve them (v.15); when evening comes, many who were demon-possessed are brought to Him, and He drives out the spirits with a word and heals all the sick (v.16); this was to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah, "He took on our infirmities and carried our diseases" (v.17).
The passage says Jesus drove out the spirits "with a word." What does that phrase tell us about how He did it?

He did it by simply speaking — no struggle, no ritual, only His word was needed. The phrase points to His authority: the spirits obey at His command. Verse 16 says He "drove out the spirits with a word and healed all the sick."
Once she was healed, the passage says the woman "began to serve them." What does that phrase show about how complete her healing was?

It shows the healing was immediate and full: she does not merely feel a little better but rises at once and is strong enough to serve. A fever normally leaves a person weak; here she is restored completely and turns straightaway to ministering to others (v.15).
Listen for the one word the passage uses for the sickness Peter's mother-in-law had while she was in bed.
"Fever." Verse 14 says Jesus "saw Peter's mother-in-law sick in bed with a fever," and verse 15 says "the fever left her."
“This was to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah: ‘He took on our infirmities and carried our diseases.’” (Matthew 8:17)
Verse 17 quotes the prophet Isaiah. Which verse in the book of Isaiah is this drawn from, and how does its wording compare to Matthew's?

Isaiah 53:4, which reads, "Surely He took on our infirmities and carried our sorrows." Matthew, applying it to these bodily healings, has "He took on our infirmities and carried our diseases," where Isaiah reads "carried our sorrows." This is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9): Matthew shows the Servant of Isaiah 53 to be Jesus.
The Gospel of Mark also records this same evening at Peter's house. Which passage in Mark tells it?

Mark 1:29-34 gives the parallel account of the healing of Simon Peter's mother-in-law and the many who were brought at evening (Luke 4:38-41 records it as well). Comparing the accounts is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); each Gospel is true, and together they fill out the one event.
After hearing how Jesus healed the sick woman and many others, what is one question you would like to ask about this passage?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder how a touch could make a fever leave, why she started serving right away, or what it means that spirits obeyed a word. Receive the question they bring rather than steering them to one you'd prefer.
The passage moves from one woman healed by a touch to "all the sick" healed at evening, and then names it a fulfillment of Isaiah. What question does that movement raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask how the healing of bodies relates to the deeper burden Isaiah 53 speaks of, why Matthew stresses that all were healed, or what it means for the Messiah to bear our infirmities. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.

The passage first describes the healings, then says this was "to fulfill what was spoken through the prophet Isaiah." What two things is the passage joining together, and why might Matthew want the reader to see that connection?

Anchor first in the text: the events (the fever healed, the spirits driven out, all the sick made well, vv.14-16) are joined to the prophecy of Isaiah quoted in v.17. Matthew shows that these healings were no accident but the accomplishing of the prophetic word — this is who the Messiah was foretold to be. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
The passage states that Jesus "healed all the sick" and drove out the spirits "with a word." What do those two details together say about Jesus in this passage?

Begin with what the text says: none who were brought went unhealed, and the spirits left at His mere word (v.16). Together these show His complete authority over both sickness and demons — nothing brought to Him resists His power, and He needs no more than to speak. Keep the meaning to what the passage states; the brief notes His word breaks the works of Satan and heals fully.

In this passage, as soon as she was made well the woman "got up and began to serve them." This week, do one specific act of service for someone in your household. Afterward, write down what you did and when.

No right answer to check. Look for a genuine, specific act toward a real person (not a vague intention), and record what was done as evidence. Note only the observable action; do not assess the learner's attitude or how well it was done.
The woman Jesus healed got up and helped the others. Choose one way to help at home today — carrying something, setting the table, or picking up toys. Then draw yourself doing it.

No right answer. The aim is one small, doable act of helping, mirroring the woman who rose and served (v.15). Note only the observable action the child chose and did; do not assess character. The drawing is a record, not a test.
Who saw the large crowd and gave orders to cross to the other side of the sea?
Jesus. “When Jesus saw a large crowd around Him, He gave orders to cross to the other side of the sea.”
The first man who came to Jesus and said he would follow Him anywhere — what kind of man does the passage call him?
A scribe. “And one of the scribes came to Him and said, ‘Teacher, I will follow You wherever You go.’”
Listen for the two people who come and speak to Jesus in this passage, and notice what each one is called.
First “one of the scribes” (v.19), then “another of His disciples” (v.21). Those are the two who approach and speak to Jesus.

On paper, draw Jesus by the sea with the large crowd, and the two men who came to talk with Him. A grown-up can help you show which man wanted to follow Jesus right away and which man wanted to go home first.

The picture should show Jesus by the sea with the large crowd (v.18), one man — a scribe — eager to follow Him anywhere (v.19), and another man asking to first go and bury his father (v.21). Those are the people the passage names; no others belong in the scene.
When Jesus saw the large crowd around Him, what did He do first?
He gave orders to cross over the sea: “He gave orders to cross to the other side of the sea.” This happens before either man comes to speak with Him.

Put these events in the order the passage tells them: Jesus tells a man to let the dead bury their own dead; a scribe says he will follow Jesus wherever He goes; Jesus gives orders to cross the sea; a disciple asks to first go and bury his father.

1) Jesus gives orders to cross to the other side of the sea (v.18); 2) a scribe says, “I will follow You wherever You go,” and Jesus answers (v.19-20); 3) another disciple asks to first go and bury his father (v.21); 4) Jesus tells him, “Follow Me, and let the dead bury their own dead” (v.22).
In your own words, retell this passage: what Jesus decided to do when He saw the crowd, the two men who came to Him, what each one said, and how Jesus answered each of them.

Checklist for a complete retelling: seeing a large crowd, Jesus gives orders to cross to the other side of the sea (v.18); a scribe says he will follow Him wherever He goes (v.19); Jesus answers that “the Son of Man has no place to lay His head” (v.20); another disciple asks to first go and bury his father (v.21); Jesus tells him, “Follow Me, and let the dead bury their own dead” (v.22).
Jesus says foxes have dens and birds have nests. What does He say about Himself that is different from the foxes and the birds?
Unlike the animals that have their own homes, Jesus has no home to rest in: “the Son of Man has no place to lay His head.” The picture shows His poverty — He has nowhere of His own to lie down.
Jesus calls Himself “the Son of Man” in this passage. Judging only by what He says here, what does the saying attached to that title show us about Him?

Here “the Son of Man” is Jesus’ own name for Himself, and the saying joined to it shows His lowly, homeless condition: He “has no place to lay His head.” Draw the sense from what the verse states about His poverty, not from ideas brought in from outside.
Jesus replies, “Follow Me, and let the dead bury their own dead.” The word “dead” is used in two different senses in that saying. What are the two senses?

The saying turns on two meanings of “dead”: those who are spiritually dead — dead to the life of God’s kingdom — may attend to burying the naturally, bodily dead, while the one Jesus calls is to follow Him. Scripture interprets Scripture: Luke’s parallel adds, “go and proclaim the kingdom of God” (Luke 9:60). The higher sense sets Christ’s call above even a near duty; it does not slight the love owed to a father.
“Foxes have dens and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has no place to lay His head.” (Matthew 8:20)
“Follow Me, and let the dead bury their own dead.” (Matthew 8:22)
Another Gospel gives a fuller account of these same callings, including Jesus’ words about a plow and looking back. Which Gospel records the parallel account of these would-be followers?

Luke’s Gospel: Luke 9:59-62 gives the fuller parallel, where Jesus answers, “Let the dead bury their own dead. You, however, go and proclaim the kingdom of God.” Scripture interprets Scripture — Luke opens the second man’s calling to the work of preaching the kingdom.

Here Jesus puts following Him ahead of even burying one’s father. Later in Matthew, Jesus promises a reward to everyone who has left family for the sake of His name. In which verse of Matthew does He make that promise?

Matthew 19:29, where Jesus says everyone who has left houses or family “for the sake of My name will receive a hundredfold and will inherit eternal life.” It confirms the priority seen here — Christ before the nearest earthly ties — while the honor owed to family remains a real and needful duty.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about the two men who came to Jesus, or about the things Jesus said to them?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder where Jesus slept if He had no home, who the scribe was, why the man wanted to bury his father first, or what happened after they crossed the sea. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say the passage does not tell us some of these things.

Two men come to Jesus. The first eagerly offers to follow Him anywhere; the second asks to first go home and bury his father. What does the passage press home by setting these two different men side by side?

Anchor first in the text: the scribe volunteers, “I will follow You wherever You go” (v.19), while the disciple asks to first bury his father (v.21). Placed side by side, the two encounters make one point — following Jesus costs something real: to the eager man He names His homelessness (v.20), and to the delaying man He puts His own call first (v.22). Keep the discussion on what the passage shows about the cost of following, not on judging the men’s hearts.

When the scribe promises to follow Him anywhere, Jesus does not simply welcome him but answers, “the Son of Man has no place to lay His head.” Judging from the text, why might Jesus meet such an eager offer with these particular words?

Stay with what the text states: to a quick, whole-hearted promise (v.19) Jesus replies by naming His own poverty and homelessness (v.20). The answer sets the real cost before the man rather than seizing his sudden zeal — to follow Jesus is to share a Lord who has “no place to lay His head.” Let learners weigh what the words reveal about the cost of following; do not press on to conclusions about the scribe’s inner state that the passage does not give.

Jesus told these men plainly that following Him would cost them comfort and would come before their own plans. What is one concrete thing you could do this week that puts following Jesus ahead of your own comfort or convenience — a specific action you will actually take?

Look for a specific, observable action rather than a feeling: keeping a set time to read Scripture even when tired, doing a task they would rather delay, giving up a comfort to help someone, or obeying in something they usually put off. Help each person name one doable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion or faith.

On an index card, write or draw one thing Jesus said would be hard about following Him — He had no home to rest in. On the other side, write or draw one thing you will do this week to follow Jesus even when it is not easy.

On the first side, confirm the child names something from the passage — that Jesus had “no place to lay His head” (v.20), or that following Him came before the man’s own plan (v.21-22). On the second side, look for a concrete, doable action for the week. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.
In this passage, who got into the boat with Jesus and followed Him?
His disciples. "When He got into the boat, His disciples followed Him" (v.23).
Where were Jesus and His disciples when the big storm came up?
In the boat, out on the sea. They were in the boat (v.23) when "a violent storm came up on the sea" (v.24).
As the passage is read, listen for who got into the boat and followed Jesus onto the sea.
His disciples. "When He got into the boat, His disciples followed Him" (v.23).
While the storm was tossing the boat and the waves were crashing over it, what was Jesus doing?
He was sleeping. The waves engulfed the boat, "but Jesus was sleeping" (v.24) until the disciples woke Him.

On paper, put these four moments of the passage in the right order by numbering them 1 to 4: Jesus rebuked the winds and the sea; the disciples woke Jesus and cried out to Him; a violent storm engulfed the boat; the men were amazed and asked what kind of man this is.

1) a violent storm engulfed the boat (v.24); 2) the disciples woke Jesus and cried out to Him (v.25); 3) Jesus rebuked the winds and the sea (v.26); 4) the men were amazed and asked what kind of man this is (v.27).
As the passage is read, listen for what Jesus does right after the disciples wake Him, and for what happens to the sea because of it.
He first answers their fear, then "got up and rebuked the winds and the sea, and it was perfectly calm" (v.26). The rebuke comes right after they wake Him, and the calm follows His word at once.
The disciples cry out, "Lord, save us! We are perishing!" What are they afraid is about to happen to them when they say they are "perishing"?

They are afraid they are about to die — to drown as the storm engulfs the boat. "Perishing" here means being lost or dying in the storm; the waves were already overwhelming the boat (v.24), so they cry, "save us!" (v.25).
Jesus calls the disciples "You of little faith." They did wake Him and call on Him to save them, so what does "little faith" describe here — that they had no faith at all, or something else?

Not that they had no faith, but that their faith was real yet weak. They turned to Jesus and cried, "Lord, save us!" (v.25) — that is faith — but it was overcome by fear, which is why He asks, "why are you so afraid?" (v.26). "Little faith" names a true but small and frightened trust, drawn from how the passage sets their cry beside their fear.

The passage says Jesus "rebuked the winds and the sea." A rebuke is a word of command or correction spoken to someone. What does it tell us about Jesus that He stills the storm this way — with a rebuke — and not with any tool or effort?

That He commands the wind and sea by His bare word, the way a master speaks to a servant. He "rebuked the winds and the sea, and it was perfectly calm" (v.26) — no rod, no struggle, only a word, and creation obeys at once. The term shows His authority over the elements, which is exactly what makes the men ask, "What kind of man is this?" (v.27).
The disciples went and woke Him, saying, “Lord, save us! We are perishing!” (Matthew 8:25)
The men were amazed and asked, “What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the sea obey Him!” (Matthew 8:27)
Finish the disciples' cry from memory: "Lord, save us! We are ..."?
"...perishing!" The whole cry is, "Lord, save us! We are perishing!" (v.25).

The Psalms already sing of the LORD stilling a storm at sea for sailors who cry to Him. Which passage in Psalm 107 says of God, "He calmed the storm to a whisper, and the waves of the sea were hushed"?

Psalm 107:28-30. There sailors "cried out to the LORD in their trouble," and "He calmed the storm to a whisper, and the waves of the sea were hushed" (Psalm 107:28-29). Set beside this passage it shows Jesus doing what the Psalm ascribes to the LORD — stilling the sea for those who cry to Him.
Mark tells this same story of Jesus stilling the storm. Which verse in Mark 4 records Jesus getting up and rebuking the wind and the sea so that "it was perfectly calm"?
Mark 4:39, the parallel account. There Jesus "got up and rebuked the wind and the sea," commanded, "Silence!" and "Be still!", "and it was perfectly calm" — the same event Matthew records in v.26.
The disciples ask what kind of man could command the sea. Which single verse in Psalm 89 says to God, "You rule the raging sea; when its waves mount up, You still them"?

Psalm 89:9 — "You rule the raging sea; when its waves mount up, You still them." It is spoken to God; when Jesus stills the raging sea by His word (v.26), the passage shows Him doing what the Psalm says only God does, which is why the men marvel (v.27).
Jesus was asleep in the boat while the storm raged, and then the wind and sea grew calm the moment He spoke. What is one thing you wonder about in this story?

Welcome any genuine wondering that stays with the passage — about Jesus sleeping through the storm, the disciples' fear, the rebuke, or the sudden calm. The aim is that the child forms and voices a question of their own; do not supply an answer or rank the questions. Record only that the child asked a real question.
The passage ends with the disciples' own question: "What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the sea obey Him!" What questions does that scene raise for you?

Let the youth voice honest questions sparked by the disciples' amazed cry (v.27) — about Jesus' identity, His power over the storm, or their fear turning to wonder. Do not resolve the questions for them; the aim is that they raise their own genuine wondering. Record that a real question was formed, not its content.
The disciples ask, "What kind of man is this? Even the winds and the sea obey Him!" Looking only at what happens in this passage, what does the way the storm obeys Jesus show about who He is?

Anchor first in what the text states: Jesus rebukes the winds and the sea, and "it was perfectly calm" (v.26), so that even wind and sea obey Him (v.27). From that plain fact the passage points to His identity — One with command over creation itself, which in the rest of Scripture belongs to God (the disciples' question is meant to be answered by that wonder). Keep the reasoning tied to the passage's own account and its closing question; do not add doctrine the text itself does not supply.

Before Jesus does anything to the storm, He speaks to the disciples, calling them "You of little faith" and asking, "why are you so afraid?" Only then does He rebuke the winds and the sea. What does the passage show by having Jesus address their fear first, before He stills the sea?

The order is deliberate in the text: Jesus answers their fear — "You of little faith," He replies, "why are you so afraid?" — and then "got up and rebuked the winds and the sea" (v.26). By speaking to their little faith before calming the storm, the passage treats their fearful trust as the thing most in need of attention, not only the wind. Keep the point anchored in the stated sequence (rebuke of fear, then rebuke of sea); do not press it into claims the passage does not make.

In your own words, retell what happens in this passage: how Jesus and His disciples set out in the boat, what happened while Jesus slept, what the disciples said when they woke Him, what Jesus said and did, and how the men responded at the end.

A good retelling follows the flow: the disciples follow Jesus into the boat (v.23); a violent storm engulfs it while Jesus sleeps (v.24); they wake Him crying, "Lord, save us! We are perishing!" (v.25); He answers their fear — "You of little faith," He replies, "why are you so afraid?" — then rebukes the winds and the sea into a perfect calm (v.26); and the men are amazed, asking, "What kind of man is this?" (v.27). This is not a word-for-word test — look for grasp of the passage's shape and its move from storm and fear to calm and wonder.

When the disciples were afraid, they went straight to Jesus and told Him. On an index card, write one thing that frightens or worries you, and a short prayer bringing it to Jesus the way the disciples did.

Look for a concrete fear the child names and writes down, with a short prayer bringing it to Jesus — following the disciples' example of turning to Him in the storm (v.25). Record only the observable action, that a specific worry was written and a prayer composed. Do not evaluate the child's faith or judge whether the fear is warranted.

The disciples, in a frightening moment, called out to Jesus instead of facing it alone. Name one specific situation this week where you tend to feel afraid or overwhelmed, and one concrete thing you will do to turn to Jesus in it — and say when.

The passage models turning to Jesus in fear rather than facing the storm alone (v.25). Look for a concrete, doable action the youth names — for example, writing a prayer, memorizing v.25, or telling a parent — tied to a specific situation and a stated time. Record the observable plan (what and when) only; do not assess the youth's faith, courage, or character.
In this passage, who came out to meet Jesus when He arrived on the other side?
Two demon-possessed men. The passage says He "was met by two demon-possessed men coming from the tombs" (v.28).
The passage names the region Jesus arrived in and the place the two men were coming from. What are they?
The region of the Gadarenes, and the men were coming from the tombs. The passage says Jesus arrived "in the region of the Gadarenes" and the two men were "coming from the tombs" (v.28).
Listen for how many men meet Jesus, and where they were coming from.
Two men, coming from the tombs. The passage says Jesus "was met by two demon-possessed men coming from the tombs" (v.28).
Draw the two men by the tombs, and Jesus arriving to meet them. Then draw the large herd of pigs feeding in the distance.

The passage names two demon-possessed men coming from the tombs (v.28), Jesus arriving in the region (v.28), and "a large herd of pigs" feeding in the distance (v.30). There is no wrong way to draw them; use the drawing to check the child can name who is there and where.
When the demons beg to be sent into the pigs, what one word does Jesus say to them?
"Go!" The passage says, "'Go!' He told them" (v.32), and the demons came out and went into the pigs.
After the whole herd rushed into the sea and died, what did those tending the pigs do next?
They ran off into the town and reported it. The passage says those tending the pigs "ran off into the town and reported all this, including the account of the demon-possessed men" (v.33).

On paper, write these five moments from the passage in the order they happen: the herd rushes down into the sea; the two men meet Jesus and shout at Him; the whole town begs Jesus to leave; the demons beg to be sent into the pigs; those tending the pigs run to the town.

Correct order: (1) the two men meet Jesus and shout at Him (v.28-29); (2) the demons beg to be sent into the pigs (v.31); (3) the herd rushes down into the sea (v.32); (4) those tending the pigs run to the town (v.33); (5) the whole town begs Jesus to leave (v.34).
Listen for what happens to the herd of pigs right after Jesus tells the demons to go.
The demons came out and went into the pigs, and "the whole herd rushed down the steep bank into the sea and died in the waters" (v.32).
In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: how Jesus was met by the two men, what the demons said and asked, what happened to the pigs, and how the town responded.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus arrives in the region of the Gadarenes and is met by two violent demon-possessed men from the tombs (v.28); they shout, "What do You want with us, Son of God?" and ask if He has come to torture them before the appointed time (v.29); a large herd of pigs is feeding nearby (v.30); the demons beg to be sent into the pigs (v.31); Jesus says, "Go!" and the demons come out and enter the pigs, and the whole herd rushes into the sea and dies (v.32); those tending the pigs run to the town and report everything (v.33); the whole town comes out and begs Jesus to leave their region (v.34).
The demons ask whether Jesus has come to torture them "before the appointed time." What does the phrase "the appointed time" show the demons expect?

It shows the demons expect a fixed, future day of fuller torment — a time already set — and they dread that Jesus might bring it early. Their own words admit that a settled reckoning is coming and that they are subject to it. Keep to the text's plain sense; do not press it beyond what the passage states.
The word "begged" appears twice in this passage — once of the demons and once of the town. Who begs in each case, and what does each ask for?

First the demons beg Jesus to send them into the herd of pigs (v.31); then the whole town, after seeing Him, "begged Him to leave their region" (v.34). Both the demons and the townspeople beg Jesus to send them away or to go — a striking echo the repeated word draws out. Note the repetition from the text; do not add beyond it.
“What do You want with us, Son of God?” they shouted. “Have You come here to torture us before the appointed time?” (Matthew 8:29)
“Then the whole town went out to meet Jesus. And when they saw Him, they begged Him to leave their region.” (Matthew 8:34)
This same event is told again elsewhere in the Gospels, with more detail. Name one of the parallel accounts of it.

Mark 5:1-20 and Luke 8:26-39 both give parallel accounts of this deliverance among the tombs and the herd of pigs. Either is a correct answer. Reading them alongside Matthew is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite the other account.
Here the demons rightly call Jesus "Son of God," yet remain His enemies. Which verse in the letter of James says that even the demons believe such truth — and shudder?

James 2:19, where James says even the demons believe that God is one — and shudder. It confirms the point of this scene: the demons hold true beliefs about who Jesus is, yet without love or submission, so right belief alone does not make a saint. Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to quote James.
The town here begs Jesus to leave their region. In Luke's telling of this same event, which verses report that the people of the region asked Jesus to leave?

Luke 8:37-39, where the people of the Gerasenes ask Jesus to depart because they were seized with great fear. It parallels the town's plea here in Matthew 8:34. Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite Luke.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why the demons were afraid, why Jesus let them go into the pigs, why the pigs ran into the sea, or why the town wanted Jesus to leave. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
The town's whole response, after two violent men are freed, is to beg Jesus to leave their region. What question does that response raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why the townspeople valued the pigs over the Savior, why deliverance drew fear rather than welcome, or what they were afraid of. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
The demons call Jesus "Son of God" and beg Him rather than defy Him. What do their words and pleading show about Jesus' power over them?

Anchor first in the text: the demons name Him "Son of God" (v.29), dread being tortured "before the appointed time" (v.29), and can only beg — "If You drive us out, send us into the herd of pigs" (v.31) — doing nothing until He says, "Go!" (v.32). Their own words show they recognize who He is and are wholly under His control: they cannot resist, cannot even enter the pigs, without His leave. The brief notes their belief is real but is not the saving knowledge of love. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
The passage first tells what happened to the herd of pigs, and then that the whole town begged Jesus to leave. How does what happened to the pigs help explain the town's response?

Anchor first in the text: the whole herd of pigs rushed into the sea and died (v.32), the herders reported it in the town (v.33), and then the whole town came out and begged Jesus to leave (v.34). The sequence links the loss of the pigs to the plea: having seen what His coming cost them, the town would rather Jesus depart than stay. Draw the connection from what the text places side by side; do not add motives the passage does not state.

The town asked Jesus to leave rather than welcome Him. This week, name one specific way you can make room for Jesus instead — a set time to read the Bible, pray, or worship. Do it, then write down when you did it and what you read or prayed.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable action the learner actually carried out (a named time and what was read or prayed), not a vague intention. Record what was done as evidence. Do not judge the learner's faith or heart — note only the observable action.
In this passage, who came and pleaded with Jesus?
A centurion. The passage says, "a centurion came and pleaded with Him" (v.5). A centurion was an officer in the Roman army.
The passage names the town Jesus had entered when the centurion came to Him. What town was it?
Capernaum. The passage begins, "When Jesus had entered Capernaum, a centurion came and pleaded with Him" (v.5).
When Jesus speaks of many coming to share the banquet in the kingdom of heaven, which three people does He name by name?
Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Jesus says many "will come from the east and the west to share the banquet with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven" (v.11).
Listen for who comes to Jesus with a request, and who that person is asking Jesus to help.
A centurion comes to Jesus (v.5), asking Him to help the centurion's servant, who "lies at home, paralyzed and in terrible agony" (v.6).
When the centurion first tells Jesus that his servant is sick, what does Jesus answer that He will do?
Jesus says He will come and heal the servant. His reply is, "I will go and heal him" (v.7).

On paper, write these five moments from the passage in the order they happen: Jesus marvels at the centurion's faith; the centurion pleads for his servant; the servant is healed at that very hour; Jesus offers to go and heal him; the centurion says just to say the word.

Correct order: (1) the centurion pleads for his servant (v.5-6); (2) Jesus offers to go and heal him (v.7); (3) the centurion says just to say the word (v.8); (4) Jesus marvels at the centurion's faith (v.10); (5) the servant is healed at that very hour (v.13).

In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: how the centurion came to Jesus, what he asked, how he answered when Jesus offered to come, what Jesus said in reply, and how it ended for the servant.

Checklist for a complete retelling: in Capernaum a centurion pleads for his servant, who is at home paralyzed and in terrible agony (v.5-6); Jesus says, "I will go and heal him" (v.7); the centurion answers that he is not worthy to have Jesus under his roof, but Jesus need only say the word and the servant will be healed, reasoning from his own place under authority, where he commands and soldiers and servants obey (v.8-9); Jesus marvels and says He has not found such great faith in Israel, and speaks of many coming from east and west to the banquet while the sons of the kingdom are cast out (v.10-12); Jesus tells the centurion, "Go! As you have believed, so will it be done for you," and the servant is healed at that very hour (v.13).
Listen for what happens to the sick servant at the very end of the passage.
The servant is healed. The passage ends, "And his servant was healed at that very hour" (v.13).
The centurion says, "Lord, I am not worthy to have You come under my roof." What is he saying about himself and about Jesus with these words?

He thinks lowly of himself and highly of Jesus: he does not count himself deserving to have Jesus enter his house. The words show humility toward himself joined to a high regard for who Jesus is. Carry the plain sense of the text; do not press it beyond what the passage says.
The centurion says, "But just say the word, and my servant will be healed." What is he trusting about Jesus' word when he asks only for a word?

He trusts that Jesus' bare word is enough to heal, without Jesus being present or doing anything more. He believes the word itself carries the power to act, even at a distance, so that saying and doing are not two things with Christ. Draw the meaning from the centurion's own words; add no more than the text carries.
Jesus says He has "not found anyone in Israel with such great faith." Reading the centurion's own words in verses 8 and 9, what is the faith Jesus calls great — what does the centurion actually do?

It is a trusting faith: the centurion takes Jesus at His word, asking only that Jesus "say the word" (v.8), and reasons from his own place under authority (v.9) that Jesus' command will simply be obeyed. He is not doing a great work; he is resting on Christ's word to heal at a distance. That confident trust is what Jesus names as great faith. Anchor in what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus warns that "the sons of the kingdom will be thrown into the outer darkness." Set beside the many who "come from the east and the west," who are these "sons of the kingdom" that are cast out?

They are those who belong to the kingdom by birth or profession and rest in that standing, yet are cast out, while outsiders (many from east and west, like this Gentile centurion) come in and share the banquet. The phrase marks people who presumed on their place but are shut out. Keep to what the passage states; do not sharpen it beyond the text.
“Lord, I am not worthy to have You come under my roof. But just say the word, and my servant will be healed.” (Matthew 8:8)
“Truly I tell you, I have not found anyone in Israel with such great faith.” (Matthew 8:10)
“Go! As you have believed, so will it be done for you.” (Matthew 8:13)
The centurion trusts that Jesus need only "say the word" and his servant will be healed. Which psalm declares that God sent forth His word and healed His people?

Psalm 107:20 — "He sent forth His word and healed them; He rescued them from the Pit." The centurion's trust that a word from Jesus can heal echoes what the psalm says of God — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote the psalm.
Another Gospel tells this same account of the centurion who pleaded for his servant. In which passage of Luke is it found?

Luke 7:1-10 gives the parallel account of the same centurion at Capernaum. Comparing the two accounts is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; the learner need not recount Luke's details.

Jesus tells the centurion, "As you have believed, so will it be done for you." Later in Matthew He heals two blind men with almost the same words, tying the healing to their faith. In which passage does that happen?

Matthew 9:29-30, the healing of the two blind men, where Jesus says, "According to your faith will it be done to you," and their eyes are opened. The same pattern — the healing given according to faith — appears in both, Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote Matthew 9.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child might wonder what a centurion is, why the centurion did not want Jesus to come into his house, how the servant got better without Jesus going there, or what "great faith" means. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.

Jesus marvels at the faith of a Roman centurion, an outsider to Israel, and then speaks of many coming from east and west while the sons of the kingdom are cast out. What question does that raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why an outsider showed greater faith than those in Israel, what it means for the sons of the kingdom to be cast out, or how the centurion came to such trust in Jesus' word. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
In verse 9 the centurion says he is "a man under authority, with soldiers under me," who tells one to go and he goes. How do those words explain why he asks Jesus only to "say the word" in verse 8?

Anchor first in the text: the centurion knows authority from his own life — he gives a command and it is carried out (v.9). Reasoning from that, he concludes that Jesus need only speak and it will be done, so he asks only that Jesus "say the word" (v.8). His experience of command under authority is the ground of his trust that Jesus' word alone can heal. Draw the meaning from what he says; do not add beyond the text.

The passage says Jesus "marveled" and told those following Him He had "not found anyone in Israel with such great faith." Looking at what the centurion said just before, why does Jesus single out this man's faith?

Anchor first in the text: the centurion has just confessed he is not worthy to have Jesus under his roof and asked only for a word (v.8), reasoning from authority that Jesus' word will be obeyed (v.9). Jesus marvels because this man, an outsider to Israel, trusts Christ's bare word to heal at a distance more fully than any He has found in Israel (v.10). The text stresses faith that rests on the authoritative word of Christ. Draw meaning from what the passage states; do not add doctrine it does not carry.

The centurion brought a specific request to Jesus and trusted His word. This week, write down one specific request you want to bring to God, then bring it to Him in prayer. Afterward, note the date you prayed and anything you noticed.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, specific request the learner actually wrote down and prayed, and record that observable action as evidence. Do not assess the learner's faith, sincerity, or whether the request was answered — note only what was done.
The centurion asked Jesus to help someone who was sick. Think of one person you know who is sick or hurting. Draw a picture of them, then ask a grown-up to pray for that person with you.

No right answer to check. Look for the observable steps: the child named a real person, drew them, and joined a grown-up in prayer for them. Record that it was done; do not evaluate the child's feelings or faith, only the action taken.
In this passage, who was brought to Jesus on a mat?
A paralytic — a man who could not walk. The passage says "some men brought to Him a paralytic lying on a mat" (v.2).
At the start of the passage, where does Jesus go after He gets into a boat and crosses over?
To His own town. The passage says Jesus "got into a boat, crossed over, and came to His own town" (v.1). The passage does not name the town, so leave it unnamed.
Besides Jesus and the paralytic, the passage names two other groups who are present and react. Who are they, and how does each one respond?
The scribes, who say "This man is blaspheming!" (v.3), and the crowds, who at the end "were filled with awe and glorified God" (v.8). The scribes object silently; the crowds glorify God.
Listen for who brings the sick man to Jesus, and how the man gets there.
Some men bring him, and he is carried lying on a mat. The passage says "some men brought to Him a paralytic lying on a mat" (v.2).
When the paralytic is brought to Him, what does Jesus say to the man first — before He heals his body?
He tells the man his sins are forgiven: "Take courage, son; your sins are forgiven" (v.2). The command to get up and walk comes later (v.6).

On paper, write these five moments from the passage in the order they happen: the man gets up and goes home; some men bring the paralytic to Jesus; the crowds glorify God; the scribes accuse Jesus of blaspheming; Jesus tells the man to get up and pick up his mat.

Correct order: (1) some men bring the paralytic to Jesus (v.2); (2) the scribes accuse Jesus of blaspheming (v.3); (3) Jesus tells the man to get up and pick up his mat (v.6); (4) the man gets up and goes home (v.7); (5) the crowds glorify God (v.8).
Listen for what the paralytic does right after Jesus tells him to get up.
He gets up and goes home. The passage says, "And the man got up and went home" (v.7).
In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: how the paralytic is brought to Jesus, what Jesus says to him, how the scribes react, what Jesus then does, and how the crowds respond.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus crosses over and comes to His own town (v.1); some men bring a paralytic on a mat, and seeing their faith Jesus says, "Take courage, son; your sins are forgiven" (v.2); some scribes think, "This man is blaspheming!" (v.3); Jesus, knowing their thoughts, asks why they harbor evil and which is easier to say (v.4-5); so they may know the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins, He tells the man to get up, pick up his mat, and go home (v.6); the man gets up and goes home (v.7); the crowds are filled with awe and glorify God (v.8).
By what title does Jesus refer to Himself when He says He has authority on earth to forgive sins?
"The Son of Man." Jesus says the healing is "so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v.6), using the title of Himself.
The word "authority" appears twice at the close of the passage. What does Jesus claim to have authority to do, and what do the crowds say God has given such authority to?

Jesus claims "the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v.6); and the crowds glorify God, "who had given such authority to men" (v.8). The repeated word ties the claim (authority to forgive) to the crowd's wonder that such authority was given. Keep to the plain sense the text states; the brief notes the crowd admired Him as a man to whom God had given such power, not yet confessing His deity.
“When Jesus saw their faith, He said to the paralytic, ‘Take courage, son; your sins are forgiven.’” (Matthew 9:2)
“When the crowds saw this, they were filled with awe and glorified God, who had given such authority to men.” (Matthew 9:8)
This same healing of the paralytic is told again elsewhere in the Gospels, with more detail. Name one of the parallel accounts of it.

Mark 2:1-12 and Luke 5:18-26 both give parallel accounts of this same cure. Either is a correct answer. Reading them alongside Matthew is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite the other account.
Here Jesus knew what the scribes were thinking, though they had said nothing aloud. Name another place in Matthew where Jesus likewise knows people's hidden thoughts.

Matthew 12:25, where Jesus "knew their thoughts" and answered the Pharisees. It parallels this passage's note that Jesus "knew what they were thinking" (v.4). Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite the other text.
The prophet Isaiah promised a day when the lame would leap. Which verses in Isaiah give that promise, which this healing of the paralytic brings to mind?

Isaiah 35:5-6, the messianic promise that the lame shall leap. Seeing the paralytic get up and walk (v.6-7) calls that promise to mind. Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite Isaiah.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder how the men carried the man, why Jesus forgave his sins first, why the scribes were upset, or how the man could suddenly walk. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
Jesus responds to the paralytic's need first by forgiving his sins, before He heals his body. What question does that order raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why forgiveness came before healing, what the man most needed, or why Jesus tied the two together. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
Jesus says He tells the paralytic to get up "so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins." Why does He perform the visible healing of the body?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus asks which is easier — to say "Your sins are forgiven" or "Get up and walk" (v.5) — then heals "so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v.6). The visible cure, which anyone can verify, is offered as proof of the invisible authority to forgive, which cannot be seen. He makes the healing evidence of the pardon. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus asks the scribes, "Which is easier: to say, 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say, 'Get up and walk?'" What does the passage go on to show is the point of His question?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus asks the question (v.5), then acts "so that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v.6), and the man gets up and goes home (v.7). Anyone could claim to forgive sins, since no one can check it; but only real power could make a paralytic walk, and that visible power proves the unseen authority to forgive is real too. Draw meaning from what the text places side by side; do not add beyond it.

The men in the passage carried their friend to Jesus. Name one specific person you can bring closer to Jesus this week — for example, by praying for them by name or inviting them to read the Bible with you. Do it, then draw or write who it was and what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable action the child actually carried out (a named person and what was done), not a vague intention. Record what was done as evidence. Do not judge the child's faith or heart — note only the observable action.

When the crowds saw the healing, they glorified God. This week, name one specific thing you can thank and praise God for, as the crowd did. Do it out loud in prayer or in writing, then note when you did it and what you praised Him for.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, carried-out act of thanks or praise (a named time and what was praised), not a vague intention. Record what was done as evidence. Do not judge the learner's faith or heart — note only the observable action.
In this passage, what is the name of the man Jesus tells to follow Him?
Matthew. The passage says Jesus "saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax booth" and told him, "Follow Me" (v.9).
Where was Matthew sitting when Jesus saw him?
At the tax booth. The passage says Jesus saw Matthew "sitting at the tax booth" (v.9).
Who objected to the company Jesus kept at the meal, and whom did they question about it?

The Pharisees objected, and they questioned His disciples. The passage says that when the Pharisees saw this, they asked His disciples, "Why does your Teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?" (v.11).
Listen for who comes to eat with Jesus at Matthew's house.
Many tax collectors and sinners. The passage says "many tax collectors and sinners came and ate with Him and His disciples" at Matthew's house (v.10).
What did Matthew do right after Jesus said, "Follow Me"?
He got up and followed Jesus. The passage says "Matthew got up and followed Him" (v.9).

On paper, write these four moments from the passage in the order they happen: the Pharisees ask why Jesus eats with tax collectors and sinners; Jesus sees Matthew at the tax booth and says, "Follow Me"; Jesus says He came to call sinners, not the righteous; tax collectors and sinners come and eat with Jesus at Matthew's house.

Correct order: (1) Jesus sees Matthew at the tax booth and calls him (v.9); (2) tax collectors and sinners come and eat with Jesus at Matthew's house (v.10); (3) the Pharisees ask why Jesus eats with tax collectors and sinners (v.11); (4) Jesus says He came to call sinners, not the righteous (v.12-13).
In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: how Jesus called Matthew, what happened at Matthew's house, what the Pharisees asked, and how Jesus answered them.

Checklist for a complete retelling: Jesus sees Matthew at the tax booth and says, "Follow Me," and Matthew gets up and follows Him (v.9); at Matthew's house many tax collectors and sinners eat with Jesus and His disciples (v.10); the Pharisees ask the disciples why their Teacher eats with tax collectors and sinners (v.11); Jesus answers, "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick" (v.12), tells them to "go and learn" what "I desire mercy, not sacrifice" means, and says, "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners" (v.13).
Listen for what Matthew does the moment Jesus calls him.
He gets up and follows Jesus at once. The passage says "Matthew got up and followed Him" (v.9).
In this passage Jesus talks about a doctor. Who does He say needs a doctor?

The sick, not the healthy. Jesus says, "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick" (v.12). He uses a sick person needing a doctor as a picture to explain why He is with tax collectors and sinners.
Jesus quotes the words "I desire mercy, not sacrifice." In this passage, what does the word "mercy" point to?

Mercy here means compassion that reaches out to the fallen — the kindness Jesus shows in seeking and eating with tax collectors and sinners rather than holding himself apart from them. He sets this mercy above "sacrifice," the outward religious observance the Pharisees prized.
