Keep to the text's plain sense: Jesus ranks mercy shown to sinners above ritual that would despise them; He is not abolishing sacrifice, but placing mercy first.
“As Jesus went on from there, He saw a man named Matthew sitting at the tax booth. ‘Follow Me,’ He told him, and Matthew got up and followed Him.” (Matthew 9:9)
“But go and learn what this means: ‘I desire mercy, not sacrifice.’ For I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners.” (Matthew 9:13)
Jesus' saying about the healthy, the sick, and a doctor appears in another Gospel's account of this same event. Name the verse in Mark that records it.

Mark 2:17, the parallel account, where Jesus says the same: it is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick, and that He came to call not the righteous but sinners. Reading it alongside Matthew is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite Mark.
When Jesus says, "I desire mercy, not sacrifice," He is quoting an Old Testament prophet. Name the verse He is quoting.

Hosea 6:6, where the LORD says He desires mercy, not sacrifice. Jesus sends the Pharisees to "go and learn" what that prophet's words mean. Reading the prophet Jesus quotes is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite Hosea.
Here Jesus says He came to call sinners. In Luke's Gospel, which verse says the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost?

Luke 19:10, where Jesus says the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost. It echoes His errand here: "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners" (Matthew 9:13). Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9); name the connection rather than requiring the learner to recite Luke.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why Matthew left everything so quickly, why Jesus ate with people others avoided, or what a tax collector was. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
Jesus chose to sit and eat with the very people others labeled "sinners." What question does that choice raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why Jesus sought such company, why the Pharisees found it offensive, or what "I desire mercy, not sacrifice" would change in them. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
Jesus says the sick, not the healthy, need a doctor. In this passage, who are the "sick" He is talking about?

Anchor first in the text: the Pharisees complained that Jesus ate with "tax collectors and sinners" (v.11), and Jesus answered, "It is not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick" (v.12), adding, "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners" (v.13). Read together, the "sick" are the sinners — the tax collectors and sinners He came to call. His picture casts sinners as those who, like the sick, need a physician, and He is the one who comes to them. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus tells the Pharisees to "go and learn what this means: 'I desire mercy, not sacrifice.'" What is He teaching them about their objection?

Anchor first in the text: the Pharisees objected that Jesus ate with tax collectors and sinners (v.11); Jesus answers that the sick need a doctor (v.12) and sends them to "go and learn what this means: 'I desire mercy, not sacrifice'" (v.13). He is teaching that their complaint prizes ritual separation — the "sacrifice" side — over the mercy God actually desires: the compassion that seeks and welcomes sinners. Their objection shows they have not yet learned what the prophet meant. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not press beyond it.
Jesus ends by saying, "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners." What does this tell us about why He came?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus has just called Matthew, a tax collector (v.9), eaten with tax collectors and sinners (v.10), and defended it by saying the sick need a doctor (v.12); then He states His errand plainly: "I have not come to call the righteous, but sinners" (v.13). His coming is aimed at sinners — those who, like the sick, feel their need — not at those who count themselves righteous. The passage presents His call as a seeking errand toward sinners. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus showed mercy by welcoming people others avoided. This week, name one specific person who is easy to overlook, and do one kind thing for them. Afterward, write down who it was and what you did.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable act of mercy the learner actually carried out (a named person and a specific action), not a vague intention. Record what was done as evidence. Do not judge the learner's faith or heart — note only the observable action.
Jesus called Matthew, and Matthew got up and followed Him right away. Draw a picture of Matthew leaving his tax booth to follow Jesus. Then tell a grown-up one way you can follow Jesus this week.

No right answer to check. The passage shows Matthew rising at once to follow (v.9); use the drawing and the child's answer to see they can retell that Matthew followed Jesus, and to note one concrete thing the child names doing this week. Record the observable action, not a judgment of the child's heart.
In this passage, who came to Jesus to ask Him a question?
John's disciples. The passage says "John's disciples came to Jesus" and asked Him about fasting (v.14).
The ones asking say that two groups fast often. Which two groups do they name?
Themselves (John's disciples) and the Pharisees. They ask why "we and the Pharisees fast so often, but Your disciples do not fast" (v.14).
Listen for who comes to Jesus and asks Him a question in this passage.
John's disciples. The passage opens, "John's disciples came to Jesus" (v.14).
What happens first in this passage — the disciples ask their question, or Jesus gives His answer?
The disciples ask first. They "came to Jesus and asked" their question (v.14), and then "Jesus replied" (v.15).
In Jesus' answer, does the fasting He speaks of come while the bridegroom is with the guests, or after the bridegroom is taken away?
After the bridegroom is taken away. Jesus says "the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast" (v.15) — the fasting comes after, not while He is present.
In your own words, retell this passage: who came to Jesus, what they asked, and how Jesus answered them.

Checklist for a complete retelling: John's disciples came to Jesus and asked why they and the Pharisees fast often but His disciples do not (v.14); Jesus replied that the guests of the bridegroom cannot mourn "while He is with them," but that the bridegroom "will be taken from them; then they will fast" (v.15).
Jesus speaks of "the guests of the bridegroom." At what kind of celebration are guests gathered around a bridegroom?

A wedding. The bridegroom is the man being married, and "the guests of the bridegroom" are the wedding guests invited to rejoice with him (v.15). Keep to this plain picture the passage gives; do not press it further.
Jesus pairs the words "mourn" and "fast." What do these words mean here, and why does He set them against the wedding He describes?

Here fasting is a religious act of devotion, and mourning is the sorrow it expresses. Jesus sets them against a wedding because the two do not fit the same moment: guests cannot mourn or fast "while He is with them" (v.15). The passage does not condemn fasting; it teaches that its fitness depends on the season, not on how often it is done. Carry the text's plain sense; do not add to it.
Listen for the word picture Jesus uses for Himself when He answers the question about fasting.
The bridegroom. Jesus answers by speaking of "the guests of the bridegroom" who cannot mourn while He is with them (v.15), picturing Himself as the bridegroom.
“How can the guests of the bridegroom mourn while He is with them? But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast.” (Matthew 9:15)
“But the time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast.” (Matthew 9:15)
This same question about fasting, and Jesus' answer about the bridegroom, is told again in the Gospel of Luke. In which passage of Luke does that account appear?

Luke 5:33-39, the parallel account, where the same question about fasting is put to Jesus and He answers with the same bridegroom saying — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the parallel; do not require the learner to quote Luke.
Jesus pictures Himself as the bridegroom. Earlier, John the Baptist had used the very same picture, calling himself the friend of the bridegroom. In which passage does John say that?

John 3:29, where John the Baptist calls himself the friend of the bridegroom who rejoices at the bridegroom's voice. Jesus takes up the same image John had already used — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote John 3.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what fasting is, who the bridegroom is, why the guests cannot be sad at a wedding, or what it means that the bridegroom will be taken away. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
Jesus says the bridegroom "will be taken from them." What question does that phrase raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask when and how the bridegroom is taken away, why His absence turns joy to fasting, or how this answer honors fasting without commanding it now. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
Jesus does not say fasting is wrong. According to His answer, why can His disciples not mourn or fast right now?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus asks how the guests of the bridegroom can mourn "while He is with them" (v.15). He pictures Himself as the bridegroom, and His presence is the joy that makes mourning unfitting now. The reason is the season, not that fasting is bad — the disciples cannot mourn while the bridegroom is still with them. Draw the meaning from what Jesus says; do not add beyond the text.
Jesus' answer moves from "while He is with them" to "then they will fast." What does this shift teach about when fasting is fitting?

Anchor first in the text: within v.15 Jesus moves from present joy — the guests cannot mourn "while He is with them" — to a coming time when "the bridegroom will be taken from them; then they will fast." The shift teaches that fasting has its proper season: it fits mourning the bridegroom's absence, not the joy of His presence. The passage sets the time for joy and the time for fasting by Christ Himself, the bridegroom. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine it does not carry.
Listen for the reason Jesus gives for why His disciples do not fast at this time.

Anchor in the text: Jesus grounds it in His own presence — the guests of the bridegroom cannot mourn "while He is with them" (v.15). The reason is that the bridegroom is present; fasting will come when He is taken away. Draw the reason from what Jesus says, not from outside the passage.

Jesus says the guests cannot mourn while the bridegroom is with them. On paper, write down two reasons your family has to rejoice because Jesus is present with His people, and share them at the table.

No right answer to check. Look for two concrete reasons the learner actually wrote and shared, drawn from the passage's picture of joy in the bridegroom's presence. Record the observable response; do not assess the learner's faith or feelings — note only what was written and said.
In the first picture, after the new patch pulls away from the old garment, is the garment mended, or does a worse tear happen?
A worse tear happens. Jesus says the patch "will pull away from the garment, and a worse tear will result" (v.16) — sewing the new patch on the old cloth leaves the garment more torn, not mended.
When new wine is poured into old wineskins, Jesus names three things that happen. Put them in the order He gives them.
First the skins burst, then the wine spills, then the wineskins are ruined: "the skins will burst, the wine will spill, and the wineskins will be ruined" (v.17). Keep to the order the verse states.
In your own words, retell this passage: the two pictures Jesus gives — the patch on the garment and the wine in the wineskins — and what happens in each.

Checklist for a complete retelling: (1) No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment, because the patch pulls away and "a worse tear will result" (v.16); (2) men do not pour new wine into old wineskins, or the skins burst, the wine spills, and the skins are ruined (v.17); (3) instead new wine goes into new wineskins, "and both are preserved" (v.17). Look for both pictures and the outcome of each.
Jesus repeats the words new and old. In the picture about wine, what is the new thing and what is the old thing?

The new thing is the new wine and the old thing is the old wineskins. Jesus warns against pouring "new wine into old wineskins" (v.17): the fresh wine is new, the worn skins are old, and the two do not belong together.
Jesus ends by saying that when new wine is put into new wineskins, "both are preserved." What does the word preserved mean here, and which two things are kept safe?

Preserved means kept safe and unspoiled — nothing is lost or ruined. The two things kept safe are the wine and the wineskins: when men pour "new wine into new wineskins," the skins do not burst and the wine is not spilled, so "both are preserved" (v.17). Take the plain sense the verse gives; do not press the picture beyond it.
Listen for the word old, and notice what is called old in each of Jesus' two pictures.

The old garment in the first picture (v.16) and the old wineskins in the second (v.17). In both pictures the old thing is worn and cannot take the new — the new patch tears the old garment, and the new wine bursts the old skins.

“Neither do men pour new wine into old wineskins. If they do, the skins will burst, the wine will spill, and the wineskins will be ruined. Instead, they pour new wine into new wineskins, and both are preserved.” (Matthew 9:17)
“No one sews a patch of unshrunk cloth on an old garment. For the patch will pull away from the garment, and a worse tear will result.” (Matthew 9:16)

Jesus' two pictures show that the new must not be forced on what is not yet ready to hold it. Paul teaches the same thing when he says he gave the Corinthians milk, not solid food, because they were still infants in Christ. In which passage does Paul say that?

1 Corinthians 3:1-2, where Paul feeds the young believers milk before solid food because they were not yet ready for it — the same care Jesus pictures in fitting the new to what can bear it (Scripture interpreting Scripture, WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote 1 Corinthians.

In these pictures Jesus fits the new to what the old is able to bear. Later, in the upper room, He tells His disciples He has many more things to say but that they cannot bear them yet. In which passage does He say that?

John 16:12, where Jesus says He has yet many things to say but the disciples cannot bear them now — the same gentle fitting of the new to what the hearer can carry (Scripture interpreting Scripture, WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote John 16.
After hearing these two pictures about the patch and the wineskins, what is one question you would like to ask about them?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder what a wineskin is, why the old cloth tears, why you cannot just use the old skins again, or what Jesus means by the two pictures. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
Jesus answers with two everyday pictures — cloth and wine — rather than a plain rule. What question does His way of answering raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why Jesus teaches in pictures, what the new and the old stand for, or why forcing them together ruins both. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
Both of Jesus' pictures — the patch and the wineskins — end in ruin. What do the two pictures have in common about joining something new to something old?

Anchor first in the text: in both pictures forcing the new onto the old ruins both. The new patch on the old garment brings "a worse tear" (v.16); the new wine in the old wineskins makes the skins burst and the wine spill (v.17). What they share is that the new and the old cannot simply be forced together — each must be fitted rightly, and the passage shows this by putting new wine into new wineskins so "both are preserved" (v.17). Draw the meaning from what the pictures state; do not add beyond the text.
Jesus says new wine belongs in new wineskins so that "both are preserved." Reading the two pictures together, what do they teach about fitting something new to what is able to hold it?

Anchor first in the text: the pictures teach that the new must be matched to something that can bear it. The old garment cannot hold the unshrunk patch, and the old skins cannot hold the new wine — force them and both are lost (vv.16-17). But new wine in new wineskins keeps "both" safe (v.17). The lesson is fitness: the new belongs where it can be held without ruin. Keep to what the two pictures state together; do not press them into meanings the text does not give.
Listen for why, in each picture, the new cannot simply be forced onto the old.

Anchor in the text: in each picture the old cannot take the new. The unshrunk patch pulls away and tears the old garment worse (v.16); the new wine bursts the old skins and spills (v.17). The reason is that the old is not fitted to hold the new — which is why new wine goes into new wineskins so both are kept (v.17). Draw the reason from what the pictures say, not from outside the passage.

Jesus shows that something new needs to be fitted rightly, not forced all at once. On paper, write down one thing you are still learning to do that takes patience to grow into, and one small step you can take toward it this week. Share it at the table.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete thing the learner is growing into and one small, doable step they actually wrote and shared. Record the observable response — what was written and said — and do not assess the learner's faith, maturity, or character.
Draw the two pictures Jesus gives: a patch sewn on an old coat, and wine poured into wineskins. Then tell someone what happens in each picture.

No right answer to check. Look for two drawings and a simple retelling: the patch tears the old coat worse, and the new wine bursts the old skins. Record what the child drew and said; do not assess the child — note only the observable work.
In this passage, who came and knelt down in front of Jesus to ask for help?
A synagogue leader. The passage says, "a synagogue leader came and knelt before Him," and told Jesus, "My daughter has just died" (v.18).
This passage names several people. Name the two people who come to Jesus for healing, and say who else is with Jesus as He travels.

The two who come for healing are the synagogue leader (for his daughter, v.18) and the woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years (v.20). His disciples go with Jesus: "So Jesus got up and went with him, along with His disciples" (v.19).
When Jesus arrives at the synagogue leader's house, whom does He find already there?
He finds "the flute players and the noisy crowd." The passage says, "When Jesus entered the house of the synagogue leader, He saw the flute players and the noisy crowd" (v.23).
Listen for the woman who comes up behind Jesus and touches His cloak.
The woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years. The passage says she "came up behind Him and touched the fringe of His cloak" (v.20).
Draw Jesus walking on the road with His disciples. Then draw the woman reaching out to touch the edge of His cloak.

The passage names Jesus going with the synagogue leader "along with His disciples" (v.19), and the woman who "came up behind Him and touched the fringe of His cloak" (v.20). There is no wrong way to draw them; use the drawing to check the child can name who is there and what the woman does.
While Jesus is on His way to the leader's house, someone else stops Him. Who reaches Him along the way, and what does she do?

The woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years. On the way she comes up behind Jesus and "touched the fringe of His cloak" (v.20), saying to herself, "If only I touch His cloak, I will be healed" (v.21).

Once Jesus is inside the synagogue leader's house, put these events in the order the passage gives them: the girl gets up; Jesus says she is not dead but asleep; the crowd is put outside; the people laugh at Him.

In order: (1) Jesus says, "The girl is not dead, but asleep" (v.24); (2) "they laughed at Him" (v.24); (3) "After the crowd had been put outside" (v.25); (4) Jesus "took the girl by the hand, and she got up" (v.25).
Listen for what Jesus does last, once the crowd has been put outside.
Jesus goes in and "took the girl by the hand, and she got up" (v.25). Afterward, "the news about this spread throughout that region" (v.26).
In your own words, retell this whole passage: the synagogue leader's plea, the woman who touches Jesus' cloak on the way, and what happens when Jesus reaches the house.

Checklist for a complete retelling: a synagogue leader kneels before Jesus, saying his daughter has just died, and asks Him to come place His hand on her so she will live (v.18); Jesus gets up and goes with him, along with His disciples (v.19); on the way, a woman who had suffered from bleeding for twelve years touches the fringe of His cloak, believing she will be healed (v.20-21); Jesus turns, says, "Take courage, daughter," and "your faith has healed you," and she is cured that very hour (v.22); at the house Jesus sees the flute players and the noisy crowd, says the girl is not dead but asleep, and is laughed at (v.23-24); after the crowd is put outside, He takes the girl by the hand and she gets up, and the news spreads through that region (v.25-26).
Jesus addresses the woman as "daughter" — "Take courage, daughter." What does that word convey in the way Jesus uses it here?

It is a tender, fatherly word of comfort. Jesus does not scold the woman for touching Him but welcomes her with warmth, calling her "daughter" as He speaks courage over her and declares her healed (v.22). The brief notes this is the same tenderness Jesus shows the paralytic He calls "son." Keep to the text's own warmth; do not add beyond what the word carries here.

Jesus says of the girl, "The girl is not dead, but asleep," though the passage has already reported that she has died. What does calling her death "asleep" convey, and why is it not a denial that she truly died?

"Asleep" is Jesus' word for this death, not a claim that she never died — the passage itself reports the father saying, "My daughter has just died" (v.18). By calling it sleep He signals that for Him her death is no more final than sleep: He will raise her as easily as waking a sleeper, and He then does, taking her by the hand so she gets up (v.25). The brief notes death pictured as sleep is short in continuance, a rest in hope for those who die in the Lord. Draw the sense from the text; do not press it past what the passage states.
To the woman Jesus says, "your faith has healed you." According to Jesus' own words, what is it that Jesus links her healing to?

Her faith. Jesus says, "Take courage, daughter," and "your faith has healed you," and "the woman was cured from that very hour" (v.22). The brief notes the cure is honored as the fruit and effect of faith reaching for Christ, not the mechanics of the touch. Keep to what Jesus says; the healing comes from Christ, and He credits her faith.
Listen for the word Jesus uses when He says the girl is not dead.
"Asleep." Jesus says, "The girl is not dead, but asleep" (v.24).
Jesus turned and saw her. “Take courage, daughter,” He said, “your faith has healed you.” And the woman was cured from that very hour. (Matthew 9:22)
She said to herself, “If only I touch His cloak, I will be healed.” (Matthew 9:21)

Another Gospel tells these same two healings — the bleeding woman and the leader's daughter — with fuller detail, even naming the ruler as Jairus. In which passage is that fuller parallel account found?

Luke 8:43-56, Luke's fuller parallel of both healings, where the synagogue ruler is named Jairus. Comparing the accounts lets Scripture open Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote Luke.
Later in Matthew, crowds of sick people beg just to touch the fringe of Jesus' cloak, the same reaching faith shown by this woman. In which passage does that happen?

Matthew 14:36, where the sick beg to touch the fringe of Jesus' cloak, and all who touch it are healed. The same fringe-touching faith of Matthew 9:20-21 recurs there — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote it.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why the woman touched Jesus' cloak instead of asking Him, why the people laughed, how the girl got up, or what Jesus meant by "asleep." Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
The passage sets one healing inside another — the woman is healed while Jesus is on His way to the dying girl. What question does that interweaving raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why Matthew tells the stories nested this way, how the father waited while Jesus stopped for the woman, or what the two encounters together show about coming to Christ. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
The passage says that when Jesus called the girl "not dead, but asleep," the crowd "laughed at Him." What does the passage show us about why they laughed?

Anchor first in the text: the mourners, flute players, and noisy crowd (v.23) were certain the girl was dead — the father himself had said she had just died (v.18) — so Jesus' words "not dead, but asleep" (v.24) sounded plainly wrong to them, and "they laughed at Him" (v.24). Then Jesus takes her by the hand and she gets up (v.25), showing His word was true and their certainty was not. Draw the meaning from what the passage states; do not add beyond it.
In this one passage Jesus meets both a long, incurable illness and death itself. Point to what the text reports in each case, and then say what these two acts together show about Jesus.

Anchor first in the text: the woman had suffered from bleeding for twelve years (v.20) — a long, incurable illness — and is cured "from that very hour" when she reaches for Christ in faith (v.22); the girl "has just died" (v.18), yet Jesus takes her by the hand and "she got up" (v.25). Together the two acts show Jesus' power over both incurable disease and death itself, and that faith reaching for Him is met by Him. The brief notes that, unlike the Levitical priest, Christ touches the dead and is not defiled; He is Lord over death. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.

The woman brought her long trouble straight to Jesus, believing He could help. On an index card, write one real need or worry you are carrying, and beside it write a short prayer bringing that need to Jesus. Keep the card where you will pray it this week.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, named need and a written prayer bringing it to Christ — a doable, paper action the learner actually completes. Record only the observable action (that a card was written and kept); do not assess the learner's faith, the size of the need, or their spiritual state.

Jesus spoke a kind word — "Take courage" — to someone who was afraid. This week, say or draw one kind, encouraging word for someone in your family who is sad or worried. Afterward, tell your leader who it was for and what you said or drew.

No right answer to check. Jesus' words "Take courage, daughter" (v.22) model the encouragement being asked for. Look for one concrete, doable act of encouragement the child actually carried out, and record what was done. Note only the observable action; do not judge the child's kindness or heart.
How many blind men followed Jesus, calling out for Him to have mercy on them?
Two. "As Jesus went on from there, two blind men followed Him, crying out, 'Have mercy on us, Son of David!'"
Where did the blind men come to Jesus after they had followed Him?
Into the house: "After Jesus had entered the house, the blind men came to Him."
After the mute man spoke, two groups reacted very differently. Which group said Jesus drove out demons by the prince of demons?
The Pharisees. While the crowds were amazed, "the Pharisees said, 'It is by the prince of demons that He drives out demons.'"
Listen for the man who was brought to Jesus after the blind men left. What was wrong with him?
He was a mute man who was demon-possessed: "a demon-possessed man who was mute was brought to Jesus." He could not speak until the demon was driven out.
What happened right after Jesus touched the two blind men's eyes?
Their eyes were opened — they could see: "And their eyes were opened."

Put these events in the order the passage tells them: Jesus warns the men to tell no one; the blind men follow Jesus crying for mercy; the men spread the news about Him; Jesus touches their eyes and they are opened.

1) The two blind men follow Jesus crying for mercy (v.27); 2) Jesus touches their eyes and their eyes are opened (v.29-30); 3) He warns them sternly to tell no one (v.30); 4) but they go out and spread the news about Him throughout the land (v.31).

In your own words, retell this passage: the two blind men and what they cried, what Jesus asked them and how they answered, how He healed them and what He told them to do, then the mute man who was brought, and how the crowds and the Pharisees each reacted.

Checklist for a complete retelling: two blind men follow, crying, "Have mercy on us, Son of David!" (v.27); Jesus asks, "Do you believe that I am able to do this?" and they answer, "Yes, Lord" (v.28); He touches their eyes, saying, "According to your faith will it be done to you," and their eyes are opened (v.29-30); He warns them to tell no one, but they spread the news (v.30-31); a mute, demon-possessed man is brought, the demon is driven out, and he speaks (v.32-33); the crowds are amazed while the Pharisees say He drives out demons by the prince of demons (v.33-34).
The blind men call Jesus "Son of David." Judging by how they use it here — begging Him for mercy and healing — what are they saying they believe about who Jesus is?

"Son of David" is the promised Messianic title: David's royal heir, the Christ. By crying it while begging for mercy and sight, the blind men confess their faith that Jesus is the promised King who has power to help them. Draw the sense from how the passage uses the title, not from ideas outside it.
Jesus says, "According to your faith will it be done to you." In this passage, what does He tie the healing to — and what had He asked the men just before?

Jesus ties the healing to their faith. Just before, He had asked, "Do you believe that I am able to do this?" and they said, "Yes, Lord" (v.28). "According to your faith" does not mean a cure measured out by the size of their faith, but a mercy granted to their faith in Him. Keep the meaning anchored in the passage's own words.
Then He touched their eyes and said, "According to your faith will it be done to you." (Matthew 9:29)

Later in Matthew, two other blind men also cry out to Jesus with the very same words, "Son of David, have mercy." In which chapter and verses of Matthew does that later account of two blind men appear?

Matthew 20:30-31, where two blind men beside the road cry out, "Lord, Son of David, have mercy on us!" The repeated Messianic appeal ties the two accounts together. Scripture interprets Scripture: the later passage confirms how this title was used to seek the Messiah's mercy.
Mark's Gospel records a blind man named Bartimaeus who makes the same Messianic appeal for mercy that the blind men make here. In which chapter of Mark is that account of Bartimaeus found?

Mark 10 (verses 46-48), where Bartimaeus cries, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!" and "Son of David, have mercy on me!" His appeal matches the faith of the two blind men here. Scripture interprets Scripture: the parallel shows the same believing cry answered by the same Lord.

Elsewhere in Matthew, Jesus points to the blind receiving sight as one of the signs that the promised Messiah has come. In which chapter and verse of Matthew does He list "The blind receive sight" among these signs?

Matthew 11:5: "The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is preached to the poor." Opening blind eyes, as He does here, is one of the marks that He is the Messiah. Scripture interprets Scripture: the later verse explains the significance of this healing.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about the blind men, about the man who could not speak, or about why Jesus told them to keep it secret?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder why Jesus told the healed men to tell no one, why they told everyone anyway, how it felt to see for the first time, or why the Pharisees said such a harsh thing. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not tell us.

When the mute man speaks, the crowds are amazed and say, "Nothing like this has ever been seen in Israel!" but the Pharisees say He drives out demons by the prince of demons. Both groups saw the same miracle — why do you think the passage sets these two opposite responses side by side?

Anchor first in the words on the page: the crowds marvel (v.33), the Pharisees blaspheme (v.34) — one and the same work of Christ. Setting them side by side shows that Jesus' miracles divide those who see them: the same evidence draws faith and wonder from some and hardened opposition from others. Keep the discussion on what the text shows, not on judging any modern person's heart.

Before healing the blind men, Jesus asks, "Do you believe that I am able to do this?" and only then says, "According to your faith will it be done to you." What does the passage emphasize by having Jesus draw out their faith before He opens their eyes?

Stay with what the text does: the question comes first (v.28), the confession "Yes, Lord" answers it, and the healing follows with "According to your faith" (v.29). By drawing out their faith before acting, the passage presents faith in Christ as the appointed channel through which He grants His mercy — the healing is given to those who trust that He is able. Keep the focus on what the passage states, not on doctrine it does not raise.

In this passage the mute man could not come on his own, so others brought him to Jesus. Who is one person you could bring to Jesus this week by praying for them or inviting them, and what is one concrete thing you will actually do?

Look for a specific, doable action rather than a feeling: naming a person and a set time to pray for them, writing them a note, inviting them to family worship, or telling them one true thing about Jesus. Help each person name one observable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion or faith.
On paper, draw the two blind men following Jesus and reaching out to Him, and draw Jesus touching their eyes. A grown-up can help you label who is who.

The drawing should show the two blind men who followed Jesus crying for mercy (v.27) and Jesus touching their eyes so they were opened (v.29-30). Those are the people the passage names in this scene; the mute man and the crowds come afterward and may be drawn separately.

On an index card, write or draw the two ways people answered Jesus' miracle: the crowds who were amazed, and the Pharisees who spoke against Him. On the other side, write or draw one way you will respond to Jesus this week.

In the passage the crowds are amazed (v.33) while the Pharisees speak against Jesus (v.34); confirm the child names both responses. For the personal side, look for a concrete, observable action — thanking Jesus, telling someone what He did, obeying in one thing. Receive whatever they offer without ranking it or judging their heart.
To whom did Jesus speak the words about the harvest?
His disciples. The passage says, "Then He said to His disciples, 'The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few.'"
According to verse 35, through what places did Jesus go teaching, preaching, and healing, and in what buildings did He teach?

He went through "all the towns and villages," teaching "in their synagogues." The verse says Jesus was "teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness" (v.35).
In this passage Jesus sees the crowds of people. On paper, draw Jesus, and draw the crowd of people He is looking at.
The passage says, "When He saw the crowds, He was moved with compassion for them" (v.36). There is no wrong way to draw it; use the picture to check the child can say Jesus saw the crowds of people.
As Jesus went through the towns and villages in verse 35, what three things was He doing?
He was "teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness" (v.35): teaching, preaching, and healing.
When Jesus saw the crowds, what happened in Him first, and what did He then tell His disciples to do?

First, "When He saw the crowds, He was moved with compassion for them" (v.36); then He told the disciples to "Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest" (v.38). The compassion comes first, the command to ask comes next.
In your own words, retell what happens in this passage, in order: what Jesus was doing as He traveled, what He saw, how He felt, and what He told His disciples.

Checklist for a complete retelling, in order: Jesus went through all the towns and villages, teaching, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing (v.35); He saw the crowds and was moved with compassion, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd (v.36); He said the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few (v.37); and He told the disciples to ask the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into His harvest (v.38).
The passage says Jesus was "moved with compassion" for the crowds. What does that phrase tell us about how He felt toward them?

To be moved with compassion is to be stirred with deep pity, so that His heart was affected by their trouble. The passage ties it to their state: He was "moved with compassion for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd" (v.36). It is not distant sympathy but pity that moves Him to act.
Jesus calls the crowds a "harvest" and speaks of "workers" and the "Lord of the harvest." Within this passage, what do these farming words picture?

The harvest pictures the many people ready to be gathered in — "The harvest is plentiful" (v.37) — set beside the crowds Jesus has just seen. The workers are those sent to do the gathering, and they are "few" (v.37). The "Lord of the harvest" is the owner of the field, the One to be asked to "send out workers into His harvest" (v.38). The brief notes the laborers are men divinely called, and it is God's own work to send them; the field is His.
Listen for the word the passage uses to say how Jesus felt when He saw the crowds of people.
Compassion. Verse 36 says, "When He saw the crowds, He was moved with compassion for them." The word to listen for is compassion.
The passage says the crowds were "like sheep without a shepherd." What does that picture tell us about how the people were?

Sheep without a shepherd have no one to lead, feed, or protect them, so they wander and get into trouble. The passage uses this to show the people were "harassed and helpless" (v.36) — troubled and with no one caring for them. That is why Jesus was moved with compassion.
“The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest.” (Matthew 9:37-38)
“The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few.” (Matthew 9:37)
Later, in the Gospel of Luke, Jesus gives this same harvest-prayer as He sends out a group of workers. Which passage in Luke records it?

Luke 10:2. As Jesus sends out the seventy-two, He tells them in almost the same words, "The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest." The command He gives the disciples here in Matthew He repeats when He sends laborers out in Luke. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
This passage calls the crowds "like sheep without a shepherd." That very picture appears back in Numbers, where Moses asks God to set a leader over the people. Which verse in Numbers uses it?

Numbers 27:17. Moses asks God to appoint a man over the congregation "so that the congregation of the LORD will not be like sheep without a shepherd." Matthew uses the same picture for the crowds Jesus sees, needing a shepherd to lead and care for them. Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9).
The passage says the harvest is plentiful but the workers are few. After hearing it, what is one question you would like to ask about this passage?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder what the harvest is, who the workers are, why there are only a few, or why Jesus felt so sad for the crowds. Receive the question they bring rather than steering them to one you'd prefer; write it down as their own wondering.
The passage sets Jesus' compassion for the crowds right beside a single command — to ask the Lord of the harvest for workers. What question does holding those two together raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why compassion leads first to prayer rather than to action, why the workers are called few when the crowds are many, or what it means that the harvest and the workers both belong to the Lord. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
Verse 38 begins Jesus' command with the word "therefore." Tracing that word back through the passage, what leads Jesus to tell the disciples to ask for workers?

Anchor first in the text: the "therefore" of v.38 reaches back through v.37 ("The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few") to v.36, where "He was moved with compassion for them, because they were harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd." The compassion at seeing the shepherdless crowds is the motive; the plentiful harvest with few workers is the need; and the command to ask the Lord of the harvest is what flows from both. The brief notes the reading move here is to trace the therefore from motive to command. Draw the meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.

In verse 35 Jesus Himself is teaching, preaching, and healing; by verse 38 He is telling His disciples to ask God to send others. Why might the passage turn from what Jesus is doing to a command to pray for more workers?

Anchor in the text: v.35 shows Christ acting — "teaching in their synagogues, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every disease and sickness"; v.38 turns to a command He gives the disciples, to "Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest." The passage moves from Christ's own work among the crowds to the need for many workers because the crowds are so great — "the harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few" (v.37). The brief notes the shift from Christ acting to Christ commanding prayer. Keep the meaning within what the text says; do not press it past the passage's own words.

Jesus' one command here is to "Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest." On paper, write a short prayer asking God to send workers, and name one place or group of people you will pray for this week. Afterward, note the days you actually prayed it.

No right answer to check. Look for a concrete, doable response that matches the command: a written prayer asking the Lord of the harvest to send workers, and a specific place or group named. Afterward, record which days the learner actually prayed it as observable evidence. Do not assess the learner's sincerity, faith, or spiritual state; note only the action they name and carry out.
Jesus tells us to ask God to send workers to help the people. With a grown-up, say a short prayer asking God to send helpers, then draw a happy helper going to the people.

No right answer to check. Help the child pray a simple prayer that asks God to send workers or helpers, in keeping with "Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest" (v.38). Use the drawing to check the child can say we ask God to send helpers to the people. Note only what the child did, not any judgment of their heart.
How many disciples did Jesus call to Himself in this part of the story?
Twelve. Verse 1 tells of Jesus calling His twelve disciples to Him.
Which of the twelve does the passage name as the tax collector?
Matthew. Verse 3 names "Matthew the tax collector."
Verse 2 names two pairs of brothers among the twelve. Who are the two pairs?
First, "Simon, called Peter, and his brother Andrew"; and second, "James son of Zebedee, and his brother John" (v2).

Make twelve small cards, one for each apostle the passage names. As you set each card down, say the name aloud together. A grown-up or older child can write the names; a pre-reader can draw a mark for each one and repeat the name after hearing it.

The twelve named (vv2-4): Simon (called Peter), Andrew, James (son of Zebedee), John, Philip, Bartholomew, Thomas, Matthew (the tax collector), James (son of Alphaeus), Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot.
In verse 1, before Jesus gave the twelve authority, what did He do first?
He called His twelve disciples to Him; then He gave them authority (v1).
According to verse 1, what did Jesus give the twelve, and what did that let them do?
He gave them "authority over unclean spirits, so that they could drive them out and heal every disease and sickness" (v1).
The passage says Jesus gave the twelve "authority over unclean spirits." In this passage, what does that authority enable them to do?
It enables them to "drive them out and heal every disease and sickness" (v1) — power both against unclean spirits and over sickness. The authority is given by Jesus; it is His, delegated to them.
Verse 1 calls the twelve "disciples," and verse 2 calls them "the twelve apostles." Looking at verse 1, what has Jesus just given these men to do?

He has given them His authority to drive out unclean spirits and to heal every disease and sickness (v1). The word 'apostle' means one sent on an errand — the passage shows them being equipped and sent by Jesus, in His authority, not their own.
Matthew alone among the twelve is given a description of his former work: "Matthew the tax collector." What did that term describe, and what does it record about the man Jesus called?

A tax collector gathered taxes for Rome — a despised trade among the people. The label records Matthew's former work plainly; as Henry notes, he brands himself the tax collector as if to have all know how deep a debtor he had been to his Lord. This is observed fact about his calling, not a verdict on his heart.
“And calling His twelve disciples to Him, Jesus gave them authority over unclean spirits, so that they could drive them out and heal every disease and sickness.” (Matthew 10:1)
Here Jesus gives the twelve authority over unclean spirits and sickness. In the parallel account of Luke 9:1-6, what does He then do with them?

In Luke 9:1-6 Jesus, having given the same power and authority, sends the twelve out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal. Matthew 10:1-4 names and equips them; Luke 9 shows the sending that follows.
This passage names "James son of Zebedee, and his brother John." Where in Matthew 4:21 were these two brothers when Jesus first called them?
Matthew 4:21 shows James and John in the boat with their father Zebedee, mending their nets, when Jesus called them. The same two brothers are now listed among the twelve apostles.
The twelve are listed here two by two. In Mark 6:7-13, how does Jesus send them out, and how does that match the pairing seen in this list?
In Mark 6:7-13 Jesus sends the twelve out two by two, giving them authority over unclean spirits. The paired arrangement of the names in Matthew 10:2-4 answers to that two-by-two sending.
The list ends, "Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus." What questions does it raise for you that this name stands among the twelve Jesus called and gave authority?

Let the learner voice their own wondering; do not resolve it for them. The passage states plainly that Judas was one of the twelve and that he betrayed Jesus — both are held together without softening. Receive the questions honestly and, where helpful, invite the family to keep reading Matthew to see how the account unfolds, rather than supplying a tidy answer here.
If you could ask Jesus one thing about how He chose these twelve helpers, what would you want to ask?

This invites the child's own question; there is no single right answer. Welcome whatever they ask and write it down as their question. Resist turning it into a lesson; the aim is that the child learns to bring their wondering to the text and to the Lord.
In your own words, retell this whole passage: how Jesus called the twelve and what He gave them, and then who some of the twelve were, all the way down to the last name.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the passage rather than grading it. Look back together to see whether it touched: Jesus calling the twelve and giving them authority to drive out unclean spirits and heal every disease and sickness (v1); the names, given in pairs (vv2-4); and the closing name, Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus (v4). If a part was missed, let the family re-read and add it; do not supply a model retelling.
The text says Jesus "gave them authority over unclean spirits." What does the passage state about where the apostles' power came from, and why does that matter?

Anchor first to the text: the authority is not the apostles' own — Jesus gave it to them (v1). It matters because their power to drive out spirits and heal flows from Christ, not from themselves; they act as men sent under His authority. Let the family state the observed fact (He gave it) before drawing out the why, and keep the point to what the verse says.

The names are given in pairs, and the roll closes on "Judas Iscariot, who betrayed Jesus." What does the passage plainly state at that closing, and why might Matthew set it there, at the end of the twelve?

Start with what the text states: the last of the twelve is named Judas Iscariot, and the passage adds that he betrayed Jesus (v4). The account holds genuine commission and coming betrayal together in one list, without softening either — the same twelve given authority include the one who would betray. Keep the reflection to what the verses say; save the details of the betrayal itself for where Matthew tells them later.

The passage shows Jesus caring that the twelve could heal "every disease and sickness." Think of one person you know who is sick. Write or draw their name on a card, and pray for them this week. A pre-reader can draw the person and say their name aloud.

The observable response is naming a real sick person and praying for them; the card is the paper evidence. Note that the authority to heal was given to the apostles by Jesus (v1) — the response we ask of the family is compassion and prayer, not a claim to that same authority. Look for a concrete name and a prayer actually offered, not a feeling.
As the passage is read aloud, listen for the names of the twelve. Raise a finger each time you hear a new name.

category.en: Listen for: the names of the twelve

text.en: The twelve names (vv2-4): Simon (Peter), Andrew, James (son of Zebedee), John, Philip, Bartholomew, Thomas, Matthew, James (son of Alphaeus), Thaddaeus, Simon the Zealot, and Judas Iscariot.
As verse 1 is read, listen for what Jesus gives the twelve and what it lets them do. Be ready to say both.
Jesus gives them "authority over unclean spirits, so that they could drive them out and heal every disease and sickness" (v1).
In verse 5, who sends the twelve out and gives them their instructions?
Jesus. Verse 5 says, "These twelve Jesus sent out with the following instructions."
The twelve are told two places NOT to go, and one people they should go to instead. Name both, according to verses 5-6.
They are not to "go onto the road of the Gentiles or enter any town of the Samaritans" (v5); they are to "Go rather to the lost sheep of Israel" (v6).
Which two cities does Jesus name in verse 15 as a point of comparison for a town that rejects the twelve?
Sodom and Gomorrah. Verse 15 says it will be "more bearable for Sodom and Gomorrah on the day of judgment than for that town."
When the twelve enter a home, what are they to do first (verse 12), and what then happens depending on whether the home is worthy (verse 13)?
First they greet its occupants (v12). Then, "If the home is worthy, let your peace rest on it; but if it is not, let your peace return to you" (v13).
According to verse 14, what are the twelve to do when a home or town will not welcome them or heed their words?
They are to "shake the dust off your feet when you leave that home or town" (v14).

Verses 11-12 tell the steps for entering a new town. On three small cards, draw or write these steps in order: (1) find out who is worthy there, (2) stay at his house, (3) greet the home. Mix the cards up, then lay them back in the right order together.
The order from the passage: find out who is worthy there (v11), stay at his house until you move on (v11), then as you enter the home, greet its occupants (v12).
The word "worthy" is repeated in verses 11 and 13 about a person or home the twelve seek out. In this passage, what marks someone as "worthy" — is it their wealth or rank, or something else?

Not wealth or rank. In the passage a worthy home is one that welcomes the twelve and heeds their words (contrast v14), on which their peace rests (v13). The commentary (JFB) calls it a congenial disposition — those disposed to receive the gospel — not social standing.
Verse 8 says, "Freely you have received; freely give." What does the word "freely" mean here — how were the twelve to give what they had?

"Freely" means without charge or gain: they received their power as a free gift and were to give it the same way, not for money. This fits the very next lines forbidding them to carry gold, silver, or copper (v9). As Henry notes, the power to heal was not to be turned to profit.
Jesus tells them to "shake the dust off your feet" from a town that rejects them (verse 14). Within this passage, what does that action stand for, rather than being a mere gesture?

It is a symbolic severance and a testimony against those who reject the message (JFB, MHC). The passage frames it as the response to a town that "will not welcome you or heed your words" (v14), followed at once by the warning of judgment heavier than Sodom's (v15) — so the shaken-off dust witnesses that the refusal, not the messenger, brings the guilt.
“Heal the sick, raise the dead, cleanse the lepers, drive out demons. Freely you have received; freely give.” (Matthew 10:8)
“As you go, preach this message: ‘The kingdom of heaven is near.’” (Matthew 10:7)
Here Jesus tells the twelve, "Freely you have received; freely give" (v8). In Acts 3:6, how does Peter live this out toward a lame man at the temple gate?

In Acts 3:6 Peter says, "Silver or gold I do not have, but what I have I give you," and heals the lame man in the name of Jesus Christ. He carries no money, just as Matthew 10:8-9 commanded, and gives the healing freely — the instruction of this passage enacted.
The twelve are told to preach, "The kingdom of heaven is near" (v7). Where earlier, in Matthew 3:2, was that very same message already being preached?
In Matthew 3:2 John the Baptist preached, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is near." The twelve are given the same message the forerunner had already announced — one unified proclamation.
Jesus sends the twelve to "the lost sheep of Israel" (v6). How does Jeremiah 50:6 describe Israel with that same image, and what does it say had happened to them?

Jeremiah 50:6 says, "My people are lost sheep; their shepherds have led them astray," causing them to wander. The phrase Jesus uses recalls a covenant people failed by faithless shepherds (MHC, JFB) — which is why the first offer of the gospel goes to them.
Jesus tells the twelve to "shake the dust off your feet" at a town that rejects them (v14). In Acts 13:51, who does exactly this, and against whom?
Acts 13:51 says the apostles "shook the dust off their feet in protest against them and went to Iconium," after being rejected. They carry out the very instruction Jesus gave here in Matthew 10:14.
Jesus told the twelve to take no money, no bag, and no extra clothes on their journey (verses 9-10). What would you want to ask Jesus about being sent out that way?

Invite the child's own question; there is no single right answer. Welcome whatever they ask and write it down in their words. Resist turning it into a lesson — the aim is that the child learns to bring their wondering to the text and to the Lord.
In this first sending, Jesus limits the twelve to Israel and tells them not to go to the Gentiles or Samaritans (verses 5-6). What questions does that restriction raise for you?

Let the learner voice their own wondering; do not resolve it here. Note for yourself, if it helps steer honestly: this is a stage-specific limit on the first mission, and Scripture itself later lifts it (Acts 1:8) — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). But do not supply that as a tidy answer; receive the questions and, where helpful, invite the family to keep reading to see how the mission widens.
Verse 8 says, "Freely you have received; freely give." What does the passage state the twelve had received, and why might Jesus tie the way they received to the way they must give?

Anchor first to the text: they received power to heal, raise, cleanse, and drive out demons (v8) — a gift, not something earned. So they must give it as freely as they got it, taking no gold, silver, or copper (v9). The point the passage makes is that the grace behind their ministry is free; keep the reflection to what the verses say and let the family state the observed fact before drawing out the why.

Jesus forbids the twelve to carry money or a spare tunic, "for the worker is worthy of his provisions" (verse 10). What reason does the passage itself give for travelling so lightly, and what does that reason assume?

Start with the stated reason: they need not carry supplies because "the worker is worthy of his provisions" (v10) — the sent worker may rightly be supported by those he serves, which is why they seek out a worthy house to stay in (v11). It assumes God will provide for His messengers through the people they reach. Keep the reflection to what the text says; this is about trust in provision for the mission, not a rule of poverty for its own sake.

In your own words, retell this whole passage: where Jesus sent the twelve, what message they were to preach, what they were to do and not to carry, and what they were to do at a home that welcomed them and at a town that did not.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the passage rather than grading it. Look together for whether it touched: sent to the lost sheep of Israel, not the Gentiles or Samaritans (vv5-6); the message that the kingdom of heaven is near (v7); healing and giving freely, carrying no money or bag (vv8-10); blessing a worthy home with peace (vv11-13); and shaking the dust off at a town that refused them (vv14-15). If a part was missed, let the family re-read and add it; do not supply a model retelling.

Jesus said, "Freely you have received; freely give" (v8). Think of one thing you have been given that you could share or give away freely this week — help, time, or something of yours. Write or draw your plan on a card, then do it. A pre-reader can draw the person they will give to and say what they will do.

The observable response is a concrete plan to give freely and then carrying it out; the card is the paper evidence. Look for a real recipient and a real action, not a feeling. Tie it back to the verse: because we too have received freely from God, we give without expecting return.

The twelve were to enter a home and greet its occupants, letting their peace rest on it (verses 12-13). Pick one household or person you will visit or greet this week, and plan one specific, kind way to bring peace as you arrive — a greeting, a word, or a small help. Write it on a card and do it.

The observable response is a named person or home and one concrete, planned act of greeting or help; the card is the evidence. Look for a specific action actually done, not a good intention. Keep it to what the family can genuinely do, and note that the peace the twelve carried came with the gospel — our greeting reflects that same goodwill.
As the passage is read aloud, listen for where Jesus tells the twelve to go and where NOT to go. Be ready to name both.

category.en: Listen for: where they are sent

text.en: Not to "the road of the Gentiles" or any "town of the Samaritans" (v5); rather to "the lost sheep of Israel" (v6).
As verses 9-10 are read, listen for the things the twelve are told NOT to carry on their journey. Count how many you can name afterward.

category.en: Listen for: what they must not carry

text.en: No "gold or silver or copper in your belts" (v9); no "bag for the road, or second tunic, or sandals, or staff" (v10).
In this passage, who is speaking and sending the disciples out?
Jesus. He says, "Behold, I am sending you out like sheep among wolves" (v.16). The "you" He sends are His disciples.
The passage says the disciples will be brought before two kinds of rulers. Name both.
Governors and kings. The passage says, "you will be brought before governors and kings" as witnesses (v.18).
According to this passage, to what place will men hand the disciples over, and in what place will they be flogged?
They will be handed over to their councils, and flogged in their synagogues. The passage says men "will hand you over to their councils and flog you in their synagogues" (v.17).
Listen for the rulers the disciples will be brought before.
Governors and kings (v.18). They are brought before them "as witnesses to them and to the Gentiles."
The passage lists what men will do to the disciples. In the order the passage gives them, what are the three things?
In order: (1) they "will hand you over to their councils"; (2) "flog you in their synagogues" (v.17); (3) you "will be brought before governors and kings" as witnesses (v.18).
When the disciples are handed over, what does Jesus tell them NOT to do, and what does He promise will happen in that hour instead?

He tells them not to worry "about how to respond or what to say" (v.19). He promises that "in that hour you will be given what to say" (v.19), for "it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you" (v.20).

On paper, put these four moments from the passage in the order Jesus gives them: they are brought before governors and kings; Jesus sends them out like sheep among wolves; they are handed over to councils; they are flogged in the synagogues.

Correct order: (1) Jesus sends them out like sheep among wolves (v.16); (2) they are handed over to councils (v.17); (3) they are flogged in the synagogues (v.17); (4) they are brought before governors and kings (v.18).
Listen for what Jesus tells the disciples to do when they are persecuted in one town.
Flee to the next town. The passage says, "When they persecute you in one town, flee to the next" (v.23).
Jesus says, "I am sending you out like sheep among wolves." In this word picture, are the sheep strong and safe, or weak and in danger?
Weak and in danger. Sheep have no defense against wolves; the picture is of the disciples sent out defenseless among those who would harm them (v.16).
Jesus tells the disciples to be "as shrewd as snakes and as innocent as doves." What does pairing these two pictures together ask of them?

Both at once: snake-shrewdness, a watchful wisdom that avoids needless danger, joined to dove-innocence, a harmlessness that does no wrong and returns no injury. The pairing rules out being merely clever or merely harmless; they are to be wisely careful and blameless together (v.16). Keep to the plain sense of the images.
The passage says it will be "the Spirit of your Father speaking through you." What does this phrase say about where the disciples' words will come from when they are on trial?

Not from themselves. The passage says, "it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you" (v.20). Their words in that hour are given by God's own Spirit, who speaks through them. This is the Spirit's help in the moment, not new revelation added to God's written Word (cf. WCF 1.6). Hold the text's sense without pressing beyond it.
Jesus speaks of "the one who perseveres to the end." What does it mean to persevere to the end here?

To keep going, to hold out and not give up, all the way through the hatred and persecution the passage has just described — "You will be hated by everyone because of My name, but the one who perseveres to the end will be saved" (v.22). Perseverance is enduring to the end rather than drawing back. Keep to the text's own sense.
The passage says, "If the head of the house has been called Beelzebul, how much more the members of his household!" Who is "the head of the house" here, and what is the point of the "how much more"?

The head of the house is the Teacher and Master, Jesus Himself (verses 24-25 name the disciple's teacher and the servant's master). Beelzebul is a slanderous name given to Him. The "how much more" means: if the master was so insulted, the members of his household — His disciples — should expect at least as much, and more. The passage grounds their coming abuse in what was done to Him. Draw the point from what the text says.
Listen for the two animals Jesus tells the disciples to be like: one shrewd, one innocent.
Snakes and doves. The passage says, "be as shrewd as snakes and as innocent as doves" (v.16).
“Behold, I am sending you out like sheep among wolves; therefore be as shrewd as snakes and as innocent as doves.” (Matthew 10:16)
“You will be hated by everyone because of My name, but the one who perseveres to the end will be saved.” (Matthew 10:22)
“For it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you.” (Matthew 10:20)
The promise that "the one who perseveres to the end will be saved" appears again later in Matthew, in the Olivet discourse over Jerusalem. In which passage is that same promise repeated?

Matthew 24:13, where Jesus again says the one who endures to the end will be saved. Matthew repeats the same promise of perseverance in the later discourse — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote Matthew 24.
In another Gospel, Jesus sends out his followers with almost the same picture, calling them lambs among wolves. In which passage does He say that?

Luke 10:3, where Jesus sends the seventy out as lambs among wolves — a parallel sending that matches Matthew 10:16's "sheep among wolves." Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the passage; do not require the learner to quote Luke.
Jesus says the one who perseveres to the end will be saved. Where in Revelation is a persecuted church told to be faithful even unto death, and promised the crown of life?

Revelation 2:10, the word to the church in Smyrna: be faithful unto death and receive the crown of life. It carries the same call to persevere under persecution that Jesus gives here — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote Revelation.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why the disciples are called sheep, who the wolves are, why people would hate them, or how the Spirit could speak through them. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
Jesus says, "Brother will betray brother to death, and a father his child." What question does that hard saying raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why the gospel divides even families, how one could be hated "by everyone" and yet be saved, or how to hold verse 21's grief together with verse 22's promise. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer.
The passage pictures the disciples as "sheep among wolves." Using what the passage goes on to say in verses 17-22, who are the wolves, and what does the picture say about the disciples' situation?

Anchor first in the text: after the image (v.16), verses 17-22 describe men who hand them over, flog them, drag them before rulers, betray them, and hate them. So the wolves are these persecutors, and the sheep-picture says the disciples go out defenseless into real danger from people who mean them harm. Draw the meaning from what the passage itself names; do not add beyond the text.

After foretelling the disciples' suffering, Jesus says, "A disciple is not above his teacher, nor a servant above his master." How do verses 24-25 give a reason for the persecution He has just described?

Anchor first in the text: the passage shifts from predicting suffering (v.16-23) to a principle (v.24-25) — the disciple is not above his teacher, nor the servant above his master. Because the master of the house was slandered as "Beelzebul," it is "enough" for the disciple to share his teacher's lot; the household should expect no better treatment than its head received. The suffering is grounded in the disciples' union with their persecuted Master. Draw meaning from what the text states; do not add doctrine the passage does not carry.
Jesus tells the disciples, "do not worry about how to respond or what to say." According to verse 20, what reason does He give for why they need not worry?

Anchor first in the text: they need not worry because "it will not be you speaking, but the Spirit of your Father speaking through you" (v.20). The reason for their freedom from anxiety is that God's own Spirit will give and speak the words in that hour. Draw the reason straight from what the passage states; do not add beyond the text.

Jesus blesses "the one who perseveres to the end." Pick one good thing that is hard to keep doing (a daily chore, a kindness, a set time of prayer). Do it every day this week, and each day you do it, make a mark on paper. Bring the paper next time.

No right answer to check. Look for a concrete, doable action the learner actually chose and the paper marks showing which days it was done — that is the observable evidence. Record what was done and how many days; do not judge the learner's faithfulness or heart, only the observable action.
In this passage, who knows the number of the hairs on your head and cares when even one sparrow falls to the ground?

Your Father — that is, God. "Not one of them will fall to the ground apart from the will of your Father," and "the very hairs of your head are all numbered." The passage names no one else; the Father is the one who knows and cares.
Jesus says persecutors can "kill the body but cannot kill the soul." What difference does the passage draw between the body and the soul here?

The passage marks a limit on what a persecutor can reach: he can put the body to death, but the soul lies beyond his power — he "cannot kill the soul." Body and soul are distinguished so that the enemy's utmost is shown to stop at the body; the soul he cannot touch. Draw the sense from these verses, not from outside definitions.
The passage uses the language of fear two ways. Whom does it tell you not to be afraid of, and whom does it tell you to fear instead?

Not to be afraid of those who "kill the body but cannot kill the soul" — persecuting men, whose reach ends at the body. To be feared instead is "the One who can destroy both soul and body in hell." The same word is redirected from man to God: fear is not abolished but turned toward the One whose power is greater. Keep the two objects of fear as the passage names them.
Listen for what Jesus says a persecutor is able to do and what he is not able to do.
A persecutor can "kill the body but cannot kill the soul" (v.28). His power reaches the body and stops there; the soul is beyond him. That contrast is exactly what the listener should catch.
Do not be afraid of those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Instead, fear the One who can destroy both soul and body in hell. (Matthew 10:28)
And even the very hairs of your head are all numbered. So do not be afraid; you are worth more than many sparrows. (Matthew 10:30-31)
Listen for how many times Jesus says "do not be afraid" in this short passage.

Three times Jesus tells them not to be afraid: "do not be afraid of them" (v.26), "Do not be afraid of those who kill the body" (v.28), and "So do not be afraid" (v.31). Counting the repeated line is the point.
Draw two little sparrows on your paper, and then draw yourself standing next to them, bigger than the sparrows. This helps you remember what Jesus said: you are worth more than many sparrows.

The drawing helps a young child hold verse 31 in memory: "you are worth more than many sparrows." Two sparrows echo "two sparrows sold for a penny" (v.29); the child drawn larger pictures being worth more. Any faithful drawing of sparrows and the child is right.
Luke's Gospel records this same teaching — do not fear those who kill the body, but fear the One who has authority to throw you into hell. In which Luke passage is that parallel saying found?

Luke 12:4-5, where Jesus tells His friends not to fear those who kill the body and can do no more, but to fear the One who has authority to throw into hell. Scripture interprets Scripture: the parallel confirms and sharpens the redirected fear taught here in Matthew 10:28.
The picture of sparrows and the numbered hairs of your head also appears in Luke's Gospel. Which passage in Luke gives that parallel about the sparrows and the hairs of your head?

Luke 12:6-7, where Jesus says sparrows are not forgotten by God, the very hairs of your head are all numbered, and you are worth more than many sparrows. The parallel repeats the Father's care that Matthew 10:29-31 sets out.

Isaiah teaches the same redirected fear — not to fear what others fear, but to regard the LORD Himself as the One to be feared. Which Isaiah passage sets the LORD as the one you should fear and dread?

Isaiah 8:12-13, where the people are told not to fear what others fear, but to regard the LORD of Hosts as holy and as the One to be feared and dreaded. It is the same move Jesus makes in Matthew 10:28: fear is turned from man to God. Scripture interprets Scripture.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about the sparrows, about fearing God, or about anything Jesus said here?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A child may wonder why God cares about small sparrows, what it means that hairs are numbered, or why Jesus talks about being afraid. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say the passage does not tell us everything.
What question does this passage raise for you — for example, about why Jesus says to fear God, or about how the Father's care fits with letting His people face those who can kill the body?

Open — invite the learner's own wondering and do not supply an answer. Genuine questions here often sit at the tension the text itself holds: the Father numbers every hair, yet the disciples still face persecutors. Let the learner name the question; note it for later study rather than resolving it on the spot.
Three times in this passage Jesus says "do not be afraid." Look at the words around each one: what reason does He give each time for not being afraid?

Anchor to the text at each of the three commands. First (v.26): nothing concealed will stay hidden — what is whispered will be made known, so there is no need to fear secret opposition. Second (v.28): fear God, who can destroy both soul and body, rather than men who can only kill the body. Third (v.31): you are worth more than many sparrows, and the Father who attends every sparrow attends you. Keep the discussion on the reasons the passage actually gives.

Jesus first warns of the One who can "destroy both soul and body in hell," and then speaks of the Father who counts every sparrow and every hair. Why do you think He sets the warning and the comfort side by side?

Stay with what the text places together. The One rightly to be feared (v.28) is the same Father whose care reaches the falling sparrow and the numbered hair (v.29-31): the God to be feared is the God who values you. Setting warning beside comfort steadies the disciple — fear turned toward God is not terror before a stranger but reverence before a Father who holds even the smallest things. Draw only what the passage joins; do not add doctrine it does not state.

Jesus says to speak in the daylight what is told in the dark and to "proclaim from the housetops." What is one specific thing you could say openly this week about what God has taught you from His word?

Look for a concrete, doable action rather than a feeling: telling a friend one thing learned from a passage, answering honestly when asked what was studied at home, or reading a verse aloud to the family. Help the learner name one observable step and, if useful, when and to whom. Affirm the plan; do not weigh their courage or faith.

On an index card, write or draw one true thing about God from this passage — for example, that He counts the sparrows and the hairs of your head. On the back, write one place — home, school, or with a friend — where you will say it out loud this week.

The front should hold a truth the passage actually states (the Father knows every sparrow and numbered hair, v.29-30; He is the One to be feared, v.28). The back names a specific, observable place to speak it, matching Jesus' call to say openly what is heard (v.27). Receive whatever the child offers without ranking it or judging their heart.
In these verses, where does Jesus say His Father is?
