In heaven. Both verses say Jesus will confess or deny a person "before My Father in heaven" (vv32-33).
The passage names two settings, two 'before whom' places. Before whom does a person confess or deny Jesus, and before whom does Jesus then confess or deny that person?
A person confesses or denies Jesus "before men," and Jesus confesses or denies that person "before My Father in heaven" (vv32-33).
Verse 32 speaks of the one who "confesses Me before men." In this passage, what does it mean to confess Jesus before men?
To acknowledge or own Jesus openly, in front of other people — to say plainly that you belong to Him rather than hiding it. The verse sets this in the open, "before men" (v32).
Verse 33 sets "denies Me" as the opposite of "confesses Me." In this passage, what does it mean to deny Jesus before men?

To disown Jesus in front of others — to refuse to acknowledge Him, the direct opposite of the open confessing in verse 32. The two verses are mirror clauses: confess is answered by confess, deny by deny (vv32-33).
The phrase "before men" appears in both verses. What does that phrase add to the confessing and the denying the passage describes?

"Before men" makes it public — done openly, in front of other people, not privately or in secret. The passage weighs the open owning or disowning of Jesus in the sight of others; that public act "before men" is what Jesus answers in kind "before My Father in heaven" (vv32-33).

“Therefore everyone who confesses Me before men, I will also confess him before My Father in heaven. But whoever denies Me before men, I will also deny him before My Father in heaven.” (Matthew 10:32-33)
Here Jesus promises to confess before His Father the one who confesses Him. In Revelation 3:5, what does the risen Christ promise to do with the overcomer's name?

In Revelation 3:5 Christ says, "I will confess his name before My Father and His angels." It echoes this passage's promise (v32): the one who confesses Christ is himself confessed by Christ before the Father.
Verse 33 warns that whoever denies Jesus will be denied before the Father. How does 2 Timothy 2:12 state the same rule?
2 Timothy 2:12 says, "if we deny Him, He will also deny us." It repeats the mirrored logic of verse 33 — a denial of Christ is answered by His denial in kind.
Luke 12:8-9 tells this same saying of Jesus. In Luke's words, before whom will the Son of Man confess the one who confesses Him?

In Luke 12:8 Jesus says the Son of Man "will also confess him before the angels of God." It is the parallel of this saying; where Matthew says "before My Father in heaven" (v32), Luke says "before the angels of God."
Jesus says He will speak about us to His Father in heaven. What is one thing you wonder about when you hear that?

This invites the child's own wondering; there is no single right answer. Welcome whatever they ask and write it down as their question. Do not turn it into a lesson or supply the answer — the aim is that the child learns to bring their questions to the text and to the Lord.
The two verses mirror each other exactly: confess and Jesus confesses; deny and Jesus denies. What questions does that exact matching raise for you?

Let the learner voice their own wondering about the symmetry; do not resolve it for them. The passage states both sides plainly and holds them together. Receive the questions honestly, and where helpful invite the family to keep reading Matthew rather than supplying a tidy answer here.
In your own words, retell what Jesus says in these two verses: what happens when someone owns Him before others, and what happens when someone disowns Him before others.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the passage rather than grading it. Look together to see whether it touched both sides: the one who confesses Jesus before men is confessed by Him before the Father in heaven (v32), and the one who denies Jesus before men is denied by Him before the Father (v33). If a side was missed, let the family re-read and add it; do not supply a model retelling.

Trace the matching parts of the two verses: a person acts "before men," and Jesus answers "before My Father in heaven." What is the same, and what changes, from the first half of each verse to the second?

Anchor first to what the text says: the verb stays the same within each verse (confess answered by confess in v32; deny answered by deny in v33), so a person's own act is returned to them in kind. What changes is the arena — from "before men" to "before My Father in heaven." Let the family point to the words before drawing out why the matching matters, and keep it to what the verses state.

In each verse the setting shifts from "before men" to "before My Father in heaven." What does the passage state about that shift of setting, and why might it matter that the answer is given before the Father?

Start with what the text states: what a person does openly before other people, Jesus answers openly before His Father in heaven (vv32-33). It matters because the final and weightiest arena is not the crowd of men but the presence of the Father — the passage sets the public owning or disowning of Christ under the eye of heaven. Keep the reflection to what the verses say and let the family state the observed shift before drawing the point.

This passage is about owning Jesus "before men" — speaking of Him openly. On a card, write the name of one person you could tell something true about Jesus this week, and what you might say. A pre-reader can draw the person and say aloud one thing about Jesus they would tell them.

The observable response is naming a real person and a concrete true thing the learner could say about Jesus; the card is the paper evidence. Look for a specific name and a specific sentence, not a feeling or a promise. This asks for one doable act of open speaking, in keeping with confessing "before men" (v32); it is not a test of the child's faith or standing.
As the passage is read aloud, listen for two opposite words Jesus uses about how people treat Him. Be ready to say both.
The two opposite words are "confesses" (v32) and "denies" (v33) — owning Jesus before men, and disowning Him before men.
As the passage is read aloud, listen for the two 'before whom' settings — where people act toward Jesus, and where Jesus answers. Be ready to name both.
People confess or deny Jesus "before men," and Jesus confesses or denies them "before My Father in heaven" (vv32-33).
Jesus says He came to turn family members against one another. Which members of the household does He name?
Three pairs: "a man against his father, a daughter against her mother, a daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law." He sums it up, "A man's enemies will be the members of his own household."
Jesus says, "I have not come to bring peace, but a sword." He is not speaking of a real metal weapon. What does "a sword" stand for here?

The "sword" pictures division and strife — the conflict the gospel stirs up, even within a household, as the next verses show. The fault lies not in Christ or His message but in those who reject Him. Draw the sense from the passage, not from ideas outside it.
Jesus says whoever "does not take up his cross and follow Me is not worthy of Me." In this passage, what does taking up your cross point to?

A cross was carried by someone going out to be put to death. In its primary sense here it means a readiness to follow Christ even to the point of death, not merely putting up with everyday troubles. It stands beside "loses his life for My sake" in the next verse.
The phrase "is not worthy of Me" repeats three times. According to this passage, what three things make a person "not worthy"?

Loving father or mother more than Christ, loving son or daughter more than Christ, and refusing to take up one's cross and follow Him: "Anyone who loves his father or mother more than Me is not worthy of Me; anyone who loves his son or daughter more than Me is not worthy of Me; and anyone who does not take up his cross and follow Me is not worthy of Me."
"Whoever finds his life will lose it, and whoever loses his life for My sake will find it." (Matthew 10:39)
"Do not assume that I have come to bring peace to the earth; I have not come to bring peace, but a sword." (Matthew 10:34)
Listen for the one thing Jesus says He did not come to bring, and the one thing He says He came to bring instead.
He came not to bring peace but a sword: "I have not come to bring peace, but a sword." The "sword" stands for division, not a real weapon.

The saying about a divided household — father against son, a man's enemies being those of his own house — is quoted from an Old Testament prophet. From which prophet's book does this household-enmity saying come?
Micah 7:6, which the brief names as the source. Jesus takes up the prophet's words that a person's enemies are those of his own household — Scripture interpreting Scripture.
Luke's Gospel records Jesus stating this same demand even more sharply — that a disciple must love Him above father and mother. In which verse of Luke does that sharper form of the saying appear?

Luke 14:26, where Jesus speaks of loving Him above father and mother (and even one's own life). It sharpens "loves his father or mother more than Me is not worthy of Me" here in Matthew. Scripture interprets Scripture.

Jesus' paradox — that whoever loses his life will find it — is stated again in John's Gospel. In which verse does John record Jesus saying the one who loves his life loses it, and the one who hates his life keeps it?
John 12:25 restates the same paradox that glosses "whoever loses his life for My sake will find it" here. Setting them side by side lets Scripture interpret Scripture.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask — about the sword Jesus brings, about loving Him more than family, or about losing your life to find it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A learner may wonder how Jesus can bring peace elsewhere and a sword here, why following Him could divide a family, or what it means to lose one's life to find it. Receive the question they bring rather than steering to one you prefer; it is fine to say some things the passage does not spell out.
As you listen, notice the hard sayings about family, a sword, and losing your life, and hold on to one thing you most want to ask about afterward.

This invites the learner's own question, not a fact to report back. Give them room to raise whatever the passage stirs — division in a household, loving Christ first, or the life-paradox — without supplying an answer in advance.

Jesus says, "Whoever finds his life will lose it, and whoever loses his life for My sake will find it." The word 'life' is used in two senses. What does the verse say happens, and why would losing life lead to finding it?

First anchor in the words: finding life leads to losing it, and losing life for Christ's sake leads to finding it (v.39). "Life" carries a double sense — the natural, temporal life and the higher, lasting life. Surrendering the lower for Christ's sake is the way the higher is kept, while grasping the lower forfeits it. Hold both senses without flattening either, and keep to what the verse states rather than speculation.

The passage moves from Jesus bringing "a sword" that divides households (verses 34-36) to what a person must love and give up (verses 37-39). What changes in what Jesus is talking about between the first part and the second?

Anchor in the text: verses 34-36 describe division coming from outside — enmity even within one's household; verses 37-39 turn inward to the learner's own allegiance and self-denial — whom you love most, and whether you will take up your cross. The passage shifts from the division the gospel provokes to the wholehearted loyalty it demands. Keep the discussion on what the verses show.
Three times Jesus says a person "is not worthy of Me." Look at what He names each time. Why would loving father, mother, son, or daughter more than Jesus make someone not worthy of Him?

Start with the words: the conditions are loving father or mother more than Christ, loving son or daughter more than Christ, and refusing to take up one's cross and follow (v.37-38). To be worthy of Christ is to hold Him above every other love, even the dearest family ties. The point is the supremacy of Christ's claim, drawn from the verses themselves, not a low view of family. Keep to what the text states.

In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in this passage: what He says He came to bring, how it affects a household, whom a person must love most, what taking up the cross means, and the paradox about finding and losing life.

A complete retelling touches: Jesus came not to bring peace but a sword, bringing division even within a household (v.34-36); anyone who loves father, mother, son, or daughter more than Him is not worthy of Him (v.37); one must take up his cross and follow (v.38); and whoever finds his life will lose it, while whoever loses it for Christ's sake will find it (v.39). Receive the learner's own wording; check that the main movements are present without requiring exact phrasing.
Jesus calls for loving Him above even the closest family. What is one concrete thing you could do this week that would show Christ comes first for you — a specific action you will actually take?

Look for a specific, observable action rather than a feeling or a promise about attitude: keeping a set time with Scripture and prayer before other things, obeying Christ in a matter where a person or habit pulls the other way, or doing a hard right thing that has been put off. Help each person name one doable step and, if useful, when they will take it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their love for Christ or judge their heart.
Draw a big heart on paper. In the middle, draw or write Jesus, because Jesus says to love Him most of all. Around the edge, draw the people in your family, whom you also love.

The passage teaches loving Christ above even father, mother, son, and daughter (v.37). The picture should put Jesus at the center with family around — helping a young child see that loving Jesus most does not mean not loving family. Receive whatever the child draws warmly; do not rank or assess their devotion.
In these verses Jesus names three kinds of people whose welcome earns a reward. Which three does He name?

A prophet, a righteous man, and "one of these little ones" — the passage names whoever receives a prophet, whoever receives a righteous man, and anyone who gives a cup of cold water to a little one. They form a descending scale, from the honored prophet down to the lowliest disciple.
Jesus says that whoever receives Him also receives one other Person. How does Jesus describe that Person in verse 40?
As "the One who sent Me": "he who receives Me receives the One who sent Me." The passage itself does not use the word Father here, only this description; keep the answer to what the verse states.
The word "receives" is used again and again in these verses. When Jesus says someone "receives" His messenger, what is that person doing?

Welcoming and taking that person in — entertaining them as a guest, showing them kindness for their Master's sake. It is more than merely meeting them; it is gladly making room for them. The sense is drawn from how the passage uses the word, alongside the cup-of-water example.
Jesus speaks of giving a cup of water to "one of these little ones because he is My disciple." Within this passage, who are "these little ones"?

Jesus' own disciples — here the lowliest and least noticed of them, as the added words "because he is My disciple" make plain. "Little ones" points to their lowliness and small standing in the world's eyes, not to their age; they are the bottom of the descending list after prophet and righteous man. Draw the sense from the passage itself.
Jesus says even "a cup of cold water" given to one of His little ones will be rewarded. Why does He pick such a small, ordinary gift as His example?

"A cup of cold water" stands for the smallest, cheapest service anyone could give — costing almost nothing. By choosing the least gift Jesus shows that even the tiniest kindness done for His sake is seen and surely rewarded, so no service is too small to count. The sense comes from the passage's own example.

The passage repeats a reason clause: receiving a prophet "because he is a prophet," a righteous man "because he is a righteous man," and giving water "because he is My disciple." What does this repeated "because..." phrase tell us about the reward?

It fixes the motive: the welcome or gift is given for the sake of who the person is in relation to Christ — because they are a prophet, a righteous man, His disciple — not for personal advantage. The reward hangs on doing it in Christ's name, out of love to Him. The point is stated in the repeated clauses themselves.
"He who receives you receives Me, and he who receives Me receives the One who sent Me." (Matthew 10:40)
"And if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones because he is My disciple, truly I tell you, he will never lose his reward." (Matthew 10:42)
Listen for the small, everyday gift Jesus says someone might give to one of His disciples.
"A cup of cold water": "if anyone gives even a cup of cold water to one of these little ones." Jesus promises that even this smallest gift, given for His sake, will never lose its reward.
Mark's Gospel records the same saying about a cup of water given because someone belongs to Christ. In which verse of Mark does that parallel appear?

Mark 9:41, which the brief names as the parallel: a cup of water given "because you bear the name of Christ" will not lose its reward. Set beside Matthew 10:42 it confirms the sense of "because he is My disciple" — Scripture interpreting Scripture.
Later in Matthew, Jesus says that kindness shown to the least of His brothers is counted as done to Himself. In which verse of Matthew 25 does He say that?

Matthew 25:40, named in the brief: "whatever you did for one of the least of these brothers of Mine, you did for Me." It carries forward the identification chain of 10:40 — that receiving His own is receiving Him. Scripture interprets Scripture.
John's Gospel records Jesus stating almost the same words as verse 40 — that receiving the one He sends is receiving Him and the One who sent Him. In which verse of John does this appear?

John 13:20, named in the brief, where Jesus says whoever receives the one He sends receives Him, and receives the One who sent Him. It restates the identification chain of Matthew 10:40 in nearly the same terms — Scripture interpreting Scripture.
After hearing these verses, what is one question you would like to ask — about receiving Jesus' messengers, about the little ones, or about the reward Jesus promises?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A learner might wonder how welcoming a person can be the same as welcoming Jesus, who the little ones are, or what the promised reward is. Receive whatever question they bring rather than steering to a preferred one; it is fine to say the passage does not spell everything out.

Verse 40 links a chain: "He who receives you receives Me, and he who receives Me receives the One who sent Me." Trace what the verse says, then explain how this makes a kindness shown to a lowly disciple reach all the way to God.

First anchor in the words: receiving the disciple is receiving Christ, and receiving Christ is receiving the One who sent Him — an unbroken chain from messenger to Christ to God. Because the sent one carries His Sender's authority, a welcome or gift given to the lowliest disciple for Christ's sake is reckoned as done to Christ, and through Him to the One who sent Him. That is why verse 42 can attach so great a reward to so small a deed. Keep to what the verse states, not speculation beyond it.
The passage moves down a scale: from a prophet, to a righteous man, to "one of these little ones." Why do you think Jesus ends with the lowest one and still promises a full reward?

Anchor first in the descending order the text lays out — prophet, righteous man, little one — with the promise reaching even the last: whoever gives so much as a cup of cold water "will never lose his reward" (v.42). By stooping to the lowest recipient and the smallest gift, Jesus shows that no service done for His sake is too small or too obscure to be seen and rewarded. The little one, low in the world's eyes, is not overlooked by Heaven. Draw the point from the passage's own movement, not from outside speculation.

In your own words, retell what Jesus teaches in these verses: whom a person receives when they welcome His messengers, the three kinds of people He names, and the promise He makes about even the smallest gift.

A complete retelling touches: receiving the disciples is receiving Christ, and receiving Christ is receiving the One who sent Him (v.40); a prophet, a righteous man, and a little one each bring their reward to the one who welcomes them (v.41-42); and even a cup of cold water given to one of these little ones because he is Christ's disciple will never lose its reward (v.42). Receive the learner's own wording; check that the main points are present without requiring exact phrasing.

Jesus says even a cup of cold water given to one of His disciples will be rewarded. What is one small, concrete act of service you could do this week for someone who belongs to Christ — a specific thing you will actually do?

Look for a specific, observable action rather than a good feeling: carrying something for an older believer, bringing a drink or meal to someone, writing a note to a servant of the church, helping a younger child at gathering. Help each person name one doable step and, if useful, when they will do it. Affirm the plan; do not measure their devotion or judge their heart.
Draw a cup of water on paper. Then draw one person you could give a cup of water to, to be kind for Jesus. This week, try giving that person a real drink.

The passage promises that even a cup of cold water given for Christ's sake will never lose its reward (v.42). The picture helps a young child connect a tiny, doable kindness with serving Jesus. Receive whatever the child draws warmly, and encourage the real act of kindness; do not rank or assess the child's devotion.
In this passage, who sent his disciples to ask Jesus a question?
John. The passage says, "Meanwhile John heard in prison about the works of Christ, and he sent his disciples" to ask Jesus (v.2).
Where was John when he heard about the works of Christ?
In prison. The passage says, "Meanwhile John heard in prison about the works of Christ" (v.2).
Listen for the man who sends a question to Jesus, and for where that man is.
John, who is in prison, sends his disciples to Jesus with the question (v.2-3).
After Jesus had finished instructing His twelve disciples, what two things did He go on to do in their cities?
He went on to teach and preach. The passage says He "went on from there to teach and preach in their cities" (v.1).

On paper, put these four moments from the passage in the order they happen: Jesus tells the disciples to report what they hear and see; John hears in prison about the works of Christ; the disciples ask whether Jesus is the One who was to come; John sends his disciples to Jesus.

Correct order: (1) John hears in prison about the works of Christ (v.2); (2) John sends his disciples to Jesus (v.2); (3) the disciples ask whether Jesus is the One who was to come (v.3); (4) Jesus tells them to report to John what they hear and see (v.4).
Listen for what Jesus tells John's disciples to do right after they ask their question.
He tells them to go back and report to John what they hear and see (v.4), then lists the works: the blind receive sight, the lame walk, and so on (v.5).
In your own words, retell this passage from beginning to end: what Jesus does after teaching the Twelve, what John does from prison, the question John's disciples bring, and how Jesus answers them.

Checklist for a complete retelling: after instructing the Twelve, Jesus goes on to teach and preach in their cities (v.1); John, in prison, hears about the works of Christ and sends his disciples (v.2); they ask whether Jesus is "the One who was to come, or should we look for someone else?" (v.3); Jesus tells them to report to John what they hear and see (v.4) — the blind receive sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is preached to the poor (v.5); and He adds, "Blessed is the one who does not fall away on account of Me" (v.6).
The disciples ask whether Jesus is "the One who was to come." Who are they asking about when they use that phrase?

The promised Messiah — the long-expected one whom God had said would come. "The One who was to come" is a recognized name for that promised deliverer, so the question is whether Jesus is He, "or should we look for someone else?" (v.3). Keep to the text's plain sense; do not add beyond what the passage states.
John heard in prison about "the works of Christ." Within this passage, what are those works Jesus points to?

The deeds Jesus lists in v.5: "The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is preached to the poor." These works are what John's disciples hear and see, and they are Jesus' own answer to who He is. Draw the meaning from what the passage names; do not press it further.
Jesus says, "Blessed is the one who does not fall away on account of Me." What does "fall away" mean here?

To be tripped up or stumbled over Jesus — to take offense at Him and turn away rather than trust Him. The word points to being scandalized by something about Christ (His lowly appearance or unexpected way), so that one draws back. Jesus pronounces a blessing on the one who is not tripped up in this way but keeps trusting. Hold the text's sense without pressing it beyond what the passage says.
“Blessed is the one who does not fall away on account of Me.” (Matthew 11:6)
“The blind receive sight, the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the good news is preached to the poor.” (Matthew 11:5)
The signs Jesus lists — the blind seeing, the lame walking, the deaf hearing — were promised long before as signs of God's own coming to save. Which Old Testament passage names those signs?

Isaiah 35:4-6, which says God will come to save, and then "the eyes of the blind will be opened and the ears of the deaf unstopped" — the lame leaping and the mute singing. Jesus points to these very works, letting the prophecy identify its fulfillment — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; do not require the learner to quote Isaiah.
Jesus' last sign is that "the good news is preached to the poor." Which Old Testament passage foretold that the anointed one would preach good news to the poor?

Isaiah 61:1-3, where the anointed one is sent "to preach good news to the poor" and to comfort the afflicted. Jesus' closing sign echoes this prophecy — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; the learner need not quote Isaiah.
Another Gospel tells this same account of John's disciples coming and Jesus answering with these works. In which passage is that parallel found?

Luke 7:21-22, the parallel account, where Jesus first heals many and then tells John's messengers to report what they have seen and heard — the blind seeing, the lame walking, and the poor hearing good news. Comparing the two accounts is Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9). Name the connection; the learner need not recite Luke.
After hearing this passage, what is one question you would like to ask about it?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. Children may wonder why John was in prison, why John asked if Jesus was the One, why Jesus answered by listing what people would see, or what "fall away" means. Receive the question the child brings rather than steering to one you would prefer.
John, who had earlier pointed people to Jesus, now sends from prison to ask, "Are You the One who was to come, or should we look for someone else?" What question does that raise for you?

Open — do not resolve it for them. Adults may ask why Jesus does not simply answer "yes," why He points to His works instead, or what it means that even John sends such a question. Let the question stand; the aim is honest wondering, not a tidy answer. Note that the text elsewhere records John's imprisonment under Herod (Matthew 14:3); this passage itself only says he is in prison.

The disciples ask a yes-or-no question, but Jesus does not simply say "yes." Instead He tells them to report what they hear and see, and He lists His works. Why might Jesus answer with what can be seen and heard rather than a bare "yes"?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus says, "Go back and report to John what you hear and see" (v.4), then lists the works of v.5. He lets the deeds answer the question — works anyone can hear and see stand as evidence of who He is, rather than a claim they must simply take His word for. The brief notes these works also fulfill what was promised of God's coming, so the works themselves testify to His identity. Draw the meaning from what the text states; do not add beyond it.

Right after listing His works, Jesus adds, "Blessed is the one who does not fall away on account of Me." How does that closing blessing connect to the works He has just named and to the question John asked?

Anchor first in the text: Jesus first names the works one can hear and see (v.5), then pronounces the blessing (v.6). The works give solid ground to recognize who He is; yet He knows some may still be tripped up over Him, so He blesses the one who does not fall away but keeps trusting. Set against John's question (v.3), the blessing gently meets the very temptation to doubt: those who weigh the evidence and are not offended at Christ are called blessed. Draw meaning from what the passage states; do not add doctrine it does not carry.

From prison, John did not keep his question to himself — he sent it straight to Jesus. This week, write down one honest question you have about Jesus, and ask it aloud to someone in your family. Afterward, note on paper the question you asked and who you asked.

No right answer to check. Look for one concrete, doable step the learner actually took — an actual question written down and asked aloud, not a vague intention — and record what was done as evidence. Do not judge the learner's faith or the content of the question; note only the observable action.
In this passage, who does Jesus talk to the crowds about?
John (John the Baptist). Verse 7 says, "Jesus began to speak to the crowds about John."
To whom does Jesus begin to speak about John?
The crowds. Verse 7 says, "Jesus began to speak to the crowds about John," after John's disciples were leaving.
Jesus asks the crowds what they went out to see. To what place does He say they went out?
The wilderness. Verse 7 asks, "What did you go out into the wilderness to see?"
Listen for the place Jesus says the crowds had gone out to, when He questions why they went.
The wilderness. Verse 7: "What did you go out into the wilderness to see?"
What is happening at the very start of this passage, just before Jesus begins to speak to the crowds?
John's disciples were leaving. Verse 7 opens, "As John's disciples were leaving, Jesus began to speak to the crowds about John."

In verses 7-9 Jesus asks three times what the crowds went out to see, building to His point. On paper, write the three things He names in the order He asks them: a reed, a man in fine clothes, and a prophet. Then write what He says John truly is.
Order: (1) "A reed swaying in the wind?" (v.7); (2) "A man dressed in fine clothes?" (v.8); (3) "A prophet?" (v.9). Jesus' conclusion: John is "more than a prophet" (v.9).
Near the end, Jesus compares "this generation" to a group of people. Listen for who they are like.
Children sitting in the marketplaces. Verse 16: "They are like children sitting in the marketplaces and calling out to others."
Listen for the first picture Jesus uses to ask what the crowds went out to see: something that sways in the wind.
A reed. Verse 7: "A reed swaying in the wind?" Jesus asks it to deny it of John, whose steadiness was the opposite of a reed bent by every breeze.
Jesus asks whether the crowds went out to see "a reed swaying in the wind." What quality does that image picture, which Jesus is denying belongs to John?

Inconstancy — a reed bends whichever way the wind blows. By asking the question Jesus denies this of John: John was steady, the same in all weathers, not a man swayed by the crowd or by fear. The image contrasts with the firm prophet of the next verses.
In verse 17 the children in the marketplace say, "we sang a dirge, and you did not mourn." What kind of song is a dirge?

A sad song of mourning, sung at a funeral. The passage itself makes it clear: a dirge goes with mourning, just as the flute in the same verse goes with dancing ("We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not mourn.").
Jesus closes with, "But wisdom is vindicated by her actions." What does "vindicated" mean in this saying?

To be vindicated is to be shown right, proved true, justified by the outcome. Wisdom is proved right by what it produces — its actions, or fruit. However this generation slandered both John and the Son of Man, wisdom's true course is confirmed by its results and by those who receive it.
He who has ears, let him hear. (Matthew 11:15)
Truly I tell you, among those born of women there has risen no one greater than John the Baptist. Yet even the least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he. (Matthew 11:11)
In verse 10 Jesus quotes a prophecy about John: 'Behold, I will send My ___ ahead of You, who will prepare Your ___ before You.' Which two words go in the blanks?
messenger and way. The full line reads, "Behold, I will send My messenger ahead of You, who will prepare Your way before You."

Of John, Jesus says in verse 10, "This is the one about whom it is written," and quotes a prophecy about a messenger sent to prepare the way. Which Old Testament prophet's book does this quotation come from?

Malachi 3:1, which reads, "Behold, I will send My messenger, who will prepare the way before Me." Jesus reads John through Malachi's written prophecy — Scripture confirming Scripture. (Isaiah 40:3 sounds the same note: a voice preparing the way of the LORD in the wilderness.)
In verse 14 Jesus says of John, "he is the Elijah who was to come." Which Old Testament prophet foretold that Elijah would be sent before the Day of the LORD?
Malachi (Malachi 4:5): "Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and awesome Day of the LORD." John comes in the spirit and power of Elijah, not as Elijah in person.
Verse 12 says the kingdom of heaven "has been subject to violence, and the violent lay claim to it." Which passage in Luke gives a parallel saying about people forcing their way into the kingdom?

Luke 16:16, which after naming John says, "the gospel of the kingdom of God is being preached, and everyone is forcing his way into it." The parallel pictures the earnest pressing-in of those who lay hold of the kingdom.
Jesus says that no one born of women is greater than John, "Yet even the least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he." What question does that raise for you? Write it down.

Keep this open — the aim is the learner's own wondering, not your answer. Genuine questions might be: how can the least be greater than the greatest? Greater in what way? Where do I stand in relation to John? If it helps later, the brief notes John is greatest by office and fuller nearness, while the least in the kingdom is greater by belonging to the age of fulfillment John only pointed toward — but let the family voice their questions first.
What is one thing in this passage about John that you would like to ask Jesus? Say it or draw it.

There is no target answer — welcome the child's real question and write it down as theirs. Prompts to draw out wondering: why did some people say John had a demon? What does 'more than a prophet' mean? Resist supplying the answer; the goal is that the child learns to bring questions to the text.

The passage says John came "neither eating nor drinking" and was called demon-possessed, while the Son of Man came "eating and drinking" and was called a glutton and drunkard. What is Jesus showing about "this generation" by setting these two rejections side by side?

Anchor first in what the text states (vv.18-19): the same generation faulted John's austerity and the Son of Man's freedom — opposite lives, both rejected. The point is that the fault lay not with either messenger but with the hearers, like the children in the marketplace (vv.16-17) who would neither dance to the flute nor mourn to the dirge. No approach would have satisfied a heart set against the message. Let the family reach this from the text before you name it; keep to what the passage shows, not conclusions about anyone's spiritual state.
Jesus calls John "more than a prophet" (v.9) and then, in verse 10, quotes a prophecy about a messenger who prepares the way. According to the passage, what makes John more than a prophet?

Start from the text: v.10 explains v.9. A prophet foretells what is to come; John is himself the promised messenger sent ahead to prepare the Lord's way — he does not merely predict the Coming One from afar but goes directly before Him. That nearness to fulfillment is why he is 'more than a prophet.' Let the learner connect v.9 to v.10 themselves before confirming.
In your own words, retell the whole passage: what Jesus asks the crowds they went out to see, what He says John really is, and then how He describes "this generation."

Keep this open — check the retelling against the passage rather than grading it against a script. Look together to see whether it touched: Jesus asking whether they went out to see a reed, fine clothes, or a prophet (vv.7-9); John being more than a prophet, the promised messenger, the greatest yet less than the least in the kingdom, the Elijah to come (vv.9-14); and this generation being like children who would not dance or mourn, rejecting both John and the Son of Man (vv.16-19). If a part is missing, let the family re-read and add it; don't supply a model retelling.

Jesus says, "He who has ears, let him hear." On an index card, write down one thing you heard in this passage and one concrete step you will take this week to act on it. Put the card where you will see it.

Aim for an observable, doable step, not a feeling or self-assessment — e.g. 're-read Matthew 11 aloud on Wednesday,' or 'ask Dad what "more than a prophet" means.' Check only that the step is concrete and within reach this week; record what was written, not any judgment about the learner's heart. "He who has ears, let him hear" is verse 15.
In this passage Jesus says, "He who has ears, let him hear." Draw your ears, then tell someone one thing Jesus said that you want to listen to and remember.

There is no wrong drawing. Use it to check the child can name one thing from the passage they want to remember to listen to — for example that John was 'more than a prophet,' or Jesus' words 'let him hear' (v.15). Record what the child says they will do; make no assessment of the child's heart or faith.
Jesus speaks a "woe" against the towns where most of His miracles had been done. Which three towns does He name?

Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum: "Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida!" and then, "And you, Capernaum, will you be lifted up to heaven?" These are the cities in which most of His miracles had been performed.
Jesus contrasts the towns He denounces with other places that, He says, would have repented. Which three other places does He name in this passage?

Tyre and Sidon (set beside Chorazin and Bethsaida), and Sodom (set beside Capernaum). Jesus says of the first pair that "they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes," and of Sodom that "it would have remained to this day."
Listen for the names of the towns Jesus says "Woe" to, and count how many He names.
Three towns are addressed with woe or its warning: Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum. These are the cities where most of Jesus' miracles had been performed.
At the start of this passage, what does Jesus begin to do, and what reason does the verse give for it?
He begins to denounce the cities where most of His miracles had been performed, and the reason given is "because they did not repent." The failure to repent is the one charge He brings.
When Jesus turns to Capernaum, He first asks a question and then gives the answer Himself. What does He ask, and what answer does He give?
He asks, "And you, Capernaum, will you be lifted up to heaven?" and answers, "No, you will be brought down to Hades!" The soaring height is refused and reversed into being cast down.
The passage says Jesus "began to denounce" the cities. What does the word "denounce" mean here, in the way Jesus uses it?

To denounce is to reproach or rebuke openly — Jesus is bringing a solemn charge against the cities, not merely describing them. In this passage the reproach is aimed squarely at their refusal to repent despite the miracles they had seen. Draw the sense from the passage itself.

The word "repent" (and "repented") is the hinge of this passage. From the way it is used here — the cities that did not repent, and Tyre and Sidon who "would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes" — what does it mean to repent?

To repent is to turn from sin in response to what God has shown — a real change, here pictured in the outward tokens of "sackcloth and ashes," the dress of grief and turning. The charge against the cities is precisely that they did not make this turn, though they saw the mighty works. Keep the sense to what the passage shows.

Twice Jesus says it will be "more bearable" for Tyre, Sidon, and Sodom "on the day of judgment" than for the towns He is addressing. What does saying it will be "more bearable" for one place than another tell us about the judgment He describes?

"More bearable" for one than another shows that the judgment has degrees — it will fall more heavily on some than on others. Here the heavier reckoning is for the towns that saw the most miracles and still did not repent: greater privilege refused means a severer judgment. The comparison is fixed at "the day of judgment."
"But I tell you, it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon on the day of judgment than for you." (Matthew 11:22)

"And you, Capernaum, will you be lifted up to heaven? No, you will be brought down to Hades! For if the miracles that were performed in you had been performed in Sodom, it would have remained to this day." (Matthew 11:23)
Listen for the phrase Jesus repeats about the "day of judgment," and notice how many times you hear it.

The refrain is that it will be "more bearable" for the pagan places "on the day of judgment than for you." It comes twice — first of "Tyre and Sidon" (v.22), then of "Sodom" (v.24) — binding the two woe-pairs together.
Another Gospel records Jesus speaking this same woe over Chorazin, Bethsaida, and Capernaum. In which Gospel passage does that parallel account appear?
Luke 10:13-15, which the brief names as the parallel woe over the same three cities. Setting the two accounts side by side lets Scripture interpret Scripture.
Earlier in Matthew, Jesus had already said it would be more tolerable for Sodom than for a town that rejects the gospel message. In which earlier verse of Matthew does He say that?

Matthew 10:15, named in the brief, where Jesus says it will be more tolerable for Sodom on the day of judgment than for the town that rejects His messengers. It echoes the Sodom comparison here, and lets Scripture interpret Scripture.

Jesus' words to Capernaum — lifted up to heaven, yet brought down to Hades — echo an Old Testament passage about a proud power that says it will ascend to heaven but is cast down. Which prophetic passage stands behind that height-to-depth reversal?

Isaiah 14:13-15, named in the brief, where one who says "I will ascend to the heavens" is brought down to Sheol. The same reversal shapes Jesus' warning that Capernaum, exalted to heaven, will be brought down to Hades. Scripture interprets Scripture.
After hearing Jesus speak woe over these towns, what is one question you would like to ask — about the miracles they saw, about repenting, or about the day of judgment?

Fully open — there is no expected answer. A learner may wonder why the towns did not repent though they saw so much, what "sackcloth and ashes" means, or how one place can face a heavier judgment than another. Receive the question the learner brings rather than steering toward one you prefer; it is fine to say the passage does not spell everything out.
As you listen, notice how Jesus compares the towns He warns with places like Tyre, Sidon, and Sodom, and hold on to one thing that puzzles you and that you most want to ask about afterward.

This invites the learner's own question, not a fact to report back. Give room to raise whatever the passage stirs — the strangeness of pagan cities repenting where Israel's towns did not, or what the comparison says about seeing much and turning little — without supplying an answer in advance.

Jesus reasons: if the miracles done in Chorazin and Bethsaida "had been performed in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago," so it will be "more bearable" for Tyre and Sidon than for them. Follow that argument: on what does the heavier judgment of these towns turn?

Anchor first in the words: the towns saw the most miracles and still did not repent, while the pagan places, given the same works, would have repented. The argument turns on refused privilege — the greater the light and grace shown, the greater the guilt of not turning — not on the pagan cities being more virtuous. Keep the discussion to what the verses state; the point is the weight of seeing much and repenting little, and it presses on every hearer, not only those old towns.

The first two towns are addressed together as a pair, but when Jesus reaches Capernaum the words change to a single question and answer about being lifted to heaven and brought down to Hades. What does that shift show about how Capernaum stood among these towns?

Stay with the text: Chorazin and Bethsaida share one woe, but Capernaum gets its own height-to-depth reversal — "will you be lifted up to heaven?" answered by "you will be brought down to Hades!" The singling out marks Capernaum as the most favored of the three (the place of most of Jesus' ministry), and so the most sharply warned. The higher the privilege pictured, the lower the fall when it is abused. Keep to what the verse shows rather than speculation.
In your own words, retell this passage: why Jesus denounces the towns, which towns He names, how He compares them with Tyre, Sidon, and Sodom, and what He says about the day of judgment.

A complete retelling touches: Jesus denounces the cities where most of His miracles were done because they did not repent (v.20); He speaks woe over Chorazin and Bethsaida, saying Tyre and Sidon would have repented in sackcloth and ashes (v.21), so it will be more bearable for them at the judgment (v.22); and He warns Capernaum, lifted toward heaven yet to be brought down to Hades, that even Sodom would have remained had it seen such works (v.23-24). Receive the learner's own wording; check that the main movements are present without requiring exact phrasing.

The towns saw much of Jesus' work and yet did not repent — did not turn. Think of one thing you already know God asks of you but have put off doing. What is one concrete step you will actually take this week to turn and obey in that matter?

Look for a specific, doable action rather than a feeling or a general resolve: mending a wrong that has been left unmended, beginning an obedience long delayed, or setting right a habit the person already knows should change. Help each person name one concrete step, and if useful when they will take it. Affirm the plan; record the observable action, and do not assess the state of anyone's heart or their standing before God.
Jesus did many wonderful miracles in these towns. Draw one thing God has done that you are thankful for. Then tell someone one way you can say thank you to God by obeying Him.

The passage warns the towns that saw so many of Jesus' miracles and still did not turn to God. The picture helps a young child notice good things God has done and connect thankfulness with glad obedience. Receive whatever the child draws and says warmly; do not rank or assess their gratitude or their faith.
In this passage, whom does Jesus praise, and by what title does He address Him?
Jesus praises the Father, addressing Him as "Lord of heaven and earth." The passage opens, "I praise You, Father, Lord of heaven and earth."
Listen for the two groups Jesus names: from whom has the Father hidden these things, and to whom has He revealed them?
The Father has "hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children." So: hidden from the wise and learned; revealed to little children.

Put these three moments in the order Jesus speaks them: the invitation "Come to Me"; His praise of the Father; His declaration that all things have been entrusted to Him. Which is first, which second, which last?

First Jesus praises the Father (vv. 25-26); then He declares, "All things have been entrusted to Me by My Father" (v. 27); last He gives the invitation, "Come to Me" (v. 28). Praise, then declaration, then invitation.

In your own words, retell this whole passage from beginning to end: how Jesus praises His Father, what He says about the Father and the Son knowing each other, and the invitation He gives to those who are weary and burdened.

A faithful retelling covers, in order: Jesus praises the Father, Lord of heaven and earth, for hiding these things from the wise and learned and revealing them to little children, which was well-pleasing in His sight (vv. 25-26); Jesus declares that all things have been entrusted to Him, that the Father and the Son alone know each other, and that the Son reveals the Father to whom He chooses (v. 27); then Jesus invites all who are weary and burdened to come to Him for rest, to take His yoke and learn from Him, for He is gentle and humble in heart, and His yoke is easy and His burden is light (vv. 28-30).
Jesus calls certain people to come to Him. What two words does He use to describe them?
He describes them as "weary and burdened": "Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest."

Jesus says, "Take My yoke upon you and learn from Me." A yoke is the wooden frame an ox wears to work under its master. Using what the passage itself pairs with the word, what does taking Jesus' "yoke" involve here?

The passage pairs the yoke directly with "learn from Me," so taking His yoke means placing oneself under Jesus as His pupil and subject — coming under His teaching and lordship. The text adds that this yoke is not harsh: "For My yoke is easy and My burden is light."
In verse 29, how does Jesus describe His own heart, and why would that description matter to someone weary and burdened deciding whether to come to Him?

Jesus says He is "gentle and humble in heart." The passage itself joins this to the promise "you will find rest for your souls" — the One issuing the yoke is not harsh but gentle, which is why the weary can come to Him and find rest rather than added weight.
Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. (Matthew 11:28)
For My yoke is easy and My burden is light. (Matthew 11:30)
When Jesus promises, "you will find rest for your souls," He is quoting an Old Testament call in the very same words. Which passage is He echoing?
Jeremiah 6:16, where the LORD calls, "walk in it, and you will find rest for your souls." Jesus takes up Scripture's own words, offering in Himself the rest the prophet held out.
Jesus says, "All things have been entrusted to Me by My Father." Near the end of Matthew He states this authority again in different words. Which passage names it?
Matthew 28:18, where the risen Jesus says, "All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to Me." What is entrusted to the Son here is declared as full authority there.
Jesus says His yoke is "easy" and His burden is "light." What is one question you would like to ask Jesus about that?

Invite the child's own wondering; do not supply the answer. Good questions may probe how a yoke or burden could be called easy or light, or what it feels like to have rest given rather than earned. Receive the question; the passage's own answer (a yoke lined with Jesus' gentleness) can wait for later discussion.
Jesus praises the Father for hiding these things from the wise and learned and revealing them to little children. What do you find yourself wondering as you read that?

Let the learner voice genuine questions rather than steering to a settled answer. Wonderings often circle who receives these things and why, or what "little children" points to. Note that the passage frames this as the Father's good pleasure and the Son's choosing to reveal; hold that for discussion, but first honor the question.

Jesus invites, "Take My yoke upon you and learn from Me." A yoke joins an animal to work under its master. What does the passage suggest Jesus means by asking us to take His yoke, and why does He add that it is "easy"?

Anchor to the text: the yoke is paired with "learn from Me," so it pictures coming under Jesus' teaching and lordship as His disciple. He calls it "easy" and His burden "light" not because there is no obedience, but because the Master is "gentle and humble in heart" — the yoke is one of grace, and under it the weary "find rest for your souls." Keep the discussion on what the passage states before reaching for wider application.

Jesus says the Father has "hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children," and that no one knows the Father "except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal Him." According to the passage, who decides who comes to know these things?

Stay with what the text states: revealing rests with the Father's good pleasure ("this was well-pleasing in Your sight") and with the Son, who "chooses to reveal Him." The passage grounds who receives these things in God's own free choosing, not in human wisdom or learning. This adoring acquiescence to the Father's will (WCF 3.7) is held reverently, not as despair; the very next words are the open invitation, "Come to Me." Draw the meaning from the passage; avoid speculation beyond it.

On one side of an index card, write or draw one thing that makes you feel "weary and burdened." On the other side, write a short prayer coming to Jesus with it, using His own promise: "Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest."

The observable response is the completed card and the prayer of coming to Jesus with a real burden — that is the evidence to note, not any judgment about the child's inner state. Keep the focus on the passage's invitation; affirm the doable act of naming a burden and bringing it to Jesus. Doable on paper with a card and pencil.

Draw yourself carrying a heavy bag. Then draw yourself setting the bag down beside Jesus. As you draw, say His words together: "Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest."

The observable response is the two drawings and the child saying Jesus' invitation aloud — note that, not any inner assessment. Keep it simple and warm: the picture of setting down a heavy bag beside Jesus enacts "Come to Me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest." Doable with paper and crayons.
